There is really no shortcut to building wealth. It requires discipline, patience and hard work. Here are a few tips to get you started:

I think this is a very bad idea. First of all, you will pay interest on the cash advance, just like any other cash advance, and it will not be at 0%. It will depend on your credit score and the amount of money you cash out, but it can go up to 23% or even 36% in some cases. Second, you will have to pay the money back in 8 months, whether you like it or not. After 8 months, the 0% APR is over, and you will have to pay 23% or 36% interest on the money you borrowed, plus a fee for the cash advance. Third, it does not make sense to borrow money just to put it in loans. If you borrow money and then pay it back, you will have to pay interest on the money you borrowed. It does not make sense to pay interest on money that you already owe. Fourth, this will not help you at all if you are short on cash. Imagine you have $3000 in your bank account. You borrow $2000 from the bank, and they give you $1000 in cash. You spend the $1000 on clothes, entertainment, and other things you want. After 8 months, you have to pay the bank back $2000, plus $500 in interest. So, you now have $1500 less in your bank account than you started with. Fifth, this can damage your credit score. If you have a credit card with a $1000 limit, and you max out the card, it will ding your credit score. If you have a credit card with a $2000 limit, and you max out the card, it will ding your credit score, plus it will make it harder for you to get credit in the future. Sixth, it does not matter how much money you have in your bank account, or how small your payments are, if you do not pay the money back, it will only get worse. You will have to pay the money back, plus interest, plus a fee for the cash advance. Seventh, this is not a solution to any financial problems you may have. If you are short on cash, the solution is to make more money, or to cut your expenses. There is no point in borrowing money just to put it in loans. Eighth, this can lead to a vicious cycle of debt. If you borrow money to pay for things you want, and then you have to pay the money back, plus interest, it can lead to a vicious cycle of debt. You will have to keep borrowing money just to pay for things you want, plus interest, plus a fee for the cash advance. Ninth, it does not matter how much money you make, if you make less than $30,000 a year, it is not sustainable. If you make $30,000 a year, you will have to pay $700 in interest, plus a fee for the cash advance, which is 23% or 36% of the $2000 you borrowed. If you make $40,000 a year, you will have to pay $1000 in interest, plus a fee for the cash advance, which is 25% or 37% of the $2000 you borrowed. Tenth, it can lead to bankruptcy. If you can not pay the money back, it can lead to bankruptcy. You will have to file bankruptcy, and it will ruin your credit score for 7-10 years. In conclusion, this is a very bad idea. It is not worth the risk. It is better to make less money, have a smaller bank account, and pay the money back, than to take the risk of not paying the money back, plus the interest, plus the fee for the cash advance.

I think you can get a good estimate of risk from looking at the credit rating of the mortgagee and the collateral available. If the mortgagee has a good credit rating, you can assume that they are capable of making payments, and you can assess the risk of default based on the collateral available. If the collateral is the house, and the house is worth 100K, and the mortgage is for 90K, you can calculate the risk of default as 1/2, or 50%. If the credit rating is poor, you may assume a higher risk of default, and adjust your estimate upward.

I hope you have deep pockets.

It is fine to have multiple free credit cards. It is the rare case where it is better to have too many, than not enough. For example, if you have a $1000 limit, and you only use 1/4 of it, the bank is making money off of the 3/4 that you don't use. They are gaining interest on that 3/4 every time that you use it, or they are losing money on it when you don't use it. In the end, they average out to making money on 100% of your card use. (That is an oversimplification, as they also make money through other means, like selling your personal information, and through the fees associated with your transactions.) Having multiple cards means that you are spreading out your use, and making it less likely that you are making a mistake. For example, if you have a $1000 limit, and you only have one card with a $1000 limit, and you make a mistake and charge $1200 to it, you are in trouble. You either need to pay it off immediately, or you need to have another card with a higher limit. Having multiple cards means that you can pay off one, and not have to worry about it, while you use the other one.

I think the answer by mhoran_psychologist provides a good starting point. In addition, I'd like to add a few points. (FYI: I'm from the US, but have worked in several other countries, including the UK and Canada, so my experience spans both sides of the Atlantic.) How much should you put away? It's really simple: If you put aside $500, and you need it for an emergency, you'll either use it or return it to your employer, depending on how you see this "emergency fund" concept. If you live in the UK, and your emergency fund is $500, and you spend $300 on medical emergencies, you'll either have to pay the $100 shortfall from your next paycheck, or you'll have to return the $200 balance to your employer. In either case, it's not really a "emergency fund" as the term is used in the US. In the US, the idea of the emergency fund is that you can use it for any emergency, not just health-related ones. So, in the US, the recommended amount is much larger - $1,000 to $2,000. Why? Because when you have an emergency fund, you can use it to pay for the next emergency, without having to go to a payday lender, or borrow from a friend/family member. (Of course, if you have a lot of friends/family members you can borrow from, you can keep them on standby for a loan, too.) What kind of emergencies can you expect in the UK? In the US, we have hurricanes, earthquakes, and wildfires. The UK doesn't have hurricanes, but it does have flooding. A lot of flooding. So do many parts of the US, but the UK isn't as accustomed to it. Also, the UK has a much higher risk of terrorist attacks, although this is a lot harder to prepare for. How can you prepare for emergencies in the UK? There are a few things you can do: 1. Make sure you have a full tank of gas. The last time I was in the UK, I was surprised to find that many gas stations don't have attendants. You have to pump your own gas. If there's a gas shortage, you'll want to make sure you have a full tank. 2. Buy food and water that doesn't need refrigeration. The UK is pretty good about keeping supermarkets stocked with food, but in the event of a disaster, you might want to stock up on non-perishable foods. 3. Consider buying a basic first aid kit. The NHS is pretty good, but it's always good to have a basic first aid kit on hand. 4. Think about where you would go if you needed medical attention. The NHS is pretty good, but it's good to know where your nearest hospital is, in case you need immediate medical attention. In the US, we have a lot of problems with medical tourism (people from other countries come to the US for medical treatment), but the UK doesn't have this problem. So, if you're from another country, and you're thinking about moving to the UK, it's good to know where you'll go if you need medical attention. 5. Consider buying insurance. The NHS is pretty good, but it's good to have some kind of insurance, in case you need immediate medical attention. There are a lot of different insurance policies out there, so it's a good idea to shop around.

It is very simple. If you want to make money, then charge more than your expenses. If you want to make a lot of money, then charge a lot more than your expenses. If you want to make no money at all, then charge exactly your expenses. Doesn't matter what the market will pay, what your friend will pay, what your neighbor will pay. What does matter is whether or not you are getting paid to be a landlord, or if you are just throwing money down the drain. Choose wisely.

It will depend on what state you're in. Here in California, you're considered a contractor, and must pay your own taxes, along with a small payroll tax for the state. You will need to file a 1099 form with the IRS and a 1099-MISC form with the state. There are many many many resources available to help you understand this process. I would recommend you to consult a professional tax preparer, or at the very least, use the resources provided by the IRS and CA Franchise Tax Agency. You can also ask your accountant, if you have one. They will be able to give you excellent advice and do the tax returns for you. Some examples of resources: http://www.sba.gov/blogs/getting-started-freelancing-how-tax-basics-work http://www.sba.gov/blogs/tax-issues-freelance-writers-and-artists http://www.ftb.ca.gov/taxes/businesses/contractors/contractor-tax-guide http://www.ftb.ca.gov/taxes/businesses/contractors/contractor-tax-guide/chapter2.shtml http://www.irs.gov/Businesses/Small-Businesses-&-Self-Employed/Freelance-Writers-Writers-Authors http://www.irs.gov/Businesses/Small-Businesses-&-Self-Employed/Freelance-Writers-Writers-Authors/topic-242.html http://www.irs.gov/taxtopics/tc503.html (for a discussion of the different names that 1099 forms might have) http://www.irs.gov/pub/irs-pdf/1099cc.pdf (form 1099-MISC that you will need to file for your contractor side job) http://www.ftb.ca.gov/taxes/businesses/1099-miscellaneous/1099-miscellaneous-reporting-requirements (the form and deadlines to file)

It is different. In Honduras for example, in order to pay for university I had to take off a year and travel to Mexico to study, because there were no universities in Honduras at the time. Also, corruption is very deep in the government and almost everywhere else. When I went to pick up my university degree in Mexico, I had to pay off the dean of the university, or else he would not give me my diploma. I am sure there are many other examples of corruption in Honduras. Poverty is not just a economic issue, it is also a political and social issue. In order to solve poverty, you have to tackle all of these issues. Printing money does not solve the problem, it just causes inflation and hurts the poor people more.

The best way to handle this is to set up a PayPal account that is linked to your credit card. That way you can avoid having to give out your bank account number. If you still want to keep your bank account number private, you can set up your PayPal account to use a pseudonym, so that when people send you money, they send it to your pseudonym, rather than to your bank account.

I agree with the other answers that it's very rare, and probably not worth the effort, but I do have one caveat. The IRA is inherited as a matter of law, and as such is entitled to an accelerated payout schedule. Specifically, the non-spouse beneficiary has the option of taking the entire distribution in the year following the IRA owner's death in equal installments over the expected remaining lifetime of the beneficiary, or the option of taking the distribution in whole or in part in any year or years that the beneficiary chooses within the 10 years following the IRA owner's death. If the IRA is disclaimed, I believe that the 10 year rule doesn't apply, and the IRA would have to be distributed within a single year. This might be a problem if the beneficiary need the money in a hurry, or if the beneficiary's lifetime expectancy is more than 10 years. This provision is still valid for beneficiaries inheriting an IRA after the SECURE Act of 2019 took effect, even if the IRA was opened before that date. See Publication 590-B (available on the IRS website) for details. Mere possession of the IRA after the IRA owner's death is enough to make the IRA inherit as a matter of law, so disclaiming the IRA would seem to be pointless. If the IRA is disclaimed, I believe that the 10 year rule doesn't apply, and the IRA would have to be distributed within a single year. This might be a problem if the beneficiary needs the money in a hurry, or if the beneficiary's lifetime expectancy is more than 10 years.

The bank doesn't need the date on the back of the check because it can be identified by the numbers and amounts listed on the front of the check. The bank can also use the numbers and amounts listed on the front of the check to reproduce the check from the deposit slip. In short, the date on the back of the check isn't necessary for the bank to know what the check is. The bank knows what the check is because of the numbers and amounts listed on the front of the check. We don't need the date because the numbers and amounts listed on the front of the check tell us exactly what the check is. The bank can use the numbers and amounts listed on the front of the check to reproduce the check from the deposit slip. The deposit slip has the numbers and amounts listed on it that the bank needs to reproduce the check. The date on the back of the check isn't listed on the deposit slip, so the bank doesn't need it. I hope this helps. Let me know if you have any other questions.

What do you mean by "issue of bonus shares"? Bonus shares are a type of share that is issued in addition to a certain number of regular shares. For example, a company might issue 100 regular shares for every 1000 dollars of capital, and then issue 1000 bonus shares for every 1000 dollars of profit. The profit part is what makes the bonus shares valuable to the shareholder. The value of the bonus shares depends on how well the company is doing. If the company is making a lot of money and thus issuing many bonus shares, then each bonus share is worth less than if the company was not doing well and thus had few or no bonus shares. In the case of an individual owner of shares, the value of the bonus shares is in how much it helps keep the company successful and thus the value of the stock. If the company stays successful and the stock price goes up, then the value of the bonus shares goes up with it.

I am not able to look at the forms you posted, but in general the numbers on the forms are different because the forms are used for different purposes. The 1099-MISC is used to show non employee compensation to independent contractors and freelancers. It shows the amount you paid the contractor, and the contractor must then use this amount on the 1040 as if it were income from a job. The amount on the 1099-K is the amount of money that you paid for goods and services, and that amount is already considered income on the 1040. So you don't need to show it again. In general, when you use the 1099-MISC, you are claiming that you are not an employee of the person you are reporting, and when you use the 1099-K you are admitting that you are an employee and that you already accounted for the income on the 1040.

Yes, if they are reasonable and directly related to the hobby. Example: A photography studio that also does portraits for weddings and corporate events can deduct the cost of equipment and supplies because those are reasonable and related to the hobby of photography, even though they also get used for other things. However, if they are unreasonable or have no relation to the hobby, then they are not deductible. For example: If someone buys a ton of paint and paintbrushes, and uses them only to paint their living room, that is reasonable. However, if they buy a ton of paint and paintbrushes and use them to paint their house, or to paint their dog, or to paint a picture of their dog, that is not reasonable.

The U.S. government cannot default on its loans, any more than a individual can. What's called default is when a debt obligation cannot be paid as promised, and in the case of sovereign states, that means they cannot pay for goods and services, or make loan payments to creditors. But the U.S. government also doesn't have to borrow money to pay for anything. It creates its own money whenever it wants. So it doesn't have to pay for anything it buys, nor does it have to pay any debt it has created. The question is not what will happen to your bank money if the U.S. defaults, because you can be sure bankers will find a way to recycle your money and make profit from it anyway. The real question is what will happen to the economy if the U.S. government creates money to pay for things it doesn't actually have. That's called monetary inflation, and the effects are: (1) Prices go up, because with more money in circulation, businesses can afford to raise prices for goods and services. (2) The value of the U.S. dollar goes down, because it's devalued by the amount of money created. (3) People have less purchasing power, because even if their salaries stay the same, the prices of things they buy go up. (4) People begin to save less, and spend more, which means businesses make more profit, but economy doesn't grow as much as it would if people continued to save and invest. (5) People begin to borrow more, because they need to borrow to buy food and other necessities. This means more profits for banks, but it also means more defaults, as people have trouble paying back what they borrowed. (6) The economy speeds up, as people spend more and businesses make more profit. More profit means more money created by banks, which means more prices go up, and so on. This can go on forever, until people decide to change the system.

Can I help you with something? Boss: Yes, I need to borrow some money. Assistant: Is this a loan that you will pay back to me? Boss: No, to the debt collection agency. Assistant: So you are not going to pay them? Boss: No. Assistant: So what do I do now? Boss: Deal with it. Assistant: Deal with what? Boss: The debt collection agency. Assistant: But I am the one who will pay them, aren't I? Boss: Yes. Assistant: So why should I deal with it? Boss: Because you are the one who owes the money. Assistant: I don't think that is fair. Boss: Life is not fair. Assistant: What can I do to get rid of this debt? Boss: Pay it. Assistant: I don't have the money. Boss: Then you will just have to take on more hours. Assistant: I already work full time. Boss: Then you will just have to take on more work. Assistant: I cannot work miracles. Boss: I know, but I need miracles. Assistant: I can't take on any more work and I don't have the money to pay the debt. Boss: Then you will just have to get a loan. Assistant: A loan? Boss: Yes, a loan from the bank. Assistant: But I don't want to take out a loan. Boss: Why not? Assistant: Because I would have to pay interest on the loan and I already don't have enough money to pay the debt. Boss: Yes, you have to pay interest on loans. Assistant: I know, but I already cannot pay the debt and now I have to pay interest on the debt? Boss: No, you will pay the debt and then the interest on the debt. Assistant: But that will still leave me in the red. Boss: Yes. Assistant: Can I at least borrow some money from you? Boss: No.

You can't. You need to be licensed as a broker or as a lender to do that. Moreover, even if you were licensed, it's not like your stock is an object that can be loaned out like a car. It's a financial instrument that can be traded, and as such it belongs to the person to whom it's registered. You would need to have the owner of the stock give you a power of attorney to manage their stocks, and even then it's not certain that the power of attorney would give you the right to use the stock for short selling. In any case, it's not something that can be done without the owner's explicit consent.

Hello! How can I help you today? Customer: I would like to invest $10,000 in stock exchange. I would like to know what would be the best stocks to invest in. I would also like to know how much dividend I can expect to receive from my investments. And how often do I get paid?! Assistant: First of all your total investment of $10,000 will be spread across the stocks you have bought. The exact breakdown will depend on the stock price at the time you purchase them. As for how much dividend you will receive and how often, I can’t give you an exact answer. It will depend on the dividend policy of the companies you have invested in. You should check with them for how much they pay in dividends, how often they pay it and what the exact timings are. This will depend on the company’s performance and how it has decided to distribute its profits to shareholders. You should also check what the tax laws are in your country regarding dividends.

I hope you're prepared for some serious downvoting. This question seems to be based on a complete misconception about insurance. Insurance is a risk distribution mechanism, not a savings and loan. If you buy a house, you should get a homeowners insurance policy, and that coverage should include both the house itself and the personal property it contains. If you fail to buy such a policy, any loss of the house or its contents is not insured, and any money you pay for the house is simply a payment for the right to own the house without insurance, just like buying a car without an engine oil change or a refrigerator without a coolant fluid. More importantly, homeowners insurance policies are written on an all-risks basis, meaning that unless specifically excluded, the insured is covered against all risks of loss, including those that may not be covered by other types of insurance, such as flood insurance or earthquake insurance. For example, if your home is located in a flood-prone area and you have a flood insurance policy, that policy will cover flood damage even if your homeowners insurance doesn't, because flood insurance is written on an all-risks basis. If you live in an earthquake-prone area and have earthquake insurance, that policy will also cover earthquake damage even if your homeowners insurance doesn't. In contrast, an auto insurance policy is only written on an liability basis, and will not cover maintenance costs or repair your stolen vehicle. A life insurance policy is written on a straight life basis, meaning that it covers only the lifetime of the person insured, and doesn't terminate if the insured person dies. A medical insurance policy is written on a major medical basis, meaning that it covers only extensive medical expenses, such as those incurred by someone who has a serious illness or injury. A dental insurance policy is written on a major dental basis, meaning that it covers only extensive dental expenses, such as those incurred by someone who needs multiple dental procedures. A vision insurance policy is written on a major vision basis, meaning that it covers only extensive vision expenses, such as those incurred by someone who needs multiple eye exams or glasses. A homeowners insurance policy is written on an all-risks basis, meaning that it covers all risks of loss. The only exception I know of is a condominium owners insurance policy, which is written on an open peril basis, meaning that it only covers losses caused by specific perils listed in the policy, such as theft by a burglar or water damage caused by a burst water main.

What do you hope to achieve by buying a bond with a 0% yield? Some possible reasons: To make a quick profit: If you buy the bond and sell it shortly thereafter, hoping to make a profit from the difference in yield and the change in the bond's price. However, this seldom happens with individual bonds, and usually only works with mutual funds or exchange-traded funds that buy and sell hundreds or thousands of bonds. To earn predictable income: If you buy the bond and hold it until its maturity date, you know exactly what the return will be: the principal, plus any interest that's been paid. It's not a lot of money, usually, but it's predictable, and sometimes that's important. To support a company or organization that you like: If you believe that the company or organization is worthwhile, and its bonds are available, why not buy some? You're not doing it for the money, but rather to support the company. As an investment diversification tool: Bonds usually move in opposite direction to stocks, so if you invest in both, your portfolio will be less volatile and more stable. This is especially true of high-yield bonds, which are usually issued by larger companies with good credit ratings. As a hedge against inflation: If inflation starts to pick up, as it did in the 1970s and 1980s, the value of your bond investments will go up, since you'll be getting paid interest on bonds issued more recently, which will be worth more in today's dollars than bonds issued farther back in time. As a way to invest in your community: If you buy bonds from a local government, or from a company that does business in your community, you're investing in your community and helping it to grow. As a way to save for retirement: If you buy bonds and hold them until your retirement, you know exactly what your return will be: the principal, plus any interest that's been paid. It's not a lot of money, usually, but it's something. As a way to give to charity: Many people invest in bonds and hold them until they mature, then use the money to give to charity. Again, it's not a lot of money, usually, but it's something. As a way to reduce your tax bill: If you have a lot of taxable income, and you invest in bonds, the interest you receive will be taxed as ordinary income, but the principal will be tax-free. As a way to leave a legacy: If you buy bonds and hold them until your death, then give them to your heirs, they'll get the full return on the bond: the principal, plus all the interest that was paid. As a way to help a child or other family member get started: If you buy bonds and give them to a child or other family member, and tell them to hold them until they mature, that's a way to help them get started in life.

If you are having trouble getting credit because of your income, you might consider getting a guarantor loan. A guarantor is someone who agrees to take on a portion of the debt if you don't repay it. This might help you get around the income requirement. A good potential guarantor is a family member. If you are married, you and your spouse could potentially act as guarantors for each other. (With the understanding that if one of you takes on this responsibility, the other will be responsible for repaying the loan if the other spouse is unable to pay.) If you don't have a family member who is willing or able to act as a guarantor, you could try a rewards credit card. These cards offer rewards for purchases, but they often have high APRs. If you can afford to pay it all back quickly, though, they might be a good option.

What is your effective annual salary? How much will you make in a year? How much will you pay in taxes? How much will you make in take-home pay? Those are the kind of questions you need to ask yourself. It sounds like you have a good idea of what you will make in a year, and how much taxes you will pay. Now you just need to calculate your monthly take-home pay. Assuming that your tax withholding is at the maximum, and assuming that you don't have any other income or deductions, your monthly take-home pay will be: Yearly Salary / 12 Months Monthly Take-Home Pay This calculation is very simple, and it assumes that you don't have any other income or deductions. If you enter these numbers into a spreadsheet formula, you can play with different numbers to see how much your monthly take-home pay will be if you make, for example, $30,000 a year, or if you pay $500 a month in rent.

It is highly recommended you have a cash emergency fund that you can use in case you lose your job or your income is interrupted. How much should you have? About three months' worth of living expenses. (More recommendations here: http://www.moneymatters.net/budgeting/emergency-fund/) The MoneyMatter blog also has other articles on investment strategies, retirement planning, and personal finance. It is not recommended that you invest in precious metals or collectibles as a part of your emergency fund. These assets are not liquid, meaning it could take days or even weeks to sell them if you needed the money immediately. You could lose your job or have an Interruptied Income (II) at any time, and you need to be able to access your emergency fund quickly. Also, the price of these assets could go down while you're trying to sell them, leaving you with fewer assets than you started with.

Mint.com is a well-respected personal finance website and app that helps users manage their finances, improve their financial health, and learn about money. It's a useful tool for anyone looking to get a better handle on their money. It's important to note that Mint.com is a third-party website and app that connects to your bank account(s). As with any website or app that connects to your bank account(s), there is a risk of fraud or theft. Mint.com takes many steps to minimize this risk, including: Using bank-level encryption to protect your login and personal information. Employing firewalls and intrusion detection systems to protect against cyber attacks. Partnering with banks and financial institutions to provide a secure and reliable service. Offering two-factor authentication to further protect your account. If you're concerned about the risk of fraud or theft, you should also consider using a password manager to protect your login credentials. Mint.com supports single sign-on, which means you can use your Mint.com login credentials to access other participating financial institutions' websites and apps. If you're concerned about the risk of fraud or theft when using Mint.com or any other website or app that connects to your bank account(s), you should also consider using a password manager to protect your login credentials. Conclusion: Mint.com is a useful tool for anyone looking to get a better handle on their money. While there are risks associated with any website or app that connects to your bank account(s), Mint.com takes many steps to minimize those risks. If you're concerned about the risk of fraud or theft, you should also consider using a password manager to protect your login credentials.

I think you're thinking of a "line of credit" as a pool of money that you can draw from. That's different from a traditional loan, where you have a fixed amount of money that is loaned to you and you pay it back over time. With a line of credit, the bank sets a maximum amount that you can borrow, and you can borrow that amount multiple times, like a credit card. (In theory, you could borrow over $10,000 on a line of credit, although most people don't need that much.) But, as far as I know, there is no "term" on a line of credit. Once you're approved, the bank gives you a certain amount of money that you can borrow, and you can borrow it whenever you want, for as long as you want. Usually, you'll have to pay the money back fairly quickly, though - like within a few months.

I don't think that's odd at all. That's exactly how I'd expect the market to react. Consider: Before the takeover, the company had X amount of assets and Y amount of liabilities. After the takeover, the company had Z amount of assets and W amount of liabilities. Now, if Z is greater than X, then the company is worth more than it was before the takeover. If W is greater than Y, then the company is worth more than it was before the takeover. So: If the market worth of the company is greater than its current worth, then it is a buy. If the market worth of the company is less than its current worth, then it is a sell. That's how I would expect the market to react to a takeover. The price at which you can buy the company is the market worth. The price at which you can sell the company is the current worth. If the market price is greater than the current price, then you should buy. If the market price is less than the current price, then you should sell.

Your funds have been used to pay off debt by the government. Do you want to open a new bank account or receive the funds in another way? Funds in closed bank account have gone to the government Use of funds for paying off debt by the government If the government had paid off its debt to the bank, the funds would have been transferred to the bank and would have become available for lending again. However, since the government paid off its debt to the bank using the funds that had been frozen, those funds are now unavailable for lending and have been used to pay off the government's debt instead. How to get money frozen by the government if you need it? If the funds are being held by the government because of some action you took, such as closing a bank account or filing for bankruptcy, you will need to take whatever steps are necessary to get the government to release the funds. This may involve going to court, depending on the reason the funds were frozen in the first place. If the funds are being held because of some action the government took, such as seizing the funds as part of an investigation, you will need to go through the appropriate channels to get the funds released. This may involve contacting the agency that is holding the funds, such as a law enforcement agency or a financial regulator, and making your case to them. You may also need to go to court to get the funds released. Overall, the best way to get your funds back if they have been frozen is to understand the reason why they were frozen in the first place and to take the appropriate steps to get them released. This may involve being patient and letting the legal process play out, as well as being willing to make concessions in order to get the funds released more quickly.

What is a profit sharing retirement plan? Retirement plans in the US can be classified as either defined benefit or defined contribution. Defined benefit plans promise a certain monthly benefit upon retirement, while defined contribution plans promise to put a certain amount of money into an account that you can draw from upon retirement. Profit-sharing plans are defined contribution plans. A profit-sharing plan allows an employer to set aside a portion of its profits each year for retirement. The employer gets a tax deduction for the contribution, and the employee gets the tax benefit of having the money in the retirement account. The profit-sharing account is usually invested in stocks or mutual funds. The investment grows tax-free, and upon retirement the employee receives the value of the account. IRS rules limit the amount that can be contributed to a profit-sharing plan, and also limit the amount of income that can be considered "profit" for the purposes of the contribution. The IRS forms that are used to file the profit-sharing plan with the employer and the IRS are called a 401(k). A 401(k) can also be used to contribute to an employee's retirement account, and is often used in conjunction with a profit-sharing plan. A 401(k) contribution is also a defined contribution, and is deducted from the employee's salary (or from the employer's profits, if the employee elects a 401(k) contribution). IRS forms 401(k) are also used to file the 401(k) contribution with the employer and the IRS. Profit-sharing plans must be filed with the IRS by the employer. A student loan is a loan that is specifically for students who wish to further their education. In the United States, student loans are handled by the Department of Education, and they will work with you to determine the amount of money that you will need in order to cover your school expenses. The amount of money that you can borrow will depend on your financial need, your expected salary after graduation, and the cost of the school that you are attending. In order to apply for a student loan, you will need to fill out the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA). The FAFSA will take into consideration your financial situation, your family's financial situation, and your expected salary after graduation in order to determine the amount of money that you will be able to borrow from the government. Once your application for a student loan has been approved, the money will be sent directly to the school that you are attending. You can then use this money to pay for any of your school expenses, including tuition, fees, books, and living expenses. In the United States, student loans have a relatively low interest rate, and they do not have any payments due until after you have graduated from school and have started earning money.

So you want to invest in stocks but don't know where to start? There's a few things you should consider first. What is your goals? Are you looking for growth, income, or both? I don't think that's an option. Once you've opened a bank account, you can usually only close it by actually using it (by withdrawing money or depositing checks). There is usually no way to just close the account without using it. If you really don't want to close the account and use it, you could just leave the money in the account and not use it. Most banks don't have a limit on how much money you can leave in an account without using it. If you're worried about the security of having that money in the bank, you could always keep it in a savings account, where the bank does limit how much you can deposit. Usually, a savings account has a higher interest rate than a standard bank account, so it's a win either way. If you're really paranoid about the bank's security, you could always keep your money in a check account, and just write checks from it whenever you needed to pay something. That would limit how much you could have in the account at any given time, but it would also limit the amount of paperwork and hassle involved.

You cannot open multiple sell orders for the same security at the same price. This is known as overlapping orders, and it's not allowed by the NASDAQ. You can only close existing sell orders earlier than expected if the order was filled. You can only open new sell orders after the previous one has been closed or cancelled. If you entered a bad order that cannot be filled as written, the system will cancel it automatically. You can't reopen the order afterwards. If you entered a valid order and it was not filled because it overlapped with an existing order, you can place a new order afterwards. If the new order overlaps with an existing order, it will not be executed. You'll have to close the overlapping order first. If the new order also overlaps with an existing order, it will not be executed and you'll have to close the overlapping order and then try again.

I think it's a case of supply and demand. When a company makes a product, they decide on the price based on what they feel will be a fair profit margin, taking into account the cost of production, their target market, and competition. The price you pay is not solely dictated by the production process, but by the market demand for the product and the supply of similar products from competitors. If a company wants to make a profit, they generally will not lower their price below the going rate for that product, even if their costs are lower, because they feel that doing so would be a loss leader, meaning they will make their money back by selling volumes, not profit margins. That being said, they may adjust their prices post-sale, such as through volume discounts for bulk purchases or subscriptions.

Hello! How may I assist you today? :):) The investor: I'd like to invest in a foreign country, specifically one that is considered "third world" or developing. I've been wanting to do this for a while now, but have had no idea where to start. Any suggestions? The assistant: I am not aware of any "one-stop shops" that will handle the investing for you, but there are many brokerages that will allow you to purchase foreign stocks. The key is to find a broker that will allow you to open an account with a minimum deposit of $1000, and that will allow you to buy stocks of different countries. For example, many brokerages will allow you to buy stocks of all the member countries of the European Union. I used to use Scottrade, but they only allowed you to buy stocks of countries that were listed on the major U.S. stock exchanges. I have since moved to TradeStation, which does not have that restriction. The catch is that you will probably pay a fixed transaction cost of $15 per transaction, plus whatever the exchange rate fees are. For example, if you want to buy 100 shares of Telmex (the Mexican telephone company) on the Mexican exchange, at today's (unjust) exchange rate of 60.77 to the U.S. dollar, your transaction would cost $1500. There are many sites that will exchange your money at a better rate -- use one of them. I also have accounts at two other brokerages, Ameritrade and PayPal, which I keep active for other reasons, but I only use them to buy stocks when I can find a good bargain. (I also have accounts at several foreign brokerages, but I only use them when I want to buy stocks that are not listed on U.S. exchanges.) There are many sites that will allow you to open an account with a foreign broker, but many of them require you to be a resident of the country where you want to open the account. If you want to open an account for investment purposes only, you might try Interactive Brokers or XETrade. They both have good reputations, but they charge varying transaction costs -- check them out. Through IB, I have bought stocks of all the member countries of the European Union, as well as Australia, Canada, and Japan. XETrade also has a good selection of stocks, but I have not used them as much as IB because they charge $18 per transaction, whereas IB charges $15. (IB also has a higher minimum deposit of $5000, though I have deposited less money into my IB account and still been able to buy stocks with transaction costs of $15.) I think you are mixing two different concepts here. You have the index fund, which is a collection of stocks, where the management fees are geared to reduce turnover and maintain the asset balance. Then you have the ETF, which is an exchange-traded fund, which is a wrapper around a basket of stocks, where the management fees are geared to reduce the impact of trading. The index fund and ETF will have different management fees, it is a core of the ETF strategy. The indice fund will have higher turnover, which means higher costs, but maybe not as bad as you think. The IndeX fund will have lower turnover, meaning less activity and thus less costs, but maybe not as effective in keeping up with the changing index. So it comes down to your risk tolerance and time horizon. If you have a low risk tolerance and a long time horizon, the IndeX fund might be better, but if you have a higher risk tolerance and a shorter time horizon, the ETF might be better.

I think you are mixing two different concepts here. You have the personal finance investment portfolio, and the company's stock performance. They are two different beasts, measuring two different things. The personal finance investment portfolio is a collection of different assets, each with its own risk and return profile. It's a matter of risk management and probability to evaluate its performance. The company's stock performance, on the other hand, is a matter of how well the company is doing, whether it makes money, and how much. It's a matter of how well the company is run, whether they make good decisions, and whether they are a profitable business. These two concepts are related, in that a company's stock price is usually a reflection of its financial health, but they are different. If you want to know how your investments are doing, you should look at the relevant financial metrics, such as the total return, the gain/loss, the volatility, and the risk. You should also look at the macroeconomic factors that affect all stocks, such as interest rates, inflation, and GDP growth. Those are the relevant metrics to evaluate your portfolio's performance. Whether the company is making money or not is not very useful information for you as an investor. It's useful information for the company's management and shareholders, certainly, but not for you. You should be thinking about your own risk and return trade-off, and how it matches with your own goals and preferences. Whether the company is making money or not has no bearing on that.

It is in fact a combination of both. In order to initiate the process of labor, the contractions have to be strong enough to strip the placenta from the uterine lining. This is a mechanical process in a sense. However, the contractions are also influenced by hormones - mainly oxytocin and prostaglandins. These hormones cause the muscles of the uterus to relax and contract, which in turn causes the cervix to dilate and efface (thin out). So it is a combination of both mechanical and hormonal influence.

What do you mean exactly? "Halal" and "haram" are terms used in Islam to describe what is permitted or forbidden. Investing in stocks, bonds, real estate etc. will always involve some risk. The returns you can expect from these investments are never totally predictable and sometimes can be quite unexpected. If you take your money and buy something that you can eat (food) or something you can use (drink, furniture, etc.) and if you are satisfied with what you bought, then you have a halal investment. If you give money to a Muslim charity, and they give you something in return, as long as what you are given is not haram, it is a halal investment. If you help a Muslim friend or family member with your money, as long as what you give them is not a loan (borrowing is haram) and they do not have to pay you back (you can not loan your money to a friend or family member), it is a halal investment. If you have to borrow money from a bank or from a Muslim friend/family member, and you have to pay the borrowed money back, it is not a halal investment. You would have to stop borrowing money from banks and Muslim friends/family members if you want to keep your investments halal.

I think there's a confusion between "efficiency" and "productivity". Both are important, but different. Efficiency refers to the cost per output. Productivity refers to the output per worker. A growing economy means more output, and therefore more income and wealth for the country. But this does not necessarily mean that the economy is becoming more efficient. In fact, in some cases, economic growth is accompanied by an increase in costs, e.g. when an economy moves towards services, rather than goods (more labor, less capital). Or when there are changes in the mix of production, e.g. more manufacturing in a service economy, or more service in a manufacturing economy. In a service economy, e.g. more doctors, more lawyers, more real estate agents, more bankers, more... etc. Each of these groups requires its own specialized infrastructure, which may not be more efficient than the infrastructure needed for a manufacturing economy, e.g. more factories, more machinery, more... etc. In a manufacturing economy, e.g. more factories, more machinery, more... etc. Each requires a different set of skills, and therefore a different set of schools, universities and research institutions. Skills are embodied in the economy as infrastructure. When an economy is based on services, these skills are less valuable to the economy as a whole, because they are less transferable to other sectors. For example, a country that has a strong manufacturing economy also has a strong service economy, and a strong service economy in turn has a strong manufacturing economy. Cities that have a strong service economy also have a strong manufacturing economy, and so on. So a growing economy may not be efficient, but it is certainly productive. And for the welfare of the citizens, it is important that the economy is both efficient and productive.

What do you mean exactly? Investing in real estate to rent it out? Investing in stocks and bonds? Those are very different investment strategies with very different risk and reward profiles. Moreover, they are affected by different economic factors. The risk and the reward from investing in stocks and bonds are different as well. The reward is higher but the risk is more likely and better understood. I would advise you to read up on the subject so that you understand the terms that are being used and the concepts that are being discussed. Because without that, it's just guessing and that's not a good way to invest your money.

I think you are mixing two different concepts here. The P/E ratio based on CURRENT price, and the P/E ratio based on HISTORICAL price pattern. The P/E ratio based on current price is useful for comparing companies in the same industry, at the same growth stage, at the same profitability, etc. It is less useful for making long-term predictions, because a company's price can fluctuate wildly over the short term. The P/E ratio based on HISTORICAL price pattern is more useful for making long-term predictions. It takes into account the entire historical pattern of a company's price, not just a snapshot of it. It is less useful for comparing companies in the same industry, at the same growth stage, at the same profitability, etc. Because different companies have different historical price patterns.

I use a tax consultant. I have no idea how accurate his work is, but I trust him to put in the right numbers and let me know if I owe any money. I don't particularly care about how he did it. I just want him to do it. I can always pay the bill. (Edited to add scope of work) A tax attorney, on the other hand, can help you understand what's going on. I've had a few conversations with a CPA/attorney who runs a tax law practice in my area, and he's been very clear about the limitations of his expertise. He will not touch tax issues that involve the IRS, and for good reason. He's willing to talk about strategies for reducing tax liability, but he's blunt about the fact that there's no guarantee any of it is worth anything. He's also very clear about the fact that he cannot provide legal advice. If you have a legal issue related to taxes, he referred me to an attorney who specializes in tax law. That's someone you should talk to. If you simply want to understand how the tax law works, he's not the right person. He doesn't really know. He knows how to apply the rules that are there, but he doesn't know what the rules are or why they're there. For that, you need an tax law professor or someone working in the IRS. As for using a tax consultant, I have no idea how these work. I've never used one. But it makes sense that they would do a numbered list of things to try to minimize your tax liability, much like a CPA does. They probably have a better idea of what the rules are that they're working with.

There is no benefit to buying shares in a wholly owned subsidiary if you own stock in the parent company. The benefit would be if the subsidiary was a profitable business in its own right, and you were able to buy the shares at a discount to the market price, because then you would be able to sell them at a profit. But if the parent company owns 100% of the subsidiary, then the parent company will probably sell goods at a loss and eat the losses, because it will make more profit that way than if it sold goods at a profit and paid the subsidiary for the goods supplied.

I think a big part of the answer is inflation. Even though rates are low, you can still make a decent amount of money just by having your money do nothing at all. For example, I can deposit my money in a savings account or I can buy treasury bills, which are essentially loaning money to the government. Both are making me money, but the treasury bills are making me more money (as you pointed out). Also, as you said, CDs are essentially loans the bank is making to you. So, the bank is making money by giving you money, and you're making money by giving the bank money. It's a win-win. (Also, as @Chris H. pointed out in a comment, these rates are already so low because the yield on US treasuries has been pushed closer to zero, so banks don't have much room to maneuver to offer higher rates without losing money).

I'm just an AI bot and I don't have access to the underlying financial data or the exact methodology used by Microsoft. I can only give you what I can infer from the information available on the internet. It appears that MIRR is a short-hand notation for the internal rate of return (IRR) or the marginal rate of return (MRR) for a series of cash flows that are either revenues or expenses. The wikipedia page on Internal Rate of Return lists the following definition: IRR = sum(((PV(cashflows) - PV(costs)) / PV(cashflows)) * (1 + Yield(rate)) - 1) / (sum(PV(cashflows)) - PV(costs)) The same page defines the Marginal Rate of Return (MRR) as: MRR = sum(((PV(cashflows) - PV(costs)) / PV(cashflows)) * (1 + Yield(rate)) - 1) / (PV(cashflows) - PV(costs)) where PV is the present value and Yield is the yield (rate) of the cashflows. The MIRR function in Excel takes the same input as the IRR function, but calculates the rate that yields the highest rate of return (instead of the lowest rate of return). See the Excel Docs for more details. The formula for MIRR is identical to the one for IRR. The only difference is the way the rates are interpreted. For IRR, the rates are interpreted as the internal rate that yields the given cash flows. For MIRR, the rates are interpreted as the rate that yields the highest rate of return.

On the face, this appears a sound method to manage long run cumulative interest, but there are some caveats. Maxing out credit cards will destroy your credit rating. You will receive no more reasonable offers for credit, only shady ones. Though your credit rating will rise the moment you bring the balance back down to 10%, even with high income, it's easy to overshoot the 8 months, and then a high interest rate kicks in because of the low credit rating. Further, maxing out credit cards will encourage credit card lenders to begin cutting limits and at worse demand early payment. Now, after month 6 hits, your financial payment obligations skyrocket. A sudden jolt is never easy to manage. This will increase risk of missing a payment, a disaster for such hair line financing. In short, the probability of decimating your financial structure is high for very little benefit. If you are confident that you can pay off $4,000 in 8 months then simply apply those payments to the student loan directly, cutting out the middle man. Your creditors will be pleased to see your total liabilities fall at a high rate while your utilization remains small, encouraging them to offer you more credit and lower rates. The ideal credit card utilization rate is 10%, so it would be wise to use that portion to repay the student loans. Building up credit will allow you to use the credit as an auxiliary cushion when financial disaster strikes. Keeping an excellent credit rating will allow you to finance the largest home possible for your money. Every percentage point of mortgage interest can mean the difference between a million USD home and a $750,000 one.

Some builders -- if given the first chance to deal with the problem, instead of being presented with a bill after the fact -- will fix the problem at no charge to the homeowner. Good faith matters. My house was built by such a builder. If I have a problem that I am competent enough to diagnose and fix, I fix it myself, at my cost. If I have a problem that that I cannot diagnose and fix myself, but that I think the builder (or his subcontractor) is competent enough to diagnose and/or fix, I contact the builder (or subcontractor directly). I am willing to pay for the diagnosis and/or fix, especially if it is an aging or wear-and-tear issue, or the logical consequence of a cost-saving measure that I voluntarily chose when the house was being designed. If the problem is a plumbing problem, I contact my preferred plumber for a diagnosis and/or repair. I pay for my preferred plumber's work. On two occasions, my preferred plumber was unable to fix the problem. Both problems turned out to be installation (or testing) errors related to work done specifically for building inspections. In both cases, I paid for my preferred plumber's diagnosis, and the builder (and/or his subcontractor) fixed the problem at no additional cost to me. The diagnosis and repair work that you describe seems like a similar situation to me. (In fact, I had my builder's subcontractor replace a few prematurely damaged shingles on my roof. This repair prevented a roof leak. I noticed the problem while trimming a tree back from the roof. The shingles were damaged because the building permit implied that the tree could not be trimmed back. I'm spotting a pattern with these problems…) In my opinion, the alleged problem with the roof pitch seems like a design flaw that should have been obvious at the time you chose the house design. I expect that any corrections of this design flaw will need to come out of your remodelling budget. In the absence of further details, I doubt that either the builder or the homeowners' insurance company is responsible for it. Some builders make a point of minimizing the warranty work they pay for, regardless of its effect on the builder's reputation. I do not know which kind of builder you have. The lawyer has probably told you whether the relevant statutes of limitations have lapsed. (The statutes of limitations vary from state-to-state, and vary depending on the alleged tort.) Starting a lawsuit is likely to further damage your relationship with your builder. The following is based on my Experian credit scoring feedback and experience here in the UK over many years. (And for further information I currently hold a credit score of 999, the highest possible, with 6 credit cards.) Now I'm assuming that while there may be some differences in particulars in your case due to the difference in locality nevertheless the below should hopefully provide some broad guidelines and reasonable conclusion in your situation: Having a large number of active credit accounts may be seen as a negative. However having a large number of settled accounts should on the contrary have a positive effect on your score. As you keep your accounts mostly settled, I think having another card will not be to your detriment and should in time be beneficial. A large total credit balance outstanding may count against you. (But see the next point.) Having your total outstanding debt on all credit accounts be a smaller proportion of your total available credit, counts in your favour. This means having more cards for the same amount of credit in use, is net-net in your favour. It also has the effect of making even larger outstanding credit balances (as in point 2) to be a lower percentage of your total available credit, and consequently will indicate lower risk to lenders. It appears from my experience the higher the highest credit limit on a single card you are issued (and are managing responsibly e.g. either paid off or used responsibly) the better. Needless to say, any late payments count against you. The best thing to do then is to set up a direct debit for the minimum amount to be paid like clockwork every month. Lenders really like consistent payers. :) New credit accounts initially will count against you for a while. But as the accounts age and are managed responsibly or settled they will eventually count in your favour and increase your score. Making many credit applications in a short space of time may count against you as you may be seen to be credit reliant. Conclusion: On balance I would say get the other card. Your credit score might be slightly lower for a couple of months but eventually it will be to your benefit as per the above. Having another card also means more flexibility and more more options if you do end up with a credit balance that you want to finance and pay off over a period as cheaply as possible. In the UK the credit card companies are falling over themselves trying to offer one "interest free" or 0% "balance transfer" offers. Of course they're not truly 0% since you typically have to pay a "transfer fee" of a couple of percent. Still, this can be quite cheap credit, much much cheaper than the headline APR rates actually associated with the cards. The catch is that any additional spending on such cards are paid off first (and attract interest at the normal rate until paid off). Usually also if you miss a payment the interest rate reverts to the normal rate. But these pitfalls are easily avoided (pay by direct debit and don't use card you've got a special deal on for day to day expenses.) So, having more cards available is then very useful because you then have choice. You can roll expensive debts to the cheapest lender at your disposal for as long as they'll offer, and then simply not use that card for any purchases (while paying off the balance as cheaply as possible), meanwhile using another card for day to day expenses.

I assume your employer does standard withholding? Then what you need to do is figure what bracket that puts you in after you've done all your normal deductions. Let's say it's 25%. Then multiply your freelance income after business expenses, and that's your estimated tax, approximately. (Unless the income causes you to jump a bracket.) To that you have to add approximately 12-13% Social Security/Medicare for income between the $90K and $118,500. Filling out Form 1040SSE will give you a better estimate. But there is a "safe harbor" provision, in that if what you pay in estimated tax (and withholding) this year is at least as much as you owed last year, there's no penalty. I've always done mine this way, dividing last year's tax by 4, since my income is quite variable, and I've never been able to make sense of the worksheets on the 1040-ES.

Your retirement PLAN is a lifelong plan and shouldn't be tied to your employer status. Max out your 401(k) contribution to the maximum that your employer matches (that's a 100% ROI!) and as much as you can afford. When you leave the work force rollover your 401(k) to an IRA account (e.g.: you can create an IRA account with any of the online brokerage firms Schwab, E-Trade, Sharebuilder, or go with a brick-and-mortar firm like JP Morgan, Stifel Nicolaus, etc.). You should have a plan: How much money do you need/month for your expenses? Accounting for inflation, how much is that going to be at retirement (whatever age you plan to retire)? How much money do you need to have so that 4.5% of that money will provide for your annual living expenses? That's your target retirement amount of savings. Now figure out how to get to that target. Rule #1 Invest early and invest often! The more money you can sock away early in your career the more time that money has to grow. If you aren't comfortable allocating your investments yourself then you could go with a Targeted Retirement Fund. These funds have a general "date" for retirement and the assets are allocated as appropriate for the amount of risk appropriate for the time to retirement.

There are, of course, many possible financial emergencies. They range from large medical expenses to losing your job to being sued to major home or car repairs to who-knows-what. I suppose some people are in a position where the chances that they will face any sort of financial emergency are remote. If you live in a country with national health insurance and there is near-zero chance that you will have any need to go outside this system, you are living with your parents and they are equipped to handle any home repairs, you ride the bus or subway and don't own a car so that's not an issue, etc etc, maybe there just isn't any likely scenario where you'd suddenly need cash. I can think of all sorts of scenarios that might affect me. I'm trying to put my kids through college, so if I lost my job, even if unemployment benefits were adequate to live on, they wouldn't pay for college. I have terrible health insurance so big medical bills could cost me a lot. I have an old car so it could break down any time and need expensive repairs, or even have to be replaced. I might suddenly be charged with a crime that I didn't commit and need a lawyer to defend me. Etc. So in a very real sense, everyone's situation is different. On the other hand, no matter how carefully you think it out, it's always possible that you will get bitten by something that you didn't think of. By definition, you can't make a list of unforeseen problems that might affect you! So no matter how safe you think you are, it's always good to have some emergency fund, just in case. How much is very hard to say.

I think you're overlooking one thing: your profit/loss is not monthly. Your profit is the property that's left after the mortgage ends. Even if you have to add extra $100 every month because you rent lower than the mortgage + maintenance + taxes, after 30 years you're left with property worth ie.$200k while you've paid for it ie. 30 years * 12 months * $100 = $36k. You can rent it lower than your costs and still make a profit in the long run.

Let me just add that while you don't need to write the date received on the back of the check, you could. Why? Let's say someone was late in paying you and you wanted to document the fact that they were late. I've had late-paying customers send me a check dated on the due date but really they just pre-dated the check and sent it 60 days past-due. So let's say I want to establish and document the pattern in case it becomes a future legal issue. When you deposit or cash a check, an image of the front and back is made and the person or company who issued the check will have those images stored as part of their transaction history. (It used to be that the original, physical, cancelled check was returned to the payer, but that was another era.) So write the date received on the back next to the endorsement, endorse the check, and take a photo of the front and back (along with the postmark on the envelope) to document that they are a late payer. This way, if it ever becomes a "he said she said" issue you can easily show they have a history of paying late. If the payer looks at their check images they'll see your received date note next to the endorsement. Granted, this is a lot of trouble for a unique situation. In 20+ years of running a business I've actually had the foresight to do this a handful of times with habitual offenders, and in (only) one case did it come in handy later on. But boy was I glad to have those photos when I needed them.

The bonus share also improves the liquidity however there is some difference in treatment. Lets say a company has 100 shares, of $10 ea. The total capital of the compnay is 100*10 = 1000. Assuming the company is doing well, its share is now available in the market for $100 ea. Now lets say the company has made a profit of $1000 and this also gets factored into the price of $100. Lets say the company decides to keep this $1000 kept as Cash Reserve and is not distributed as dividends. In a share split say (1:1), the book value of each share is now reduced to $5, the number of shares increase to 200. The share capital stays at 200*5 = 1000. The market value of shares come down to $50 ea. In a Bonus share issue say (1:1), the funds $1000 are moved from Cash Reserve and transferred to share capital. The book value of each share will remain same as $10, the number of shares increase to 200. The share capital increases to 200*10 = 2000. The market value of shares come down to $50 ea. So essentially from a liquidity point of view both give the same benefit. As to why some companies issue bonus and not a split, this is because of multiple reasons. A split beyond a point cannot be done, ie $10 can be split to $1 ea but it doesn't look good to make it $0.50. The other reason is there is adequate cash reserve and you want to convert this into share holders capital. Having a larger share holders capital improves some of the health ratios for the compnay. At times bouns is used to play upon that one is getting something free.

Well, you won't be double taxed based on what you described. Partners are taxed on income, typically distributions. Your gain in the partnership is not income. However, you were essentially given some money which you elected to invest in the partnership, so you need to pay tax on that money. The question becomes, are you being double taxed in another way? Your question doesn't explain how you invested, but pretty much the options are either a payroll deduction (some amount taken out of X paychecks or a bonus) or some other payment to you that was not treated as a payroll deduction. Given that you got a 1099, that suggests the latter. However, if the money was taken out as a payroll deduction - you've already paid taxes (via your W2)! So, I'd double check on that. Regarding why the numbers don't exactly match up - Your shares in the partnership likely transacted before the partnership valuation. Let's illustrate with an example. Say the partnership is currently worth $1000 with 100 outstanding shares. You put up $1000 and get 100 shares. Partnership is now worth $2000 with 200 outstanding shares. However, after a good year for the firm, it's valuation sets the firm's worth at $3000. Your gain is $1500 not $1000. You can also see if what happened was the firm's valuation went down, your gain would be less than your initial investment. If instead your shares transacted immediately after the valuation, then your gain and your cost to acquire the shares would be the same. So again, I'd suggest double checking on this - if your shares transacted after the valuation, there needs to be an explanation for the difference in your gain. For reference: http://smallbusiness.findlaw.com/incorporation-and-legal-structures/partnership-taxes.html And https://www.irs.gov/publications/p541/ar02.html Here you learn the purpose of the gain boxes on your K1 - tracking your capital basis should the partnership sell. Essentially, when the partnership is sold, you as a partner get some money. That money is then taxed. How much you pay will depend on what you received versus what the company was worth and whether your gain was long term or short term. This link doesn't go into that detail, but should give you a thread to pull. I'd also suggest reading more about partnerships and K1 and not just the IRS publications. Don't get me wrong, they're a good source of information, just also dense and sometimes tough to understand. Good luck and congrats.

Yes, your business needs to be in the business of making money in order for you to deduct the expenses associated with it. I suppose in theory this could mean that if you take in $10,000 and spend $30,000 every year, you not only don't get a net deduction of $20,000 (your loss) but you have to pay tax on $10,000 (your revenue). However this is super fixable. Just only deduct $9500 of your expenses. Tada! Small profit.For all the gory details, including how they consider whether you have an expectation of profits, see http://www.cra-arc.gc.ca/E/pub/gl/p-176r/p-176r-e.html This "expectation of profit" rule appears to apply to things like "I sell home décor items (or home decorating advice) and therefore need to take several multi week trips to exotic vacation destinations every year and deduct them as business expenses." If you're doing woodworking or knitting in your home and selling on Etsy you don't particularly have any expenses. It's hard to imagine a scenario where you consistently sell for less than the cost of materials and then end up dinged on paying tax on revenue.

There are many different things that can happen, all or some. Taking Russia and Argentina as precedence - you may not be able to withdraw funds from your bank for some period of time. Not because your accounts will be drained, but because the cash supply will be restricted. Similar thing has also happened recently in Cyprus. However, the fact that the governments of Russia and Argentina limited the use of cash for a period of time doesn't mean that the US government will have to do the same, it my choose some other means of restraint. What's for sure is that nothing good will happen. Nothing will probably happen to your balance in the bank (Although Cyprus has shown that that is not a given either). But I'm not so sure about FDIC maintaining it's insurance if the bank fails (meaning if the bank defaults as a result of the chain effect - you may lose your money). If the government is defaulting, it might not have enough cash to take over the bank deposits. After the default the currency value will probably drop sharply (devaluation) which will lead to inflation. Meaning your same balance will be worth much less than it is now. So there's something to worry about for everyone.

Immediately move your Roth IRA out of Edward Jones and into a discount broker like Scottrade, Ameritrade, Fidelity, Vanguard, Schwab, or E-Trade. Edward Jones will be charging you a large fraction of your money (probably at least 1% explicitly and maybe another 1% in hidden-ish fees like the 12b-1). Don't give away several percent of your savings every year when you can have an account for free. Places like Edward Jones are appropriate only for people who are unwilling to learn about personal finance and happy to pay dearly as a result. Move your money by contacting the new broker, then requesting that they get your money out of Edward Jones. They will be happy to do so the right way. Don't try and get the money out yourself. Continue to contribute to your Roth as long as your tax bracket is low. Saving on taxes is a critically important part of being financially wise. You can spend your contributions (not gains) out of your Roth for any reason without penalty if you want/need to. When your tax bracket is higher, look at traditional IRA's instead to minimize your current tax burden. For more accessible ways of saving, open a regular (non-tax-advantaged) brokerage account. Invest in diversified and low-cost funds. Look at the expense ratios and minimize your portfolio's total expense. Higher fee funds generally do not earn the money they take from you. Avoid all funds that have a nonzero 12b-1 fee. Generally speaking your best bet is buying index funds from Fidelity, Vanguard, Schwab, or their close competitors. Or buying cheap ETF's. Any discount brokerage will allow you to do this in both your Roth and regular accounts. Remember, the reason you buy funds is to get instant diversification, not because you are willing to gamble that your mutual funds will outperform the market. Head to the bogleheads forum for more specific advice about 3 fund portfolios and similar suggested investment strategies like the lazy portfolios. The folks in the forums there like to give specific advice that's not appropriate here. If you use a non-tax-advantaged account for investing, buy and sell in a tax-smart way. At the end of the year, sell your poor performing stocks or funds and use the loss as a tax write-off. Then rebalance back to a good portfolio. Or if your tax bracket is very low, sell the winners and lock in the gains at low tax rates. Try to hold things more than a year so you are taxed at the long-term capital gains rate, rather than the short-term. Only when you have several million dollars, then look at making individual investments, rather than funds. In a non-tax-advantaged account owning the assets directly will help you write off losses against your taxes. But either way, it takes several million dollars to make the transactions costs of maintaining a portfolio lower than the fees a cheap mutual/index fund will charge.

You havent signed a contract, so you are only an authorised contact so you have nothing to worry about at all. Your credit reference can only be affected if you are a signed party on the contract. If you really want peace of mind, you could write to them and confirm that you are not authorised to be contacted by phone or in writing regarding the debt, and that you are not in anyway liable for the debt and that your contact details must be put beyond use, and as you are concerned, say that if they take any further action against you such as affect your credit ref etc then you will take them to 'your' local magistrates court to seek compensation. This would most likely be more than enough to stop further contact.

What you need will depend on a number of factors that aren't clear from the question. This coverage is simply called "Vacant home insurance", but not all companies are willing to offer this coverage. Unfortunately, in New York, insurers can also legally drop your standard homeowners' coverage if they become aware that your property has become vacant for 30 days or more. The Insurer's Concerns Typically, a "standard" homeowners policy will have an exclusion clause for vacant homes. The insurance company's concern is that without someone in the home, they will be at risk for break-ins, squatters and vandalism. If you've ever seen "Flip Men" on Spike, you'll know this is a serious concern (great show, by the way). They will use a risk model to calculate an estimated risk for the property (this is why a seasonal vacation home in a sparsely-populated area is often less of a concern than a family home in an urban area). If they estimate the risk to be low, some insurance companies will allow to you buy back that exclusion so that vacant properties are covered. In your case, they have probably decided that either: Your Options First, you need to find a company that is comfortable with taking on the extra risk of a vacant home. This will vary quite a bit by location, but the main ones are Farmer's (they use the Foremost brand name in New York) and Castle Rock. There are lots of insurance agencies that also advertise these products, but most of them are middlemen and use one of these two companies to actually write the coverage. Additionally, since this is a specialty policy, make sure you understand all of the details of the policy, and how they vary from a regular policy including: How to Reduce your Premium costs These are general tips from the Murray Group's website (an independent broker in NY) on how to lower the additional cost of vacant coverage: This may sound expensive, but these steps will all reduce the risk of something really bad happening when you're not there. Additionally, do you know anyone you completely trust (relative, unemployed friend) that might want to live in your old house rent-free for a while? This could work out for you if they are willing to keep the place 100% clean around the clock so that you can show the house at any time. If you have additional/specific questions, you should be able to find an independent insurance broker in your area that would be willing to advise you on your specific situation for a flat fee. Best of luck with getting the home covered and sold quickly!
