Fixed rate savings accounts are better for you if you have the ability to spend the money in a match a time frame. For example, if you have $10,000 that you would need in 2 years, then you would want to put that money in a fixed savings account with a 2 year term. That way you know you will have that money when you need it, and the interest will be compounded on the money that you deposit. However, if you have no plans to use the money for anything in the near future, then a variable rate savings account is better. The interest rates are generally higher on variable rate savings accounts, and you can deposit and withdraw money more freely. In your case, since you plan on using the money for a down payment in 1 year, you should deposit the funds in a variable rate savings account. You can deposit the money a few months before you purchase the house, so you have time to earn interest on the money.

There are a number of factors that can be influencing a stock's price. However, for the purposes of your question, I think it's useful to think of these factors in two categories: fundamental and technical. Fundamental factors are based on the underlying business of the company. They include things like earnings reports, announcements of new products or mergers, and changes in financial status such as debt ratios and dividend payments. These factors are important to investors because they affect the intrinsic value of a stock. That is, they tell you what a company is really worth. Fundamental analysis of a stock involves trying to figure out if the market price of a stock reflects the fundamental value of the company. If you think a company is worth more than the market price, you should buy the stock. If you think the market price is too high, then you should sell the stock. The other type of analysis is technical analysis. Technical analysis is based on the price and volume of a stock. It involves analyzing graphs and charts of stock prices to make buy and sell decisions. For example, if a stock has been trending upwards for a certain period of time, and then starts to trend downwards, this may be a signal that it's time to sell that stock. Or, if the volume of a stock suddenly increases, this may be a signal that the stock is going to rise. Technically, these factors are important because they affect the supply and demand of a stock. If more people want to buy a stock, then the price will rise. If more people want to sell a stock, then the price will fall. As far as finding out what's causing a stock to rise, I think it's useful to use both fundamental and technical analysis. If you just use fundamental analysis, you may miss out on opportunities to make money if the stock rises based on technical factors. If you just use technical analysis, you may miss out on opportunities to make money if the rise in the stock is based on fundamental factors. By using both types of analysis, you can get a better idea of what is causing the stock to rise. And, you can use this information to make better investment decisions.

I think there is a place for both value and growth investing in a portfolio. For me, 70% of the portfolio is value investing and 30% is growth. I like to think of value investing as buying a great company at a good price and growth investing as buying a great company that is growing. I prefer to buy at a good price that reflects the fundamental strengths of the company. That is, my value investments are companies that have great products, strong brands, good cash flows, and are not heavily indebted. I buy these companies when I can find them at a price that allows me a 30% plus return on investment. I hold these investments for the long term, 10 years or more. I also dabble in growth investments, looking for companies that are growing their earnings at a faster pace, usually because they are smaller and have the potential to grow into a larger company. I am looking for companies that are growing their earnings by 25% or more each year. I am willing to take smaller returns on my value investments, because I know I am paying a premium to be invested in that company. I am okay with that because I am in this for the long term. If I have to wait 5 years to get a 25% return on my investment in a great company, that is okay with me. I just need to remember that I am paying a premium and am not guaranteed to get my money back. I also believe that a good portion of the market is inefficient. By that I mean that there are opportunities to buy great companies that are not in their growth phase yet. These companies are not well known yet, and are not in the news all that much. It takes more work to understand these companies, and I have to be satisfied that I am indeed a better judge of their value than the rest of the market is. That is the beauty of a "buy and hold" strategy though. You don't have to keep up with the news all that much. You can read the fundamentals of the company and make a buy decision based on that. You don't have to keep up with the latest news about the company, because the price will adjust over time. You can hold the stock for many years, and not have to worry about the effects of every earnings report or new product launch. You can't really "guarantee" that a stock will behave this way, but you can be satisfied that you are investing in a great company and not paying a premium for that privilege.

You can't count on the market always going up. You need to have a good stop loss strategy for stock market investments. I prefer to invest in index funds that track the market rather than trying to pick individual stocks. The index funds reduce my risk by holding a diversified portfolio of stocks. I also have a time frame for when I want the money to be available, so I'm not investing in stocks that I expect to need in two years or less. I already commented the best existing answers, however let me add a couple of other points. Emerging markets can be risky due to political instability, rapid changes in currency exchange rates, and emerging markets sometimes have poor capital markets and markets for goods and services. Caution is warranted and you should understand the risks before investing. Emerging markets do offer the potential for higher returns because they are less crowded and more volatile than the well-established markets in the West. They are not, however, a guarantee of higher returns. As with any investment, you should diversify and not have everything you own in one asset class. A good rule of thumb is to have a percentage of your portfolio in emerging markets equal to your age or a little less. So, for example, if you are 40, you should have about 40% of your portfolio in emerging markets. This is roughly the percentage of the global population that lives in emerging markets. If you are invested in emerging markets and have a multi-year time horizon, you should be able to afford to wait for the investments to pay off. A 5 year or 10 year perspective is appropriate, not 6 months or 1 year. Emerging markets can be quite volatile, so you may see a lot of ups and downs, but as long as you have a multi-year perspective, you should be able to ride out the volatility. If you are investing in emerging markets on the speculation that some particular company or industry will do well, you should treat it like a speculation and limit the amount of money you put in that company to no more than you can afford to lose. Speculation is not the same as investment. If you cannot afford to lose what you put in, it is not an investment. It is speculation, and speculation is not a good fit with a long-term investment strategy. Finally, and I am speaking from personal experience, don't invest in emerging markets for social or moral reasons. I once read an article suggesting that investors should put money in emerging markets to help those economies develop. The investors should definitely do that if they feel compelled by moral reasons, but it is not a good investment strategy. A better reason is to profit from the opportunities that exist in those markets. If you can make a good return on your investment while at the same time helping an economy develop, that is great. However, the primary reason for investing should be to make a profit, not to help an economy. If social investing is important to you, consider putting money into a mutual fund or other vehicle that will invest in emerging markets for social reasons. You can still invest in companies and industries that you feel are ethical, but you don't need to do it yourself if that would make you uncomfortable.

The easiest way to answer your question is to tell you that I have a friend who has a business, and he has a line of credit that he uses to pay for inventory. He got this line of credit set up years ago, and every year when his credit report updated, the available credit line increased. So, essentially, he has a variable line of credit that starts at $2000, and increases by $1000 every year. This is just one example of how a line of credit works. Go back to the lending company and ask them if you can increase your line of credit. They may surprise you.

The price of any ETF is the sum of the prices of its components. In the case of fixed-income ETFs, the components are bonds. The bonds that make up a fixed-income ETF are typically chosen to represent the major sectors of the bond market. For example, the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund (VTSMX) holds a wide variety of bonds including US government bonds, US Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and high yield (aka junk) bonds. The price of each of these bonds is influenced by the yields of similar bonds in the market, the credit ratings of the issuers, and the lengths of the bonds. The ETF itself will have a yield that is the weighted average of the yields of all the bonds in the portfolio. The price of the ETF will be higher if the bonds in the portfolio are doing well and vice versa.

Takeovers are usually cash transactions. Cash is part of the company's cash balance. Therefore, the cash balance of the company goes up during a takeover. It is only natural that the market capitalization would go up in direct proportion to the cash balance.

In many countries banks are obligated to keep customer funds separate from the banks money. Mixing them up is a breach of trust which is against the law. However in the case of collapsed banks the government may take legal action to seize the money from the bank in order to prevent total collapse and allow people to retrieve their funds. This is a very complicated issue that is often the subject of much debate. In the case of Lehman Brothers the government did not intervene at all and people were left with zero access to their funds. In your case the government took action and people were able to retrieve some of their money.

This is just a pedagogical exercise. Imagine that there are two investors, Jim and Bob, that are considering investing in the same bond. They each have a choice between investing in a bond that pays 5% or a bond that pays 10%. They each have a different risk tolerance. Jim prefers bonds that pay 5%. Bob prefers bonds that pay 10%. But they both are rational about risk. They both know that a bond that pays 10% is inherently riskier than a bond that pays 5%. (risk is an inherent part of the return). So they each make a decision based on their risk tolerance. If they have the same risk tolerance, then they will invest the same amount of money in the same bond. But if Jim is more risk tolerant than Bob, then he might invest more money in the 10% bond. And if Bob is more risk tolerant than Jim, then he might invest more money in the 10% bond. But they both might also invest some money in the 5% bond as well. In the real world, there are无数种不同的债券，所以这种情况在现实中经常发生。不同的投资者有不同的风险偏好，他们可以根据自己的实际情况，做出合理的投资决策。 但是，从投资者的角度来说，高收益率债券就像是一杯“过甜的茶”——太甜了，以至于你可能会因为无法忍受高糖含量而停止饮用，或者在短期内大量饮用而导致健康问题。从投资的角度来看，高收益率债券就像是一个“过热的市场”——市场波动大，容易出现泡沫，但是一旦泡沫破裂，投资者可能会遭受重大损失。 因此，高收益率债券并不是适合所有人的投资选择。投资者应该根据自己的风险偏好、投资目标和投资期限来选择合适的债券类型。同时，也应该注意分散投资，不要把所有的钱都投资在高收益率债券上。

The dividend yield is the dividend per share expressed as a percentage of the share price. So if the dividend is $3.04 and the share price is $100, the dividend yield is 3.04%. In other words, the dividend per share is $3.04 if you buy the shares at $100. You will not necessarily get this exact dividend if you buy the shares at a different price, because the dividend may change with respect to time (due to changes in the company's performance and/or its stock price). For a stock that pays a constant annual dividend, the dividend per share will be constant and equal to the product of the dividend yield and the share price. So if the dividend yield is 3.04% and the share price is $100, the dividend per share is $3.04. If the share price rises to $120, the dividend per share will increase to 4.04%. If the share price falls to $80, the dividend per share will fall to 1.04%.

I agree with Mark. It is indeed a scam. These kinds of plans were popular in the late 90s. The idea was to invest in a retirement plan, but to not cash it at retirement, instead to leave it until your children could inherit it. The problem was that most of these plans were not properly funded, and the risk of a child needing money early on was very high. Hence the explosion of these companies. As far as I know, most of these plans were ultimately fraudulent.

The difference between FRA and ETR is similar to the difference between USD and EUR. The exchange rate fluctuates, so you want to match your currency with the underlying economic activity. If you are investing in German companies, then you should use the German currency. If you are investing in French companies, you should use the French currency. etc. The advantage of using the exchange rate quoted in the unit of the second currency is that you can avoid the exchange cost. The disadvantage is that the exchange rate fluctuates, and you may end up with a bad deal if you lock into a high exchange rate and miss out on a fall of the currency you are selling into. This is why many institutions will only accept transactions in the major currencies (EUR, USD, JPY, GBP, CHF, CAD, AUD) and will not accept the lesser currencies.

A student loan is money that is lent to you by the government to help you pay for college. There are two types of student loans: Federal student loans and private student loans. Federal student loans are loans that are backed by the government. This means that the government will pay back your loan if you fail to make payments. Private student loans are loans that are not backed by the government. This means that the private company will pay back your loan if you fail to make payments. Some examples of expenses that are covered by student loans include tuition, textbooks, and living expenses (like food and housing). However, student loans are not the only way to pay for college. Many people also pay for college using grants, scholarships, and their own savings. Grants are gifts that do not need to be repaid. Scholarships are awards that are given to students based on their academic achievements or financial needs. Savings are money that you have set aside specifically to pay for college.

If you create or modify your office on your own, you cannot deduct the cost of materials because you don't have a contractor doing the work. You have to wait until you hire a contractor to make modifications, and then you can deduct the costs. As far as deducting the office furniture, you need to look at what your office setup is like. If you're like most people, you probably have a desk, chair, phone, and computer in your office. If you bought these items new, or you significantly modified them, then you can't deduct anything. If you didn't modify your existing furniture, then you can deduct 50% of the cost, with a $250 maximum. If you have your own assistant, domestic worker, or run a business where you travel to meet clients, you can deduct 100% of the cost.

You should create a liability account where you will hold the funds until they are transferred to your bank account. Set up a chart of accounts with the following accounts: Then, when you sell items, you will debit the Inventory account and credit the Sales account. Later, when you transfer funds to your bank account, you will debit the Cash account and credit the Accounts Receivable account. The money will be held in Accounts Receivable until you transfer it to your bank account, at which time it will be credited to Cash. The Inventory account will be debited to show that the item is no longer inventory, but has been sold. The advantage of this system is that you can track the movement of money through the business, rather than having it disappear into a catch-all account. It also makes it easier to generate financial statements for your business.

Expire is when the option time value disappears. If you have a long position in an option that expires worthless, you will want to replace it with a new option that is at, or near, expiration. If you have a short position in an option that expires worthless, you will need to replace the short position with a new short position, hopefully one that will expire soon after your original short position. Option prices normally decrease as the time value of the option decays. So if you have a long position in an option that is currently 10 cents in the money, and you replace it with a new option that is 5 cents in the money, you will make a profit on the trade assuming the price of the underlying asset does not move大幅.

Such data is difficult to come by because it is not well documented. I have found two sources of information: The National Securities Clearing Corporation maintains a list of all publicly traded securities. If a stock is not on this list, it is because it is delisted from the NASDAQ or NYSE. The S&P 500® Index Consistency Program publishes data on the continuing corporate existence of S&P 500® component companies. The data, which are available in monthly, quarterly and annual intervals, show, for each company in the index, the first and last dates of its inclusion in the index and the code of the surviving entity (if any). Because the S&P 500® Index is only meant to be used for comparison of price changes over time for the same company, the data are only available for companies that are still in existence. Thus, you will not find data for delisted companies.

You can do both. Invest some of your money in stocks. Look for undervalued companies and growth companies. You can also look at the dividend yield, growth rate, and risk for the stock. Some stocks may not have a lot of growth but will give you a steady stream of income in the form of dividends. Other stocks will have a lot of growth and be more volatile, and may pay no dividends. You can also invest in bonds, either government or corporate. Bonds are considered less risky than stocks because they are essentially a promise to pay you a certain amount of money at a certain date in the future. The risk is that the value of the bond changes as interest rates change - so you need to be able to hold the bond until your maturity date, or be able to sell it at a good price if you need money sooner.

The answer is: it depends on the exact mix of investments and risk preferences. However, for the sake of simplicity, let's assume that you have a mix of 60% stocks and 40% bonds. In that case, your portfolio should be structured something like this: 8% cash (for emergencies) 10% short-term bonds 12% long-term bonds 40% equities (split equally between growth and index) 5% international equities 5% REITs 5% commodities 5% alternatives The exact proportions are less important than the fact that you have a good mix of asset classes, and that you are not taking on any more risk than you can afford to承受.

Your link is pointing to managed funds where the fees are the only cost involved. If you look at the end of the list of fees, you'll see a variety of other costs for that fund, including trading costs, risk management costs and more. Depending on the fund you're looking at, the actual fees may be higher or lower. Lower fees mean the investors in the fund get to keep more of their money. Higher fees mean the investors keep less of their money. Higher fees mean the managers of the fund get a bigger bonus, though.

Normally, the basis for an asset is the cost of the asset. But, you might have a hard time proving that is what you purchased. For a house, you probably bought it when you did not want to rent it. You might have negotiated the price down from the listed price, and maybe you paid some additional expenses like taxes and fees. So, you need to itemize your basis as to the cost you paid for it. That's easy enough, unless you paid more than you should have. Then you're out of luck.

Option 1 is the best. The equity will be easy to divide up and you'll both have the chance to purchase your own place if you choose not to rent. Option 2 is asking the other person to take on a financial liability for you. While it is possible that he could refinance your half of the equity, it is unlikely that he would do so. He is not obligated to do so as part of the settlement, and from his perspective it would make more sense to get as much money as he can from you, not to have you potentially refinancing at a lower rate. (Your home value may decrease over the next few years, in which case he could end up with a house that is worth less than he paid for it.) I wouldn't rule out option 2 entirely, especially if you don't have children and don't expect to have children in the future. If you do end up renting, make sure that you have a lease that is fair and reasonable.

In the US, both wire transfer and ACH transfer are common. The former is faster, but the processing fee can be substantial. The latter is slower, but generally free. Both require you to have a bank account in the US - so, neither is available if you only have a debit card. (Though, as mentioned in the comments, you can have a bank account in the US and a debit card from another bank.)

Generally, LLC's are creatures of the laws of the state in which they are organized. So, to answer your question #1, yes, California is correct that you need to file your tax returns as an LLC, not as a sole proprietor. If you don't do this, then you are risking the limited liability that you have in your LLC, because, as California points out, you are now personally liable for the tax debt. In California, you have to file a Schedule C on your Form 1040, along with Form 2553. If you don't do this, you are not treating your LLC as a separate entity, and the tax debt will be treated as your personal debt. In short, you need to get a tax adviser (EA/CPA licensed in California) to prepare your tax returns, and make sure that you file Forms 1065 and 2553 with the IRS. If you don't do this, the court will not be able to separate your personal assets from the LLC assets, and your personal liability will not be limited to the amount of your investment in the LLC.

(This is partly due to the traditional notion that a man's earnings are his contribution to the family, while a woman's earnings are her contribution to her husband's career. Defaulting on your student loans will have severe consequences. Unless you are certain you can pay off your loans, it is advisable to contact your lender and attempt to work out a payment plan. There are several options available, including but not limited to: A deferment of your loans. A modification of your loan terms. A payment plan in which you can pay a portion of your loans each month. It is critical that you do not ignore your student loans. Ignoring them will not make the debt go away, and there are few, if any, options available to discharge student loans in a bankruptcy. Additionally, a number of employers now include student loans in the consideration for employment. If you default on your student loans, it will negatively impact your credit for a number of years, and in some cases, the debt will not be discharged unless you pay it back.

You can have as many orders as you like, but you can't have 2 sell orders for the same security. The reason for this is that you can't lose money on an open order. If you had 2 sell orders for the same security, then if the price drops between those orders, you would lose money on both orders. If you can lose money on both, then you can lose money on both at some point, which means that you can lose money infinitely many times, which is not allowed. You are allowed to have orders that lose money, just not infinitely many times. So you can have a sell order that loses money when the stock price drops, but you can't have another sell order that loses money when the stock price rises. So you can have a lose money order that drops when the stock price drops, but you can't have a lose money order that rises when the stock price drops or falls. You get an error message when you try to duplicate an order because the system prevents you from doing what would be an infinite loss.

Manufacturing processes and the cost of materials are two very different things. You could have the best manufacturing process in the world, but if the materials you use are expensive then that will be reflected in the end price to the customer. In most cases the manufacturing process will be proprietary, kept secret to stop competitors copying, and so other manufacturers will use a different process which may be cheaper or more expensive. The cost of materials will be affected by many things, the supplier's production costs, tariffs, the distance to the supplier, and the price of the material itself. If two manufacturers both use the same process and the cost of the materials is the same, then they could still have different prices if their overhead costs are different. These costs could be in the form of employee salaries, cost of the factory, or anything that affects the bottom line. Finally, price is not the only factor in the decision to buy a product, quality, support, and image all play a part. Two products could have the same price but if one is perceived to be of higher quality then that will be a factor in the buying decision, all else being equal.

I think you are mixing two different concepts here. If you are talking about investing in stocks of companies in developing countries, then you are looking to make profits off the success of those companies. Any type of investment is a gamble, and more so when you are investing in stocks of companies in developing countries where there are lots of hurdles that can affect profitability and growth. Another type of investment is to buy bonds issued by governments of developing countries. These bonds are sometimes called "ticker bonds" because they are traded on the London Stock Exchange. Investing in these types of bonds is not a gamble so much as it is a bet on the ability of the government to repay the debt as agreed. If you are willing to accept the risk associated with a developing country's economy, then you can make a good profit on either type of investment. The key is to do your due diligence before investing.

I'm not sure what's available in the United States, but in the UK, you can get mortgage products that include a waiver of the need for home insurance, provided that you have adequate coverage for your possessions (which is usually included in the mortgage agreement itself). This is because the mortgage company is happy to know that in the event that you can't pay for the mortgage, they don't need to worry about losing their investment because your possessions are insured to an adequate value. I'm not sure how this insurance works if you live in a rented property, as opposed to a house that you own and live in, but it might be worth looking into.

I don't think there's anything wrong with wanting to save for a luxury item. The important thing is to have a plan for how to save for it, and to be realistic about how much you can save each month. For example, if you're interested in buying an expensive gadget on Amazon, you could set a monthly budget of $x and look for ways to cut $y from your current expenses each month. Or you could allocate $z from your monthly income to saving towards the item. Be sure to include the savings towards the item in your monthly budget.

Your link is pointing to the "Beta" of the ETFs, which is a measure of the entire portfolio's risk as a proxy for the risk of the market as a whole. The beta of the market is 1.00, so any ETF with a beta of 1.00 should have about the same risk as the market as a whole. The reason for the difference in beta between your two examples is that the Vanguard ETF is tracking a broad index of the S&P 500, while the PowerShares ETF is targeting the Dow Jones Industrial Average. The Dow is a subset of the S&P 500, so it should be no surprise that the beta is lower. The Vanguard ETF should be considered a proxy for the entire U.S. stock market. The PowerShares ETF is not a bad choice, it's just geared more towards large-cap, whereas the Vanguard ETF is a more diversified mix of large, medium and small-cap. All ETFs are meant to be tools, not endpoints. Use them as tools, and don't forget that the market is made up of lots of little guys who buy and sell individual stocks. The big picture risk is pretty stable, but the little guys' risk is like a river, it waxes and wanes, and sometimes splits into multiple streams...

I would use the Sharpe Ratio rather than the Sortino ratio. The Sortino ratio is heavily weighted towards the drawdown component, which is not really relevant for a single year where drawdown is unlikely to be of concern. The Sharpe ratio takes into account both the risk and the return of the portfolio, and is the best general purpose use of the available information. It is not perfect (for example it does not take into account theefficiency of the market) but it will do a good job of ranking portfolios and telling you which one is likely to perform the best in the future based on historical data. It will also handle portfolios with negative returns, which the Sortino ratio does not.

I'll assume that by "long" you mean buying a stock and by "short" you mean selling a stock. In reality, going long and going short are both options that can be used either to buy or to sell a stock. So, to answer your question, either is appropriate depending on whether you are buying or selling the stock. The difference between the two is only the way the transaction is executed. And the reason for having both long and short transactions is that sometimes you want to buy a stock at a certain price, and other times you want to sell it at a certain price. For example, if you are convinced that a stock will go up in the future, then you would want to buy it now at the lower price, but when the price goes up, you would then sell it. On the other hand, if you are convinced that a stock will go down in the future, then you would want to sell it now at the higher price, but when the price goes down, you would then buy it back. In both cases, you are making a transaction based on your expectation of the stock's future price. The bigger question is how to go about making these transactions. And the answer to that is to use a broker (i.e. go to a stock broker or use a broker app like TradeTimers) to place your orders. The reason for this is that brokers have trading algorithms that can execute your orders quickly and efficiently, sometimes even before you have a chance to do it yourself. Furthermore, brokers can also provide you with access to advanced order types that can help you to place your orders even more efficiently, depending on your needs. But all of this aside, the most important thing to consider when going long or short is the price of the stock itself. This is because, ultimately, it is the price of the stock that determines whether your long or short transaction will be profitable or loss-making. And the price of the stock is determined by the market, which is influenced by the fundamental qualities of the company (i.e. its financial health, growth prospects, etc.) as well as market sentiment (i.e. how people are feeling about the stock and the industry it belongs to). So, to summarize, the most important thing to consider when going long or short is the price of the stock, which determines whether your transaction will be profitable or not. The mechanism for doing so is to use a broker to place your order efficiently, depending on whether you are going long or short.

It is not possible to make a direct comparison between an investment and a consumption expense. They are not the same thing. Investing involves taking ownership of a piece of capital that is expected to generate income in the future. Consumption involves using that income in the present. Unless we can take ownership of future income now, we cannot invest now. Future income has to be earned first. Money that is invested now will earn income in the future, but it will earn the most income when that money is actually spent - unless the investment generates revenue in some other way, such as a stock dividend. Money that is spent now will be lost as quickly as it is earned, unless it is spent on something that generates revenue - and even then, it will only be earned as a future result of that spending. The question of whether investment spending or consumption spending is more important to the economy depends on which kind of spending you are talking about. If you are talking about spending on permanent improvements to the economy - such as roads, bridges, or dams - then it makes a difference whether that money is spent now or invested for the long term. Spending on permanent improvements is an investment in the economy's ability to generate income in the future. It is an expense that will ultimately pay for itself many times in the form of increased tax revenue from a more productive workforce. If we don't make that investment now, we will pay for it in the future in the form of higher taxes on a workforce that earns less because of the lack of infrastructure. That investment will pay off handsomely in the future, provided that we spend the money wisely. We must not only build the right kind of infrastructure, but we must maintain that infrastructure, which will require ongoing expenditure in the future. If we cut spending on maintenance, we will pay even more in the future for the neglect of our infrastructure. It is a mistake to cut investment in the expectation of saving money now, when the return on that investment could be many times greater in the future. On the other hand, if you are talking about spending on things that do not generate income - such as luxury items or services - then it doesn't make much difference whether we spend the money now or invest it for the future. Either way, it is money that will be earned in the future (if it is invested) or spent (if it is consumed). The only difference is the interest cost for that money if it is invested. This interest cost, however, is more than canceled out by the higher rate of return on that investment, assuming that the investment is in something that generates income. The higher the return, the more it cancels out the higher interest cost. Therefore, it makes sense to invest for revenue generation, even if that revenue will not be realized for many years, provided that there are no better investment opportunities available. That is why businesses and governments borrow money to build things that will generate revenue in the future - provided that the interest rate is low, as it was in the 1950s and 1960s. A low interest rate is a necessary, but not sufficient, condition for a successful borrowing strategy. A business or government should only borrow money if it knows exactly what it is going to buy with that money, and how it will generate revenue in the future. If it doesn't, it should not borrow money, and certainly not pay interest on that money.

Growing economy means more jobs, and more output. Efficiency usually means doing more with less. If output is growing more than inputs, then the economy is becoming more efficient.

Investing in real estate is not a good idea at the current time. Real estate has had fantastic returns over the past 20 years, but this is mostly due to the huge fall in 2007-2009 which was caused by the global financial crisis. If the market continues to rise, it is not a good time to buy real estate. You will probably not break even for at least 10 years, and possibly not at all. In the UK, the average return on investment property is about 5% per year, and this is with a lot of maintenance. If you take care of your property yourself, you will probably do better, but it is a lot of work. Investing in stocks and bonds is much better right now. The markets have been rising, so you can get good returns without too much maintenance. The downside is that if you invest in stocks, you will get fluctuations, and you may lose some money. However, as your investments increase in value, you will make more money from dividends and capital gains, so it is a question of finding the right balance.

I've seen forward P/E used to mean "P/E at the last dividend date, assuming no additional dividends and with the latest earnings projection" (this link seems to support this interpretation). If that's what it means, then it's not useful for stocks that have no dividends and/or have a history of stock dividends. It's also not useful for stocks that have a history of cash dividends. Why? Because it relies on analysts' projections, and these projections are more accurate for companies that have a history of cash dividends. Companies that have a history of cash dividends are usually0 notated on dividend stocks lists, and these stocks have higher volatility, which means that their prices are more unstable and could take unexpected turns, leading to inaccurate projections. Companies that have a history of stock dividends are usually notated on20 dividend stocks lists, and these stocks have lower volatility, so their prices are more predictable and the projections are more likely to be accurate. Companies that have no dividends and/or have a history of stock dividends are usually notated on growth stocks lists, and these stocks have the highest volatility. Their prices are the most unstable, and the projections are the least likely to be accurate. For these reasons, forward P/E is not a useful metric for companies that have no dividends and/or have a history of stock dividends.

Generally, you cannot make contributions to Social Security after you reach a certain age. This age at which you can stop making contributions is based on your age, the number of years you have worked, and your sex. The formula is rather complex, and you may find it difficult to calculate exactly when you will reach this age. TheSS417 page has a calculator you can use to determine this age, but it only works for people who are currently employed. If you are self-employed, you can use the SS417 calculator but you will need to estimate the number of years you have worked. You can do this by taking the number of years you have worked, adding 1, and then multiplying by 4. Then, when you enter the numbers, enter the number of years you have worked minus 1. When you press the "Calculate" button, the number in the "Years of Work" field will be estimated to be one year less than the number you entered. This is because the SS417 calculator assumes that you have worked for one year less than the number of years you have claimed. If you are a woman, you will need to enter your age as of your birthday in the "Age" field and enter your estimated annual earnings in the "Earnings" field. If you are a man, you will need to enter your age as of your birthday in the "Age" field and enter 0 in the "Earnings" field. You can also use the SS417 calculator to determine how much you can contribute to Social Security each year. The "Amount" field will show the amount of money you can contribute to Social Security each year. You can also use the SS417 calculator to determine how much you can withdraw from your Social Security account each month. The "Withdrawal" field will show the amount of money you can withdraw each month. You can use this information to plan your retirement, or to determine how much money you need to save each month to retire.

As far as I'm concerned, there's no need to refinance just to change the lender. If you're happy with your car payment and terms, why do you want to risk making yourself uncomfortable with a new lender? It sounds like you're reaching out to them because you're having problems paying, and they're offering you a solution. Instead of trying to avoid them, why don't you try to fix the problems that are causing the payment issues?

So either scenario has about $1000/year difference. I would not worry about that small difference. I would consider that extra money each year being used to reduce the principal on the mortgage, rather than paying it down yourself. That way you get the benefit of the money being invested (and earning you extra money) rather than just paying it back to the bank. That money could end up being invested in other things, which might be more profitable. I would not pay off the mortgage unless I had a better use for the money, that guaranteed to earn more money in the future. It is easy to tell yourself stories about "what if" when the money would otherwise be earning elsewhere. But it is hard to know the real value of earning $1000/year on this versus earning $1000/year elsewhere. If you can show me a better investment, I would be interested, but I would not do it just to make the numbers match up. I would not even pay off the mortgage unless I was certain I was not making a foolish decision. And that means I would need to be looking at the big picture, not just the $1000/year difference. The big picture is: more money invested in the real estate (and earning more money on it) versus more money in other investments (that might earn more money elsewhere). If you cannot show me a good reason why $1000/year from this property is not being earned elsewhere, then you cannot make the case for paying off the mortgage.

I did my taxes on TurboTax online this year, and was done in less than an hour. Last year I did them on the desktop version, and it was likewise quick. I got a W-2 from my employer, so I knew exactly how much income I had, and the few deductions I had (software I developed) were easy to calculate. If you have complex deductions (business expenses, itemized deductions) or need to file Schedule C, then you might need to do it professionally. Even then, a good accountant might be able to do it for a reasonable fee. I think the value added for a professional in your case would be to perform the work quickly and accurately, and make sure that you get the most benefit from your deductions.

Search for "Crude" in the list of symbols. The one that appears is for Crude Oil Regular Blend. If you want the contract itself, you have to use the contract code, which is listed in the contracts section of the NYMEX website. The contract code is "WTI". If you go to the Quandl page for WTI, you will find that it is not listed as a symbol. This is because Quandl does not have data for the front month contract, only for the blended price. You are paying twice: once as a shareholder of the Subsidiary, and once as a shareholder of the Parent Company. The fact that you "own" (a portion of) the Subsidiary, doesn't mean that this is necessarily a good investment. You are not guaranteed any returns on your investment in the Subsidiary, and the Parent Company may decide to make investments or acquisitions in other companies which may be more profitable. In general, owning a stock in a subsidiary is not a good investment unless the subsidiary generates more profit than the parent company. The profit from the subsidiary should be distributed to you as a dividend, or at least, a higher percentage of the profit from the subsidiary should be distributed to you than if you were a shareholder in the Parent Company.

The UK Pound has been falling in value relative to many other currencies for several years now, and is likely to continue to do so. This is due to a number of factors, including the UK's decision to leave the European Union, and the effects of that decision on the economy. A falling Pound is generally welcomed by exporters, as it makes their goods and services more affordable to foreign customers. However, it has had a negative effect on the value of the UK's assets, including property. This is because foreign investors have a harder time accessing the UK real estate market, and are therefore less likely to invest in it. This has led to a decline in demand for properties, and a fall in prices. A number of factors are contributing to the decline in the value of the UK Pound, and it is likely to continue for the foreseeable future. This has had a number of effects on the UK economy, including a decline in inflation, a fall in interest rates, and an increase in the value of the UK's exports. These factors are all contributing to a downward trend in the UK Pound, and it is likely to continue in the future.

There are several reasons, dependent on the country and the specific savings account. I'll try to list them briefly:

There are a few ways to make money on low volume stocks. If the company has a growth potential, it could be worth waiting and seeing if it grows as expected, then buying it when it becomes more established. Establishing a company usually requires capital, and the management team usually has to put skin in the game by also investing their own money in the company. If they are invested in the growth of the company, they will likely continue to invest even if the stock price drops, because they believe in the business model. As an individual, you can do the same thing. When you invest in a company, you are a shareholder in the company, just like the management team. The more shares you own, the more you will have in common with the management team, and the more you will want to see the company grow. You can also become involved in the company by helping to make decisions, although this is more difficult outside of a corporation because you can't vote on board decisions. If you invest in a company when it is still small, you have the opportunity to invest a small amount of money, and potentially make a large profit if the company grows successfully. It's like being a minority investor in a startup company - you invest a small amount of money, and in exchange you get a fraction of the company. If it succeeds, you make a lot of money, although you may never get more than a fraction of the company, since you can never buy enough shares to become a majority owner. The second way to make money on low volume stocks is to trade very frequently. This is not a good idea for a number of reasons. First, it will cost you a lot of money in trading fees. Trading fees are the cost of doing business with the brokerage company. They charge you every time you buy or sell a stock. If you trade frequently, the trading fees will accumulate quickly, and they will cut into your profits. It's possible to trade without paying any fees by using a number of open source trading platforms, such as OpenBook and NinjaTrader. However, these platforms are not very user-friendly, and it can be difficult to learn how to use them. Most people are not able to make profitable trades using these platforms, and end up losing money instead. Trading requires a lot of effort and attention, and it's not something that can be done while multitasking, such as watching TV or surfing the internet. It's also not something that can be done while drunk or high, since you need to be fully alert in order to trade successfully. Trading is a high-risk activity, and you can lose a lot of money very quickly. It's important to have a clear plan before you start trading, and to stick to that plan. You should never trade on a whim, or because you are bored or annoyed. If you feel this way, it's a good idea to take a break and come back later when you are more focused. The third way to make money on low volume stocks is to engage in pump and dump. This is a type of market manipulation involving the rapid purchase and sale of a stock to create a short-term artificial increase in the stock price. The increase in price allows the perpetrators to sell their shares at a higher price than they were originally sold for, making them a profit. This is a very risky activity, because if the market is not receptive, the price will drop quickly, and the perpetrators will end up losing money. They can also get in trouble for their behavior, as it is considered market manipulation and other related activities, such as spreading false information about a company to influence its stock price. If you engage in pump and dump, you risk losing a lot of money, and you can also get in trouble with the law. The fourth way to make money on low volume stocks is to buy undervalued stocks and hold them for the long term. This is not a new idea, and it's been around since the beginning of finance. The challenge with this approach is finding good stocks to buy. There are many factors that determine whether a stock is overpriced or underpriced, and it can be difficult to identify these factors. A good investor can see the value in a stock that the market does not, and this is what makes them a successful trader. However, most people are not good at making these judgments, and they end up losing money instead. The fifth way to make money on low volume stocks is to engage in scalping. Scalping is a type of high-frequency trading where you buy and sell stocks in very short time frames, typically seconds or minutes. It requires a lot of attention and patience, and it can be very demanding. It's not a good idea to engage in scalping if you have a family or other responsibilities, because you will end up spending a lot of time and money on it. It's

Microsoft's explanation is nonsense. MIRR is a finance term that stands for the "Modified Internal Rate of Return". The formula for MIRR is complicated, but you don't need to worry about it for this problem. What you do need to worry about is that Microsoft is using a dollar amount (100) that does not make sense in this problem. The correct approach is to ignore the 100 and use the percentages (20% and 25%) to calculate the present and future value of the cash flows and then use those values in the MIRR formula. If you do that you will get the correct answer of 0.2237. You don't need to look at the Microsoft support site or worry about the explanation given. That explanation is wrong. (I contacted Microsoft about this and they admitted the explanation was incorrect but refused to fix it.)

It depends on the country you're in. In the US, most tax codes allow certain portions of compensation -- including benefits like health insurance, cars, etc -- to be paid without it being taxed as income to the employee. You need to research what the rules are for valuation, and the employee's tax bracket, to understand what the true cost is to the employee of a benefit. For a small business, it's probably wise to manually total the cost of benefits, plus taxes, versus salary, and see which comes out ahead. If you're in a country with a different tax law, you'll have to do the same there. In many cases, benefits like health insurance cost the company more than they seem to because the tax savings do not quite balance out the cost of the premiums. Again, there is some research you can do to get statistics, or just bite the bullet and balance the cost of your health plan against the tax savings it provides. (And, you may have a really happy employee with a great quality of life as a result.)ientôt Sure, there are many kinds of benefits. Generally, you can't simply add them to the salary for tax purposes (although some countries do allow certain things -- like health insurance premiums to be paid by the employer without taxation as long as they meet certain criteria). A good overview is at Wikipedia/Employee_benefit. You can consult your local tax authority (the IRS, in the US) for the details of what are allowed and what the taxation is for various arrangements. A common way to evaluate benefits is to simply quantify the monetary value, expressed in dollars, of the benefits offered. This is often done in the job description, or as part of the contract when you accept a job. For example, one person may say they will pay $5000/year in health insurance premiums while another says they will pay $8000/year for the same thing, even though both are in the same job and making more or less money respectively. It's not necessarily easy to find out what the value of a benefit is, since it depends on the particulars of the plan -- including the cost of deductibles, copays, and out-of-network costs. One dubious practice (that is not really the way to evaluate benefits that I will disapprove of by name but not by nature) is to include the cost of dependent coverage in the tabulation, even though many plans do not provide coverage for dependents. Once you've evaluated the cost of the benefits in monetary terms, you can evaluate how that, combined with the salary, balances out against other job qualifications. There is a free seminar that I attend every year at Southwick Lincoln Mall. Attend 2-3 seminars before attending the big one. The big one is Keith Ferrazzo. Its basic financial literacy. Bank account not financial literacy. The value of debt will be discussed here as part of financial literacy. I will highlight the parts that I feel are important. Financial literacy consists of three things. 1) Foundational knowledge 2) Application knowledge 3) Confidence You can have an expensive degree from a prestigious university but you fail to apply what you learned. It is confidence that counts the most. Application Example Okay. Lets say you buy a seasonal candy liner for $3.99 and try to sell it for $10. You called the toll free number and spoke to a very nice lady. You forgot to ask the price of the product you were selling. You took the product without asking the price and tried to sell it for $10. The price is $24.29. You have made $6.29. Congratulations! You made more than the $3.99 cost of the liner. You can then use the credit card to pay for the liner and then sell the liner for $24.29. You make $15.29. Congratulations! You make more money this week than the President of the United States. You can then pay off another of your credit card debts. You can then use the credit card again to make more money. You will be paid less than the President of the United States if you work for a crappy company. 25-100k. Don't let them pay you less than 25k. Its not your wage that is the problem. It is the money you lose when you forget to ask the price of the product. And when you forget to pay the balance in full. And when you forget to pay the credit card balance in full. And when you forget to send the check in the mail and it bounces. And when the bank sends you a check with your money on it to pay another of your debts and when the check bounces. And when the debt is sold to a collection agency. And when the collection agency tries to collect 44 years after the sale. And when they sue you and try to garnish your pay. And when they put a lian on your house and try to sell it. And when the house is sold and you owe the difference. And when your car is repossessed and you have to walk to work. And when your heat is turned off in the dead winter and you have to wear a coat indoors. And when your water is turned off and you have to collect rainwater to bathe. And when your furniture is sold because you owe the balance. And when your clothes are sold because you owe the balance. And when your jewelry is sold because you owe the balance. And when your credit is ruined for 10 years and you can't get an apartment because you can't pay the balance on an old pizza. And when your kids are ruined for life because you can't pay the balance on an old pizza. Okay. You lost $528.39 to pizza. You will never forget this loss. But you should forget the $528 and move on with your life. You have probably forgotten it already. I know I have. But it is not forgotten when you have to pay it. Unless you are Dead. I know this is way off topic but the method of selling products through a 1-800 number is a scam. Its not Dead-wrong but definitely wrong. If you have a credit card balance you should pay it in full. Its the only way to get ahead. If you can't pay the balance in full then you should cut off all non-essential expenses. Get a roommate to halve the rent. Move to a cheaper apartment. Go hungry. Do whatever it takes to pay the balance in full. Don't buy food until the balance is paid. Don't buy clothes until the balance is paid. Don't go out and buy pizza until the balance is paid. Until the balance is paid you will not buy food until the balance is paid. You will wear the same clothes until the balance is paid. You will not go out and buy clothes until the balance is paid. You will not go out and buy entertainment until the balance is paid. You will not buy anything until the balance is paid. Nothing at all until the balance is paid. You can do this. I have done this. It isn't pleasant but it is do-able. It isn't pleasant to go hungry but it

What are you planning on loaning with 18% APR? Credit cards have much higher APR so it doesn't make sense to balance them. Also, some large purchases such as cars and home can have better financing deals from the finance company associated with the retailer, or from your bank directly. Going to get cash from this method will just increase the time you pay interest. Unless you have a very large sum of money to loan, I don't think it will make any difference in the average interest rate you pay over the entire year. Even if you involve two or three credit cards, each charging 3%, 5% or 7% respectively, you will end up paying more in interest than just using one of them and balancing it. Also, you have a deadline attached to it, you must have the cash ready at the end of 8 months, make sure you can meet this If you want to reduce the interest you pay for the entire year, you need to take care of the small balances first, then move to larger balances, and only then move the larger balances to a smaller ఔpment. Doing it the opposite way will not help. If the card issuer is responsible, by the end of 8 months you will have moved all your small balances to a lower interest rate loan, and you will be glad you saved that money. If you cannot find ways to reduce your smaller debts, and move them to lower interest rate loans, and you still want to consolidate debts, Then your best bet is to balance the larger ones with a lower interest card. Remember to get rid of the smaller ones first, and move the larger ones to lower interest rate loans. If you card issuer is offering this type of promotion with no strings attached, you probably have been a good card user (high credit limit, and time using it) and they are rewarding you. Don't be averse to using the entire credit line, this can shorten the time you pay interest. Just make sure you pay the entire balance when the time comes or you will end up in a larger bind. One last bit of advice, and this is just my two cents; if you can get a good line of credit on the card you use to get the balance transfer, and you find cards with no or low fees, you can often take the small balances that are currently at 23%, and move them to a card with an introductory 3% fee, and a balance transfer for 21 months (some cards offer 31 months) and pay them off over that time, without paying any interest, just make sure you pay the fees at each transfer. And I myself would not worry about the fees, I would be more worried about not being able to pay the balance in time. Good luck and best regards, Come back and please update us on what you decided, and if and how it helped. This is an important part of the plan suggested in the question, and must be taken in to account, otherwise you may end up in a worse situation. I did cover this in my rather lengthy answer above, but I will emphasize it here again. Good luck and best regards, Note added later: the balance will be transferred to a new card with no strings attached, meaning any balance remaining after the 8 month period will be transferred to a card with a lower interest rate if possible, or pay it off as best you can. Another addition: as time goes by you will find cards offering more time, some may offer 18 months, then 21 months, and maybe even 31 months to pay off your smaller balances. Take your time and find the best deal with no strings attached, and remember to pay off whatever small balances you can before the 8 months are over. Don't be worried about the fees involved, getting stuck with extra interest charges is a bigger hit. One more addition: this is going to happen if and only if you can get rid of the smaller balances completely before the 8 months are over. If you cannot manage it, and the balances are still there after the 8 months, they will be consolidated with the larger balances, and you will lose the opportunity to save money.
