While the US Securities and Exchange Commission ("SEC") allows for the development of "industry, group, or composite indices" without SEC registration, the caveat is usually that such indices are licensed works and must be trademarked. The broad strokes of the Investable Real Estate Indexes you are looking for are permissible, but you need to put a trademark on them to protect usage. The details of the indexes and the data behind them would dictate the level of protection that is needed. On the publicly-available, data-driven, broad-strokes United States Real Estate Industry & Large Commercial Properties Index, for example, the data is de-aggregated by state and then by property type (e.g., Office, Industrial, Retail, Apartment Houses, Hospitality). The index values are a simple sum of their respective market capitalizations. The market capitalization of a property is the sales or transacted price, times one million, times the U.S. Federal Income Tax on Gains and Losses Muffins. This index and its data is copyrighted but not trademarked, and the data can be licensed for use in research, print, and electronic forms. The index itself can be licensed for use in financial products. On the national level, the major real estate indexes move in tandem, and the national indexes and the state/property type indexes can be used for products that span the entire United States or specific regions within it, respectively. Regional products would use a subset of the national indexes, and products that are limited to a specific property type would use the corresponding index. The national indexes are also useful when developing exposure to the real estate industry as a whole. The United States Real Estate Industry & Large Commercial Properties Index, and similar indexes developed by the National Association of Real Estate Appraisers ("NAREA"), can be used as the basis for various financial products. Financial products that invest in publically-traded real estate investment trusts ("REITs") can be especially effective if the underlying indexes and the REIT indexes move in tandem. The NAREA also has indexes that define the various sectors within the real estate industry that can be used to create products that span specific areas within the industry. For more information on the indexes, see the white paper, "Real Estate Indexes As A New Asset Class." Regarding the matter of data sources, the National Society of Professional Real Estate Appraisers ("NSPREA") maintains the Data Source Standards for appraisers that define standard data sources for various property types in the United States. The Data Source Standards are updated every 5 years and have been recently updated for 2014-2019. These data sources are not industry indexes, but they are valuable for anyone developing industry indexes. ## Conclusion The answer to your question is yes, there are such indexes. You can use these indexes as inspiration for products that span the entire real estate industry in the United States or specific regions and property types within it. You will, however, have to trademark the names unless you are working for a company that owns the names and intend to distribute products in the United States or to U.S. residents. Also, the data sources for the indexes provide a route to develop other financial products. For more information on the data sources, see the article, Data Source Standards For Appraisers Updated. Also, the NAREA (which created the indexes mentioned in the question) is a good source of information on industry metrics, which answer questions such as, "What is the size of the U.S. real estate industry?" The NAREA is also home the Center for U.S. Real Estate which produces various reports and data-driven articles and papers regarding the real estate industry. --- Note: The National Society of Professional Real Estate Appraisers ("NSPREA") and the National Association of Real Estate Appraisers ("NAREA") are different organizations although they have similar names. The NSPREA is mentioned in the article on data sources, and the NAREA is mentioned as the source of the indexes mentioned in the question. The NSPREA was formed in 2007 as the result of a merger of the Society of American Appraisers and the American Society of Industrial Realtors. The NSPREA offers various credentials including the Certified General Real Estate Appraiser™. The NAREA, on the other hand, appears to focus more on industry metrics and legal matters although it also offers credential certifications. The NAREA also appears to have more members and more resources as evidenced by its web site which is more professionally done and has more articles and research on it. The NSPREA on the other hand maintains a smaller and less informative web site. The NSPREA and NAREA also appear to have differing memberships categories and fees. In general, the NAREA appears to be the larger and more well-funded organization with a more commercial focus whereas the NSPREA appears to have a more residential focus and offers more credentials. Note, however, that these are two different

It is not possible for a typical bank mortgage to lose money if the house appreciates. The risk is in the upside by design – the house can appreciate so much that you owe more than the house is worth, and the bank can seize the house and sell it, taking whatever remains after paying off your loan. If you care enough about the house to collect the full appreciation, you should invest the money you would have otherwise used for a downpayment in a way that provides a positive expected return, which is difficult to do (possibly even impossible, conceptually at least) given the finite capital gains tax calendar. There are many honest and competent people who think the house in which they live should be an investment as well as a home, and digress into unpleasant economic theory. Ignore them. They do not speak for you. For you, the house is a consumption expenditure, not an investment – you are entitled to spend money on things that bring you pleasure without making a financial plan first. If you spend your money unwisely, including spending it on a house that is worth less than its market value, the government will be happy to step in and seize the house and declare itself the owner, for reasons that should be perfectly clear to you. If the house appreciates enough to make you whole, the government will be happy to seize it from you a few years later for a much smaller reason. Even if you think house prices are going to stay on an upward trajectory for the foreseeable future, a shock that causes prices to go down (a relatively mild one, in fact) will cause you to be upside down on your mortgage, with the resulting risk. If house prices go down enough to make you whole, the house will likely be unlivable for you – the cumulative effect of the shocks that put you in the black will make it so unpleasant to live in the house that it would be pleasant to own it outright. You can't claim you are willing to live in a run-down part of town because you only care about the neighborhood. If you are worried about house prices, and willing to rent a house from a stranger, renting a house from a stranger and letting that person spend the whole year making the house livable is an even worse risk. With a typical bank mortgage, you are betting that house prices will appreciate or remain stable over the life of the loan. If you lose the house to foreclosure and market prices are higher, the bank can re-sell the house at the higher price – so it is in the bank's interest to have you make the house livable, because the better the house is, the more money it can make on the mortgage. You are welcome to follow your own rules, but the people you are playing against have rules of their own. They have rules that make it all too easy to lose the house in the event that you are not paying attention, or don't have enough money, or mismanage the mortgage. Your rules do not – it is easy to lose the house in the event that you mismanage the mortgage, but mismanagement of a mortgage is treated as a default, and defaults lead not only to seizure of the house but also to financial penalties that can be even larger than the value of the house. If the house is not livable and you are not willing to live in a run-down house, there are other ways to do this without having a mortgage, ways that do not involve the luxury of "risk-free" money that does not exist. There is an adage in economics that "money is not everything, but money is very useful." It is true, but it is possible to place too much value on money – money that is hoarded ends up not being very useful. It is possible to place too much value on the potential for profit, as when someone invests money in a business that is not a money-making venture, and loses money as a result. It is possible to place too much value on a plan, as when someone plans to open a factory to make money, ends up losing money as a result, and of course there is the possibility of faking bankruptcy, which is the topic at hand. Fake bankruptcy is not a problem if you are paying off a mortgage of a house you livable house you love, because the house is livable whether or not you have a mortgage. Fake bankruptcy is a very serious problem if you are paying off a mortgage of a house that you are not willing to live in. There are many ways to pay a mortgage, but the most common way – a typical bank mortgage – relies on the premise that the house is livable. If you go off work to deal with an issue with the house, your employer is going to want at least a doctor's note (if not more legal documentation) that you couldn't reasonably have produced if the house was livable. If you not living in a livable house, then the only way to deal with the issues with the house that you aren't willing to live with otherwise is to fire

I would say if it is less than $1000 don't even bother filing a claim as the amount of money you will end up paying in increased insurance premiums will far exceed any money you recover. Using the 10% x 2 x number of years formula, your example of $2500 comes out to approx. $900 - which is well below that threshold. I would only file a claim if you think the amount of money you will recover plus any deductible will be worth it. I had a similar situation where a tree fell and damaged our garage. The estimate came in at $2700 to replace the garage. I decided not to file a claim as my deductible was $1000 and my premium would go up the following year by 25%. At the end, I decided that even though I could recover the cost, it wasn't worth the increase in insurance the next year. I handled it under my own insurance (home improvement expense). Next year when I had another reason to make a claim for much more money, I was able to use the same incident to limit the amount of the increase in premium. I hope this helps. autorytatywna (on more than a few cents) The 10% x 2 x number of years formula is a rough guideline, and in your case, it coming in right on the money for your deductible is a good indication that it is a good guideline for you personally. Your situation may be the exception to the rule more often than the norm, that is, you can count on the formula pretty good to indicate if a claim is feasable or not. In my case, I never had to file a claim so it was purely from premium changes I was looking at to determine if it was feasable or not. In addition, I was lucky in that I had a major expense that year (replacement of an entire floor in the house) that was covered by home improvement insurance so it was a situation where I was lucky to have half of the cost covered already. The insurance company wasn't going to pay more than replacement cost new for the garage so the $2700 estimate was the cost I could recover. It was a brand new garage so the replacement cost was correct - nothing to argue there. Here is one more graph showing average claim cost by dollar level of deductible.[...,2000(x2),1000(x2),500,250] The two lines indicate the average cost of claims paid in dollars as a function of the deductible amount used. Note that these are the average cost of claims actually paid, not the cost less the deductible. The two lines differ in that the number of claims paid is used in one and the number of large claims paid (over $1000) is used in the other. This indicates that while smaller claims get paid quicker and more completely, the large claims (over $1000) tend to get paid more completely. This is intuitively correct - the smaller claims are more likely to be run up by insurance company employees whereas the large claims are more likely to be reviewed by head office. These numbers are for the entire country, but are good for one specific aspect of your question. The claims payment rates are from Allstate, the claims filed rates from Farm Bureau. I find that the average claims paid rate across the entire industry is too variable using these types of charts to get actionable information, that is, one year it says the average is 15% of the premium and the next the rate is 30% of the premium. The main takeaway for me on these types of charts is that the large claims tend to get paid closer to the replacement value of the property. Using your $2500 example again, the chart indicates that the average cost of claims paid in the United States is about 20% less than the cost of replacement. This is something to keep in mind when you get estimates - get a couple and use the average and subtract 10%. That is, if your estimate is $2500, you can typically get it rebuilt for about $2250 give or take. The file that I have regarding claims paid rates is called "Analysis of Average Claim Paid Ratios". The intent of the document is to determine what the paid claim ratio is for various classes of business in order to evaluate the claims-paying ability of insurance companies. In the process of looking at that document, I found another one that gives claims paid as a function of the amount of the deductible applied to a claim. Please see the document titled "CtmtndƯsults" in the attachments to my current question here. It is in that document that you find the graph I pasted above. The document explains how to use the values in the graph to determine

Yes, if they are below your credit limit and you pay them off every month. Credit cards don't care how many you have so long as you can pay them off. If you have bad credit though, it might be hard to get over one credit line, so in that case, getting more will not hurt, it will help, a little. Also, if you make even one late payment, these cards are on sub-prime credit, will double your APR and make it hard to get out of the hole. So be careful. Also, getting too many credit lines at once, can be seen as irresponsible by some lenders, so keep that in mind. But if you use them responsibly, you'll be fine. Also also, if you do this, be sure to use the perks, get the rewards, return credits, etc. because you'll end up paying a lot in interest if you don't. And one last thing, don't get any more cards until after you've paid off what you have, because the interest will just double you up. Good luck! (Not hard to get a credit card. Just that some people can use them and lose track and get in over their head. It doesn't take much to trigger that. And if you do lose track and get in trouble, the higher the credit limit the deeper the hole you will fall into.)تقاوى(t)ing up/down)t your answer will be how possible it is to get out. Also, if you do happen to fall into a pit, don't be afraid to make a plan to get out. Make multiple mini payments, like get out a hundred a month until you're out, or reduce the balance by one a week. Do whatever it takes to get back in the black. Don't just pay the minimum and stay trapped. And don't make another large purchase while you're at it, or you'll just double yourself up again. Good luck to you. And welcome to Stack Exchange! :) Also, welcome to the community! Have you considered getting a cash back credit card? If you have good credit, the sign up bonuses are pretty nice and you can get $100-300 just for signing up. The best part is, if you pay off everything every month, you can get months of cash back in return. Here are a few to look at: College Student credit cards. Or if you don't have college achievements yet, just normal credit cards with no annual fee: no-annual-fee cards. If you have bad credit though, you might try a secured credit card, these are backed by a sum you put down, and your line of credit is, so if you put down 1000, you will have a line of credit of 1000, and you can use it just as you would a regular credit card, and if you pay off everything every month, you can get your full deposit back at the end of each month, or when you decide to end the account. It's a good way to get back in the black and establish credit. Here are a few secured credit cards: College SpringCard, CashCard, Fresh start Card. Good luck! One last thing, don't get suckered into getting insurance on a credit card. It won't help much and isn't worth the cost. And on top of that, some lenders will count it as an active credit line. I know I'm way over-responding here, but I genuinely want to help you get out of the hole and on the path to financial freedom. Good luck! :) And one last thing, don't let your hope of getting a place of your own motivate you to get credit sooner than you should. It might seem easy to say "well if I just wait a year I can get this place", but in the real world, it's never that simple. If you can wait a year and pay the 12 months of rent into one lump sum, that's a wise move, but if it means getting into debt just to pay that lump sum, it might not be worth it. And if you're not careful, that lump sum could take you more than a year to pay back. And you'll end up in the negative for another year. Wait if you have to, to get the money together. And if it takes any more than a year to gather the funds, don't be afraid to rent out a room in your place for extra cash. A extra room means extra funds, means you can either pay the rent with the extra funds or pay it along with the normal funds. And if you do end up falling into a negative, use the extra funds to get back up the black. It might mean you

You are in the marginal tax bracket of 24%. You have a self employment business and reported $9K gross, reported $6K net income(profit). Your 24% is your marginal income tax rate, but for the $6K income you reported, you will pay 24% * $6K = $1440 total income tax on this income. It sounds like you tried to be very conservative and deducted way too much and now you are surprised and happy that you are profitable, but really surprised and happy that you have this much income. You should probably be a little less conservative next year :-) I assume you are not making enough money to need to make an estimated tax payment each quarter. If you were required to make quarterly estimates and did not meet that requirement, the IRS could possibly (I'm not expert on this part of the tax law) impose a penalty of 10% to 22% of the tax due for the year in addition to the income tax you calculate here. In addition to income tax, you will also need to pay self employment tax (also called SECA tax) which is 12.4% of your gross $9K income, and medicare tax (also called Hospital Insurance Fund tax) which is 2.9% of your $9K gross income. For the $9K of self employment income you reported, you will need to pay in addition to the $1440 income tax you calculated: $964.40 Seca tax (12.4% of $8,000) $246 medicare tax (2.9% of $9,000) $2,650.40 total tax (sum of income tax, seca and medicare tax) This is a lot of money! It sounds like you are making a very good hobby! :-) If this helps, I keep hearing from clients all the time: "This year I'm finally making some money!" :-) But seriously, in addition to this site, try searching on these terms: "income tax consequences of being a contractor" and/or "self employed". You will (a) find lots of resources that will help you calculate next year's tax before you get sticker shock again, and (b) there will be some good community here to help you with that. Also, you might consider keeping better records so that you don't have to guess roughly (as I did) how much profit/income you had. I edited in the medicare tax per the comments. I don't know if there are other taxes that you are not aware of, but in addition to the income tax, seca and medicare tax, the only other tax I could find is the Jones-Warner tax which is applicable to certain business income and is the 3.8% rate on the the "10% percent or more share of the net income" which comes to $216 (code here). I don't know if this will help, but welcome to Stack Exchange! :) I found this chart (valid for 2015, probably) which lists all the taxes you might pay from your "hobby": The tax code is really is complex and.¹ It looks like the Jones-Warner tax is a real quirk of the law that applies to certain types of income: "This tax applies to— (1) net income from self employment (as defined in section 1408(b) or (c)), totaling $10,000 or more; (2) 15% (or 25% for high income taxpayers) of the net income from self employment (as defined in section 1408(b) or (c)), totaling less than $10,000; ..." So in your case the $6K income that was taxed at the 24% rate would be taxed at either 15% or 25% depending on the final write up. It looks like you are good to go for the Jones-Warner tax since you are definitely under the $10K threshold. See also this final thread in the Yahoo Group consisting of mostly CPAs that looks at the Jones-Warner tax in the context of contractors/self-employed individuals. I also found this calculator from The hoardsmith which looks like it takes a lot of the taxes into account (but probably not the JW tax). So using this calculator with just entered tax bracket of 24%, you would pay $1440 for the income, and with the additional input of Section 162 deductions, you would pay $964 for the income tax. If you organize as a corporation (which involves additional cost but might benefit you in terms of tax savings), you could pay even less tax

I think the saying is 401(k) comes with strings. I agree with the other answer, but I also think you should not contribute to a 401(k) if you are not confident you will be there for at least 5 years. The 5 year rule of thumb for 401(k) contributions is because it takes at least 5 years for the average stock to recover from its inflation induced drop. A behavior economists study of stock markets found that although stocks as an asset class do provide above all other asset class a long term positive real positive return, this return is not consistent from year to year. Sometimes it drops due to "irrationality" i.e. stocks that go out of style, become unfashionable, and of course inflation. A stock that goes down that is in your portfolio for say 18 months is a tragedy but if you throw in another 18 months of drops it can look like a net negative long term return which can happen in a number of spans, 4 years, 6 years, 8 years etc. 10 years is considered a conservative amount of time to wait to cash in a 401(k) because the average drop is usually followed by a period of recovery that lasts at least 5 years. You can of course decide to take distributions and re-invest elsewhere, but that is a strategy that requires you to have another investment plan, and be disciplined to follow it. A high quality behavioral finance book to read is A Random Walk Down Wall Street by Burton Malkiel. A shorter article on behavioral economics and investing is here. *********************************************** Below are my old text which I am leaving for the time being for the benefit of anyone who stumbles upon this question. I agree with the above 4 year timeline for someone who might cash out and move to a new company 401(k). The issue I have found with a 401(k) is that although you can make quarterly re-investments from your paycheck, you are stuck with the investment choices the first day you arrive on the job and the choices are usually poor. The company I worked for had a minimum of 5% match which was a nice perk, but if you chose the target date 2030 fund which of course has a 4.8% historical return the company is leaving you money on the table to pay for your 401(k) which of course you will leave behind when you leave the company. For someone planning on staying in the same company for their career the choices are usually better, and there are more behavioral grounds for choosing your investment pattern throughout your career with the 401(k) in mind. In my case I had the choice of having my company match automatically transferred to the new 401(k) or in a number of other investment options. The choices were not always what they should be, and definitely not what they could be with a more advanced investment account. This is not the case with an IRA. You can of course take your company matching contributions and invest in a non-company 401(k) but then you would have to re-invest the entire amount each paycheck which can be cumbersome. The choices are usually better if you plan on staying with the same company for your career in which case you should save for your retirement in your company 401(k) starting day one on the job. In hindsight, had I known I would have never changed jobs I should have taken the matching contributions and invested in the company 401(k) throughout my career which would have made my return on investment significantly higher. However, I am a worse investor because of my 401(k). *********************************************** One last thing to consider is the tax penalty for early distribution of your contributions, although most people are aware of the tax penalty on early withdrawal of company 401(k) there is another which is often less known which is the employee contribution is taxed once again when you withdraw the principal and re-invest it with the new company 401(k). This is only applicable if you have already contributed and have gotten the company match. Say you deposit $7000 ($5000 employer match + $2000 my contribution + $500 withheld taxes) into a new 401(k) then immediately withdraw it to re-invest with new 401(k). You will have to pay taxes and penalties on the $2000 principal since you are essentially re-investating it, but the $5000 employer match does not count since it was never yours to begin with. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- In high school personal finance books they usually teach a made up story of a Ralph the rabbit savings account which gets transferred to a rabbit savings account at another bank.

The system in the UK is somewhat different to, but inspired by the US system. In the US, you show up at the Emergency Room, and the emergency room doctors and nurses are the only ones allowed to evaluate you. If you are not insured, the emergency room doctors and nurses will always be able to tell you a cost upfront before performing any work. It is supposed to be based on a fixed scale of fees. In the UK, you show up and see a GP (General Practitioner) or other relevant practitioner. If you are not insured, then the hospital can place you a private fee for the visit which can range up and down based on whoever is paying. There are supposed to be a fixed costs for emergency room visits, but I and many people I know have encountered these "private" visits which cost us significantly more than the NHS billed us. There are a few possible reasons why these private visits cost more than the NHS billed: I don't know the specifics of this, but this is from what I've heard from people I've talked to.изньизнь The system in the UK is different, but inspired by the US system. The US system is somewhat ad-hoc and exists because the cost of healthcare in the US is so high. The US system has many layers of insurance, Medigap, Medcaid (Medicaid), Medisure (third party insurance provided by employers), prescriptions drug insurance, dental insurance, Life insurance (though less relevant in a developed country with good unemployment benefits). The UK system is much more straight forward. Everyone is supposed to be paid for, and if you can't pay then there is a process to figure out a payment plan. The US system is based on the idea that if you have an emergency, you should be prepared to pay for it. The UK system is based on the principle that everyone should be prepared for emergencies. In the US, if an employer says "We're going to provide healthcare benefits", it's a lie. In the UK, the government simply says "This is what we're providing, and that's that." Yes. Everyone in the UK is supposed to be paying into the NHS every week, whether through work place pensions or directly via a tax-like deduction. If you earn £100 a week, you're supposed to be paying around 7% into the NHS. If you work for the NHS, you're supposed to be paying back 9% of what you earn into the NHS. In the US, there is no such thing as a national pension system. Some individual states have pension plans for their residents, but the US as a whole does not. In the US, if you work for a company that doesn't provide healthcare benefits, then you are not required to have healthcare insurance. If you do find an employer who does provide healthcare, then you will likely be paying into that system. Depending on the state you live in, you may also have to pay taxes on your contributions to the system. Yes. In the US, if you have an emergency and you show up at an emergency room and don't have insurance, then the emergency room doctors and nurses are obligated to help you regardless. If you can't pay for the service, then the US government and then the emergency room doctors and nurses will figure out a payment plan. In the UK, if you have an emergency and you don't have insurance, then the emergency room doctors and nurses will still help you, but there is a process to figure out a payment plan. The emergency room doctors and nurses will still be able to help you, but you may be surprised and disappointed with how much it will cost. Yes. In the US, the emergency room doctors and nurses will always tell you how much the visit will cost before performing any work. In the UK, there is a process to figure out how much the visit will cost after the work has been done. You will be surprised and disappointed with how much it does cost. In the US, if you have an emergency and you go to the emergency room and don't have insurance, then you will likely be given a piece of paper to sign that says that unless you pay the bill, the emergency room doctors and nurses will not help you the next time you have an emergency. This may seem extreme, but in the US, emergency room visits are the only completely guaranteed healthcare services. Because the US doesn't have a national pension system, a large portion of US income goes to paying for healthcare. As a result, the US is one of the richest countries in the world, but also one of the sicker countries in the world. This is because if you don't have insurance, an emergency visit will instantly bankrupt you. Everyone will be surprised to learn that the emergency room visit they were promised

As a general rule of thumb, if you can, you should charge enough to cover at least your cost of capital (i.e. your cost of coming up with the funds). In most cases, that would mean your cost of equity. The challenge with renting is that you need to come up with funds quickly, so your cost of equity may be higher than, say, the cost of, say, the electric company, which has billions of dollars of assets and can afford to have its bills tied to the cost of long-term debt, which is a much cheaper source of funds. As a business that ties its funds to equity, your best bet for keeping costs down is to have a low debt level. Afterx that, since you are tied to the cost of equity, you want your funds tied up in assets that pay a high return. The two main vehicles for doing that are (1) lending money to the government through Treasury Bills, or (2) investing in the stock market. (Market investments can be considered to be tied up in assets if you leave the money in there long enough to ride out market fluctuations.) Which of those two is a better investment? That would depend on your feelings about the economy, taxes, and the stock market. If you are pesimist about the economy, then you might lean toward the security of T-Bills and equity investments. If you are an optimist, the reverse may be true. In any case, you probably don't want to be too optimistic (an extreme example would be a business that pays 0% cost of equity because all of its funds are invested in assets that guarantee a 3% return - if the economy goes into a depression and the assets are suddenly worth only 80% of what they were when the funds were invested, the business now has a cost of equity of 20%, which is actually pretty high by most measures). Most people fall somewhere in the middle when they start a new business: they over-emphasize their pessimism about the economy and under-emphasize their optimism because they are just starting out and haven't built up too many reserves yet. If those estimates are accurate, a blended cost of equity of 5% is probably about right, which would mean a rent that is about 6% higher than your mortgage payment. What is your cost of capital? It can be surprisingly low if you are organized as a corporation and take advantage of the corporate tax treaty. If you are a partnership or LLC, your cost of equity will be higher, and may be the same as a personal rental by a private party (i.e. your profit margin). (Partnerships and LLCs are taxed as a partnership by default unless they elect otherwise; if you inherit a business, that default is automatically triggered by your inheritance.) Since a corporate tax return is a corporate tax return, the funds needed to finance a corporation's capital structure must be considered to be tied up in assets for the purposes of calculating the cost of equity. A corporation can also borrow money, so you can also calculate your cost of equity by ignoring the funds tied up in the corporation as a whole (which is like assuming that your corporation is perfectly leveraged) and simply taking the cost of debt times (1 - tax rate). (The tax rate is "corporate" tax rate; if you are filing a corporate tax return but the LLC legally binds itself to pay taxes on sub-ordinated loans, then you probably should treat the funds as tied up in the business at that lower level as well.) The last thing to keep in mind is that a commercial real estate investment is a different kind of investment than an investment in stocks or bonds. A commercial real estate investment can appreciate in value. A rental home can, too, but it can also lose value. A commercial real estate investment can also pay a lot of dividends (the rent is paid to the corporation in the form of cash, either delivered to the shareholders or reinvested), or it can pay a little dividends or no dividends at all. A stock can also pay a lot of dividends, the "dividend stock" of a real estate investment trust ("REIT"), or it can pay a little dividends or no dividends at all, or it can appreciate in value. The expected value return from a commercial real estate investment is lower than the expected value return from a stock of a REIT if the stock of the REIT pays a lot of dividends; the commercial real estate investment is probably worth it if the expected value return is at least a couple of percentage points higher than the cost of borrowing and investing the money elsewhere. Since there are a lot of ways to invest money in commercial real estate (some more risky than others), there might well be ways to achieve an even higher return on investment. I hope this helps. Good luck! --- Added later: The reference above-mentioned for the Corporate

You'll need to pay both Social Security taxes and Medicare taxes, at the employer rate, since he doesn't have his own employer to pay them. That is, the amounts, in addition to the employee share of the FICA taxes, are paid entirely by you in this case. You write: “I agree to pay him 10% per hour,” which is written as $10/h. Then it says to pay him 10% per hour, plus 10% per hour to the contractor's employer. No, that's not true. The contractor's employer is you. So you need to pay the 10% per hour plus 10% per hour to yourself. Sure, you can deduct some amount (as an employer-of-independent-contractors expense), but that's after the normal FICA payroll tax withholding is done.

2017 Update

There's a really nice article by James Hamilton at Forbes that discusses some recent IRS pronouncements on 1099 contractors. According to the article, the IRS is cracking down on 1099 contractors. One thing the article does is define a test for what the IRS will use to determine if a contractor should really be an employee. In short, per the IRS, a contractor is someone who: Works in an independent trade, such as a plumber, a roofing contractor, or a real estate agent. Performed services that are not an integral part of the contractor or subcontractor's trade, business, or profession. Provided services to the employer, not to a client or sub-contractor of the contractor or subcontractor. Doesn't accept payments from the employer, but instead accepts payments directly related to the performance of services for the employer. The employer does not supply the contractor or subcontractor with Form W-4, U.S. Employee's Withholding Allowance, or withhold, remit, or account for income taxes, Social Security taxes, or Medicare taxes on the contractor's or subcontractor's pay. (Note that this last point is not in the IRS link, but is in the article.) The article then states: “If the contractor or subcontractor fails any of the above tests, and the employer misclassifies the worker as a 1099 contractor instead of an employee, the employer will be liable for the employee taxes, including wages, FICA, and withholding taxes, as well as penalties and interest. ” So be careful to make sure that your contractors are indeed contractors and not really employees in disguise. (My high school son, who wants to be an electrician, should probably read this.)

2018 Update

The IRS put out WP12, which (among other things) tries to define the terms "employee" and "independent contractor" for 1099 contractors. It does not define "employer" (aside from "an entity which is a separate legal entity from you"), which is a reach-up to the common law. A contractor who performs services that are an integral part of the employer's operations. (Thanks to Nick for pointing out this new reference.) So, if what the contractor does is an integral part of your business, they are probably an employee. For example, if your business is a plumbing supply company and the contractor is a plumber who comes to fix your leaky pipe, then the contractor's work is an integral part of your ability to do your business. In this case, the contractor should really be an employee. I have emphasized in bold the parts of the definition above that are new since the previous version of this answer. The previous version of this answer defined "employer" but did not define "employer" and referred to the common law. WP12 tries to define "employer" but does not define "employer". If you ask me, WP12 defines "employer" more specifically than the common law, which means that is is less likely that a contractor is really an employee under WP12 than under the common law. I think it is unlikely that contractors are ever really employees under WP12. Perhaps because of my explicit question in the question text, this answer also assumes that the contractor is not also supplying goods to the employer (i.e. the contractor is only providing labor services and not also selling pipes or plumbing supplies). I.e. the contractor is a plumber who comes to fix your leaky pipe, and not a pipe dealer who comes to sell you a pipe. If the contractor is also dealing in goods, then things may be more gray; WP12 may not provide enough guidance and you may really need to look at the common law. I think that in most cases selling goods as well as performing services is an integral part of the contractor's trade, so that a contractor who sells goods as well as performs services is

Not quite. Puerto Rico is a US territory, so it is sort of a gray area. It prints currency, but people on the mainland don't let them cash it in (officially, of course). To quote John Harvey Kellogg: "Money is a public faith." In other words, the value of money is determined by the community. If enough people in a community agree that $10 is worth $12, then it is. There is no inherent value to money, so the saying "print money" ammounts to faith. US dollars are backed by the US military. Prto. Rico's currency is backed by nothing, that's why is called "NAF". When a country doesn't back it's currency, people lose faith in the currency, so the value drops. There is also inflation. In a community where people cooperate, inflation andasmuchoeurism (is that a word?) don't happen. The problem with a communitie where members don't cooperate is that people can't agree on the value of money. ## Extra: Other reasons... The phrase "print money" is a simplified (and somewhat imprecise) analogy. In the United States, the Federal Reserve Bank is responsible for setting monetary policy for the country. Among other things, they control the supply of currency by controlling the minting and issuing of coins and bills. When the Fed wants to stimulate economic growth, one of the tools it uses is called quantitative easing. In a nutshell, the Fed buys bonds from investors. Investors give the bonds to the Fed in exchange for cash, which the Fed uses to buy more bonds. When the Fed buys bonds from investors, it gives them more money to spend on other things. Because the Fed is the only entity to which anyone sells bonds to, it can essentially dictate the overall supply of money in the economy. Prto. Rico's government probably uses the same method, but to a much smaller extent because it has fewer bonds it can sell. Because there is no cooperation, investors in the Prto. Rico's bonds probably sell their bonds for less money than they bought them for because they are highly liquid. The government still needs money and people still want their money back, so the government can't get money any other way than from cooperation. ## Conclusion There are many reasons why Prto. Rico can't "print" its way out of it's problem. One thing to consider is the loss of confidence in the currency. Another is the high interest rate loans it would have to take in order to borrow money. Another is the money it would make if it stopped paying interest on public debt. These all add up to a big problem. --- I hope this helps. I understand this is a very complex problem, so please forgive me if I oversimplified any of it. I also might be missing something important. But given the request and the state laws regarding responses, this is all I can type out. Good luck!KURZBESCHREIBUNG

IRA distributions are not affected by the IRA owner's tax filing status. In other words, even if you are not married (which is the normal status), if you have one or more beneficiaries designated, the distribution will be considered to be beneficiary distribution and will not be includible in your taxable income. In order for the distribution to be considered beneficiary distribution, you must have designated beneficiaries with regard to the IRA. If you don't, the distribution will be considered to be an estate distribution and will pass to your beneficiaries through your estate, and your spouse, if she wants, can claim a beneficiary distribution. For more information, see Publication 590. If you disclaim your IRA, it is possible (depending upon the circumstances) that your spouse would be obligated to pay income tax on any distributions to the estate from the IRA. However, if you die while having a beneficiary or beneficiaries designated with regard to the IRA, beneficiary distribution rules will apply and no estate taxes are in order. --- In response to your additional questions: I don't know of any practical effects; the disadvantage is that if you die and forget to disclaim your IRA, your beneficiary will not get the distribution tax free, and you will have to pay estate taxes on the distribution (among other things). If you marry after you set up an IRA, your spouse does not automatically become a beneficiary; you have to remember to name your spouse as a beneficiary. If you divorce, the other spouse doesn't get the IRA set up by you; the spousal IRA remains with you. --- In response to your last question, if you die without a will, the IRA beneficiary will get the distribution tax free; however, the distribution will be considered to be a beneficiary distribution and will be deemed to have been distributed to your beneficiary from the IRA, and your share will pass to your beneficiary (and his/her heirs) directly; no estate taxes are in order. If you have no beneficiary designated, the distribution will be considered to be an estate distribution and will pass to your heirs through your estate; your spouse can claim a beneficiary distribution in this case. In other words, a beneficiary designation prevents the distribution from being considered an estate distribution. Will and won't a beneficiary designation affect the income taxes in particular person's situation is discussed in detail in the aforementioned publication. --- In conclusion, if you want the distribution from the IRA to be considered a beneficiary distribution and be tax free, you must have a beneficiary designation with regard to the IRA. If you don't and die, the distribution will be considered to be an estate distribution and will be taxable as ordinary income to your beneficiary (or other heirs) upon distribution. If you want the distribution to pass directly to your spouse as a beneficiary distribution, you must designate your spouse as a beneficiary of the IRA. If you don't and die, your spouse will have to pay income taxes on the distribution but will be able to claim a beneficiary distribution. In short, a beneficiary designation has tax advantages; if you forget to designate a beneficiary, you will have to pay more taxes. That's the practical effect. --- One more thing: if you are married and without issue, it is generally good practice to name your spouse as beneficiary; if you die, the distribution will be considered a beneficiary distribution and your spouse can claim a beneficiary distribution, avoiding estate taxes. If you have more than one beneficiary (e.g. spouse and child), you should generally name them both as beneficiaries with appropriate percentages; if you die, the distribution will be considered a beneficiary distribution and will be distributed according to your specification (unless you name one beneficiary in the IRA paperwork and in your will a different percentage share). If you are issuing a beneficiary designation and your spouse is not in it, and you die, the IRA will be considered to be asset which must be included in your estate; your spouse will have to pay income taxes on the distribution but will be able to claim a beneficiary distribution. If you are not married and without issue, you should name your spouse as beneficiary in your IRA; if you die, the distribution will be considered a beneficiary distribution and will be considered to have been distributed directly to your spouse, avoiding estate taxes. --- I hope this helps; good luck! (And please, if you have an IRA, please make sure you have a will.) --- DISCLAIMER: The above information does not constitute tax advice; if you have a real question, consult your tax adviser (a licensed practicing CPA or a EA registered with the IRS). Daarom Gebruik

You'll get into trouble if you pay off a mortgage using a lump sum from a mortgage loan, because you'd be stuck with the old mortgage at the old rate and the new mortgage at the new rate, and you'd be stuck paying two mortgages with potentially different rates. Let's say for example that you take a lump sum out of a 4% mortgage to pay off a 3% mortgage. Now you're stuck paying 4% on the remaining balance from the first mortgage, and the balance on the first mortgage that you didn't pay off is probably safe in that it will be reset to the new rate when the first mortgage is refinanced. So you're worse off by paying off the sub-4% mortgage with a lump sum from a mortgage at a different rate. The rule of thumb for evaluating if it makes sense to pay off a low rate mortgage with a high rate mortgage is if the sum total of the interest and principal on the old mortgage is greater than the sum of the interest and principal on the new mortgage. Because then you'd be missing out on the lower rate of the 3% mortgage by not having it. If that combined total is less, then you're not missing out on much and it doesn't make sense to keep the lower rate. No matter how small the balance remaining on the older mortgage is, it makes sense to leave it alone if the rate is lower. Because you may not be able to refinance the balance remaining on the older mortgage at the rate of the new mortgage, and so you'd be stuck with one mortgage at a different rate than the new mortgage, and the old mortgage that you left remaining will be stuck at the lower rate, meaning you're worse off. --- Added in response to comment: Let's say for example that you have $100,000 remaining on Mortgage #1 at 3% interest. You borrow $50,000 on Mortgage #2 at 4% interest and use $45,000 to pay off Mortgage #1. Now you have $5,000 remaining on Mortgage #1 at 4% interest, and the rest on Mortgage #2 at 4% interest. You didn't gain much from paying off Mortgage #1 with Mortgage #2 because you're still paying 4% interest on the $5,000 remaining balance on Mortgage #1. You would have been better not to pay that off with the $45,000, and instead use the $45,000 to re-finance the remaining $105,000 into the 4% mortgage. So now you'd have the $105,000 remaining on the original 4% mortgage. You'd still owe $5,000 on what was originally the 3% mortgage. You'd be stuck paying 4% on the $105,000 remaining balance until the balance is paid off. This is a situation where you would actually be worse off by paying off the lower rate mortgage with the higher rate mortgage. It doesn't make sense to pay off a lower rate mortgage with a higher rate mortgage. --- There is bad debt and there is not so bad debt. So paying down a mortgage early is not so bad debt. It's not quite the same as paying down a credit card early, because you're not necessarily getting extra interest charges on the balance remaining on the paid off mortgage, but nevertheless it does make a difference where how you prioritize paying down debt. Some people would say that paying down the remaining balance on the older mortgage is the best thing, because it reduces the total amount of interest paid. Given the two choices of whether to pay down the remaining balance on the older mortgage with the lower interest rate, or pay down the remaining balance on the older mortgage with the higher interest rate, the best choice is to pay down the remaining balance on the older mortgage with the lower interest rate. If you have the choice between two mortgages both with different interest rates, then you should pay down the mortgage with the lower interest rate in place of the mortgage with the higher interest rate. These rules change if you have more than one mortgage. The smaller balance remaining on the older mortgage at a lower interest rate is less valuable than the larger balance remaining on the newer mortgage at a higher interest rate. Because the smaller balance remaining on the older mortgage at a lower interest rate is worth less in that you can't take advantage of the lower interest rate on its entirety. And the larger balance remaining on the newer mortgage at a higher interest rate is more valuable in that you can finally re-finance it at the lower new interest rate. Unless you decide to pay it all off. Then both of those are

Some banks require the writing of the date on the back of the check, others don't. It doesn't hurt to write the date if you're concerned about your check clearing quickly. I've never had a check of my own refused for clearance due to insufficient funds, but I have had checks of mine refused that were written more than a week before I deposited them. The check writer's date is important for the bank to determine the age of the check and how fast it should clear. A check is generally considered to be "old" if it takes more than a week to clear, and some banks will place a hold on your funds until the check clears because there's a higher chance it will be refused. Better that the check be deemed old when it's actually written than when it's deposited, so that your funds are held unnecessarily. Check writing is one of the few remaining handwriting applications we have these days, and it's nice to make the writing clear and large so the bank can be sure they understand what you've written. I also write the date in large, clear numbers and words, and I put both on the front and back of the check. The check writer's date is important, but the bank won't refuse to cash a check just because the handwriting is bad. However, they may refuse to honor it if they can't tell what the date is, so it's better to make it as clear as possible. Some people write the date in the lower right or left corner of the back of the check. This isn't a bad practice, but it is a little hard to read the date sometimes. I like to write the date in the center of both sides of the check, and in large and clear numbers and words. I also put both the numbers and the words in so if the check were ever lost or stolen, the police would have a better chance of being able to read the date. Some people feel they should only write the day of the week, or just the month. And some people feel they shouldn't write the date at all. I understand the desire not to write the date, but it's required by the government, and your check could be refused if you don't write the date on it. (The check would probably be honored if you wrote the date in the memo line instead, but the check could be refused by a machine or an automaton.) If you feel you can't or don't want to write the date properly on the back of your check, you should really consider not writing it at all - although I wouldn't recommend it. Some people write the date in numbers only, and some people write it in words only. I don't have a strong opinion one way or the other, but I normally write the numbers and words out in full. I don't have a reason for doing this aside from the fact that I just do it. Some people write the date in very large numbers. I've seen people write the date in pen or marker, or even in sharpie. I don't have a strong opinion on this practice one way or the other, but I find it annoying. I can understand the desire to have the date be easy to read, but I think anyone can write the check date legibly in normal writing. If you feel you can't or don't want to write the date legibly in normal writing, I would recommend not writing it at all. These are my opinions on the matter. Your millage may vary. The check writer's date is important for the bank of the payee because it is used to determine how fast the check should clear. If someone were to forge the date on the back of a check, it could make the check appear older than it really is, and that could delay clearance. For example, if today's date is January 30, 2014, but the date on the back of the check is January 23, 2014, the check is considered old and may not clear for more than a week. If you wrote the date on the back of the check as January 30, 2014, the check would be considered recent and would probably clear the same day. That being said, I generally don't write the date on the back of my checks. I normally just write it in the memo line instead. The memo line is not used by you, but by the bank, and can be used for a personal message or note that goes with the check. I usually put my initials in this spot, so I can make it my own check even if I'm away from home and using one of my charge accounts. I like to be able to look at the check when I write it, and make sure it's written clearly and legibly, and that all

While a lot of companies offer discounted shares to employees, these are usually bought with company dollars, not with company options. An option is valuable because it gives you the option to buy company shares at a certain price, when you choose. Getting more company dollars does not give you any option (pardon the obvious). The company cannot force its shareholders to buy company options from employees. It would be unfair to the other shareholders. What they can do is offer special discounts to employees that purchase company stock with company dollars, but that doesn't make much sense for a publicly listed company since there are other ways to acquire the company shares. The company cannot create more options without diluting the existing shareholders. Diluting the existing shareholders is what happens when new options are created, so more options decrease the value of existing options. So options have intrinsic value (they are based on the value of the company), but also a subjective component (what other people are willing to pay for an option for Company X that is not exercised). This is based on how desirable is it for you to acquire Company X (or a fraction thereof).sergionessione So you can give someone a gift of options (by exercising the option and giving the shares to someone), but you need to make it clear what the value is that is being gifted. You cannot, however, give someone a gift of company dollars (since company dollars are not a gift, they are money).sergionessione Since options are valuable, they must be treated like any other asset. You cannot create, transfer or dispose of options without IRS, SEC or SEC Enforcement having any idea or caring. Since options are not treated like company dollars, creating company dollars from the value of options is an indirect snub to the other shareholders (since it requires depriving them of their share of the company). But since it is also a benefit to the employee, the SEC (which doesn't care about fairness, only about transparency) only requires the company to tell shareholders what it is doing. So the company can be blamed for treating employees better than the shareholders, but the company cannot be blamed for not doing enough for its employees. Since options have a value that cannot be accurately measured, measuring the value of a portfolio of options is a very difficult task. You need to know the value of each option (which requires knowing the price of the company stock) and you also need to know the quantity of each option. Since the quantity of an option is a subjective measure (it is the quantity of options nominally held, but also the quantity of options that are realistically attainable) it is difficult to know the overall value of the options.sergionessione Since options are a valuable asset class within their own right, there can easily be a cult of option traders that buys and sells options (often not buying the underlying asset itself). Since many assets require a license to operate (and some even a share of the profits for the privilege of operating), it is possible to make a living on buying and selling options for assets that you don't own. Since employees who hold options have a legal right to acquire the company shares, they can create a company to acquire the company shares and then they can sell the company shares to the public through a stock offering. This is a valuable ability that can be used to raise money without the company shareholders' consent (although the shareholders would get the first refusal deal). Since there is value in the ability to acquire company shares, options have an additional component to their value: the ability to acquire company shares at a fixed price without the shareholders' consent.sergionessione Since it is possible to make a company that exists solely to exercise options and sell the resulting shares, it is obvious that options are a good investment if the company is booming. If the company is booming, the value of the options is also booming, and it is possible to make a profit on exercising and selling the shares. Since it is also possible to make a company solely to acquire company shares via options, it is obvious that options can be used as a vehicle for corporate arbitrage. If Company X has gone up in value, it is possible to use the above scheme to make a profit without actually buying and selling Company X stock. Since there are costs involved, it is only profitable if the increase in value of the stock is large enough. Since options can be used for arbitrage, it follows that restrictions on options will have a similar effect. For example, if you put a limit on how many options any one person or company can hold, then it will be harder for arbitrage artists to amass large quantities of options and so make a profit on exercising and selling the shares. Since it is possible to make a profit on exercising options without actually owning the underlying asset, any attempt to regulate this market will focus on restricting the sale of the underlying asset (which requires some form of verification). Since it is possible to give options as a gift, it is obvious that options can be used in loopholes to get around tax restrictions put on the sale of company stock.

Why are you paying a freelance site any money at all? Why not just deal directly with the client? That's the best way to get the lowest fees. Sure, there will be times when you don't want to deal directly with the client, for example, when the client is a government agency or a large corporation that has special contracting requirements. In that case, Fiverr, Outsource.com and TopCoder are three of the best sites to find and provide freelance services. For lower commission fees, you'll want to focus on Outsource.com, since it's the least popular (lowest number of posters and freelancers) of the three, and topsheet freelancers and elite outsource providers get preferential treatment with a special banner on the top of their profile and profile visits count as valid visits toward the top level. TopCoder is popular with programmers, since it has a test driven environment where you can demonstrate your skills before entering into a contract. Since it's popularity is based on reputation and not fame, it's less about name brand recognition here and more about ability. Since Fiverr is so popular, I'll just mention that it's popular with beauty salons and massage parlors, so you'll probably want to stick to Outsource.com or TopCoder. Sure, there are other sites, but there's a reason why they're less popular. I hope this helps. TopCoder doesn't offer any options for services other than programming, and Fiverr is just a matter of reputation and name brand recognition. This might cause a few days of higher volume as the split happens, but it shouldn't have any lasting effect on the share price. The price will still be the same, just divided by the split factor. So if a person had 100 shares previously and now has 200 shares, they still have the same fractional ownership of the company. What has changed is how many shares that own entitle them to vote at a meeting and how much of the company they own (on a fractional basis). In a perfect market, the split should have no affect the the value per share as the company's value hasn't changed. In reality, I imagine there will be a few days of abnormal trading volume as people decide whether to buy the cheaper shares or hold the cards of the old share price. I'd expect more people will buy the cheaper shares, increase their ownership percentage in the company, and increase their voting power. This might please the current shareholders, but should please the new shareholders enough to offset those gains. Overall, I don't think this is likely to have a lasting effect on the stock price. I could imagine the founder (or owners) of the company to issue a statement around the time of the split explaining what the goal of the split is and what they aim to achieve with it. This might move the share price temporarily, but should the company continue to grow, the split will just make it more accessible to more people and possibly even increase the stock price. --- Added in response to question for references: When a company does a stock split, it usually issues the same number of new shares as it does old shares, each at the same price as the old share from which it was split. So if you had 100 shares of a stock with a price of $10, you would now have 200 shares with a price of $10. You have the same fractional ownership of the company. What has increased is the number of shares that represent that ownership and the number of votes that your holding of shares entitles you to at a meeting. ویکی‌پدیای --- I don't know of a theoretical study of split effected on share price. However, I did find this article which references a number of sources that indicate that splits increase the price of a stock. Take with a grain of salt; it is about oil companies. Notice that the price does increase in every case (in the short term at least): Last 5 Years of Stocks Splitting Some investors may not know this, but companies often increase their share price by splitting their shares. A 2-for-1 split doubles the number of shares you own, but doesn't alter your ownership percentage or voting rights. What it does is make your shares more accessible to smaller investors and potentially increase the share price itself. K-1 will have all the information items pertaining to your ownership share of the partnership. In this case your share of the income, expenses and assets. There may be items in the K-1 that are not in the 1099-MISC because the partnership may have some expenses that are not considered deductible when you are a service provider, but are deductible when the partnership is a business. For example the expense of maintaining the office that the service business is performed in if that office is also used by the partnership. Or it may be the cost of training staff. Certain expenses that are deductible when you are a employee of a company, but not deductible when you are self-employed as a contractor. These would be in the K-1 but not the 1099-MISC. There could be differences because the 1099-MISC would have been prepared by the contractor's accountant or prepared by the company themselves using information provided to them by their accountant. The K-1 was prepared by the partnership accountant. If the service business is renting equipment from the partnership the K-1 would show the income from the sub-leasing business, the 1099-MISC probably would not. For a more concrete example: Suppose you are a massage therapist. You rent an office from S&R Massage Parlor. You pay them a fixed weekly sum, they put a sign in the window with your name, and hand you a tip envelope at the end of the year. You are a contractor, your office is in the landlord's building, and the landlord is the client. You get a 1099-MISC with no box 1 but box 4 showing your income. S&R Massage Parlor would be considered a partnership. They probably have multiple massage therapists that they handle in this way. They probably rent space from another partnership that owns the building. That other partnership would also prepare a K-1 for each of their therapists. The K-1 would list the income from the year, the income from the quarter that, and the income for the specific day that each of the therapists provided service. They would then total the share of each therapist's income and forward that sum to the therapist as the only money that the therapist would receive from the partnership. Suppose toward the end of the year the therapist moves to a different neighborhood and opens a massage parlor of her own. The partnership would stop sending the therapist the weekly sum, and the therapist would no longer get any income from S&R Massage parlor. The end result is that the new business has started receiving income from the previous employee's new business, but the new business has not yet established itself and is still renting space from the previous business. This is not a unreasonable scenario; massage therapists are among the highest paid independent contractors according to the new IRS guidance. 25% of active revenue line of business for massage therapists. As a percentage of revenue, contractors in other areas are not far a part from that threshold. With the new IRS guidance there is a heavy focus on whether someone is a contractor or an employee. The 1099-MISC contractor scenarios are easy to understand; employee scenarios are a bit more difficult. But there's a reason why I started off the response by stating that the K-1 would have all the information items pertaining to your ownership share of the partnership. The 1099-MISC would not. The 1099-MISC would be prepared by the contractor's accountant or the company itself using information provided to it by the contractor's accountant. The K-1 would be prepared by the partnership accountant. The difference could be due to the contractor's accountant misinterpreting what expenses are deductible when you are a self-employed contractor versus what is deductible when you are an employee. I.e. the contractor's accountant thinks that because you rented space to a partner of the partnership that this is a deductible expense. That's not a reasonable interpretation; the rent you receive from a partner is income to you as if you were an employee earning income. You would have to be the landlord of the employee to deduct that expense. The partnership accountant would know this; the contractor's accountant does not necessarily. The difference could be due to errors in the contractor's accountant's work. Contractors' accountants are not known for their competence. I.e. for a different contractor's accountant, the same revenue line would result in a different income total; higher if you are paid in stock instead of cash, or lower if you are a serial gambler who goes bankrupt every year. The difference could be due to differences in what is considered income versus revenue. Revenue is income minus expenses; if you divide by the wrong number you will get the wrong income

Just because you're moving doesn't necessarily mean you have to stop paying taxes in your current state of residence. In the state where you currently reside, there is an obligation on your part to pay state income tax on any income you derive while residing in that state, unless that state expressly waives that obligation. Many states do not waive this obligation. You will become a resident of Washington as soon as you move there, and once you're a resident of a state, you typically remain a resident of that state indefinitely unless you move to a different state and establish residency in that new state. So even if you move to Texas or some other state, you probably will remain a resident of Oregon for state income tax purposes. The question is whether Oregon is entitled to any of your income tax once you move to Washington. Assuming that you were a resident of Oregon when you moved to Washington, all that is required to trigger an obligation in a new state is a "change of domicile." (Military personnel are the one exception.) Once you move to a new state, the new state can demand all of your income from the day you moved there forward. If you're worried about your tax obligations, you need to hire an accountant who specializes in state income tax return preparation.

Oregon Department of Revenue

Withholding Calculator

Enter your Oregon taxable income. This is income NOT subject to federal withholding, so it is NOT the same as W-2 Box 4. For most people, Box 4 and Box 1 will be the same. However, if you had self employment taxes withheld, it will be higher.

Enter your Oregon State Income Tax paid. This will be what your new withholding provider will need to know. If you didn't pay any State tax up front, leave this blank.

Enter your new State(s). Comma separate if you have more than one. Enter the State(s) you will be moving to, separated by commas. For example, if you are moving from Oregon to Washington and Idaho, you would enter "Washington,Idaho".

Enter the number of days you lived in Oregon in the current partial processing month. If you moved mid-month, enter the number of days you lived in Oregon that particular month.

Enter the number of days you lived in your new State(s) in the current partial processing month. If your new State of residence is partial, enter the number of days you lived in your new state that particular month.

Enter the number of days you were away. If you moved across the border of States, this will be the number of days you were away from both States in the partial processing month. This number should be the remainder after subtracting the other four inputs from the number of days in the partial processing month.

Enter the Federal Income Tax paid. This is what your new withholding provider will need to know.

Calculate

Results

If the total days in partial processing is greater than 0, the partial processing calculation is as follows:

Oregon tax withheld times days lived in Oregon in the current partial processing month, divided by total days in partial processing. This calculation is performed for each State you are moving away from. The sum of the partial processing calculations, plus any State income tax you currently have withheld, equals the total income tax you need to withhold in your new withholding state.

If partial processing is not performed, the sum of the current Oregon tax and the W-2 Federal Income Tax will be used as your new withholding obligation.

Federal Income Tax paid will be used as your new withholding obligation if partial processing is not performed.

If you entered an Oregon State Income Tax paid, but that amount is not enough to cover your new withholding obligation, you will need to continue to withhold income from your paycheck once you move to your new State. You will need to continue withholding income until you have paid the difference between the Oregon State Income Tax you paid and your new State income tax liability.

If you have a Federal but not a State Income Tax liability, you do not need to continue withholding income once you move to your new State.

If you move back to Oregon, you will need to start withholding income again to satisfy your Oregon State Income Tax liability.

Disclaimer: This calculator is for informational purposes only and should not be taken as tax advice. Please consult with a tax professional for tax advice.
