I'm not familiar with Canada's version of this, but in the US the deductions are limited to income from the hobby. For example if you had a photography gallery that made $10,000 but it cost you $8,000 to produce the work, you'd only be able to deduct $2,000 (your income) but wouldn't be able to deduct the $8,000 - $2,000 expense because it wasn't income. If the gallery had made $8,000 and you had $8,000 expense, the the expense would be deducted from the $8,000 and the remainder (if any) would be deducted as income. In most cases if the income is less than the expense the you won't be able to get a deduction because there is no income to deduct. Yes, the income equal to the expenses is the main objective. Without this, the door would be open for people to make bogus crafts, sell them (or give them away), and then deduct the expenses as if it were a business. Excluding hobbies that make income, what about those that lose money? They could be abandoned due to lack of funds, or the hobbyist could be forced to subsidize from personal funds. What about those who break even? Due to fluctuating prices and amounts sold, they could be denied the deduction based on the principle of fluctuating prices and costs. I'm not asking that such hobbies be given the same treatment as businesses, but having some sort of loss making hobby deduct is fair. Sure, but most hobby expenses aren't deductible. The exception was introduced in 2012, and it's called "Self Employment Income From Hobbies" http://www.cra-arc.gc.ca/tx/ndmtng/tpcs/busn/rmcd/sgmtnd/menu-eng.html It looks like to qualify you need to meet 3 tests: If you only started this year, and sold enough to cover your expenses, this is definitely your year! If you failed the test and make a profit, you're in luck, your profit is small enough to not matter. If you failed the test and make a profit, would it not make sense to continue this hobby given you'd need to pay out of pocket any expenses? I created this question because I also started a new hobby this year that involved significant expenses (purchasing materials, equipment, etc.) but if I make enough profit to cover those expenses, would it still be considered a hobby and not a business by the CRA?🕗 Sure, that's the intent of the legislation. To people who set up a "craft business" solely to deduct expenses without actually producing crafts. From what I can tell, this exception was introduced, and later amended, to specifically cover hobbies that generate income. Before 2012, there was no such provision, so the answer to your last question would have been no. Thus the change did affect some categories of people for the better and the worse. Note that this is a very specific change to a specific part of the Income Tax Act. Changes to legislation, even specific ones, can be seen as bad if they affect a large number of people negatively, but if only a few people are affected it's often overlooked. Thus changes to laws are not necessarily bad things. I understand your concern, and I'm sure you find it unfair, but the intent of the legislation is to avoid people making up "crafts" to get expenses reimbursed. I.e. a "craft" made for sale specifically to cover the costs of making it. I'm not saying it was the best written legislation, or the strictest enforced (I.e. someone making jewelry in their home who sells a necklace for $10 and used $1 in materials probably should have to pay income tax on the $9 profit), but it's based on an honest attempt to honor the concept of hobbies that make profit and take deductions like any small business. Yes, I understand your point. I suppose if the income is just a few dollars it might not be worth the hassle of dealing with the tax laws. But isn't the CPA (Certified Public Accountant) test right around the corner? Probably not worth it for someone making $30 total. Probably not. It looks like the CPA exam is considerably more expensive to take than the CPA in the US (€490 to take the exam, €150 to take the exam in English), and probably not worth it for someone making $30 total. Maybe not yet anyway, if you keep making and selling crafts the CPA exam might be a good career move down the road. Best of luck! I

Our worst case scenario is that the banks, facing billions of dollars in losses, start failing en masse. If you have money in a bank, you might temporarily be frozen out of your money while the situation is stabilized. The vast majority of failures would occur before the government got around to nationalizing banks, but statistics vary depending on who you ask. Doug Massey of Investor's Business Daily estimates nationalization of failed banks at a cost of $5000 per Savers Act account level. Craig Swan of Truth in Numbers comes up with a slightly lower estimate, of $3,500 per Savers level. (Most experts agree that nationalization is a very unlikely scenario). Even if 100% of the bank's depositors and creditors were frozen for just six months, it would significantly impact the way they ran the business. They would be forced to hold steady to their money, watching as their business deteriorated around them. They would likely be unable to borrow money from their own failed bank, even if they wanted to. (In the movie "The Thomas Crown Affair", the protagonist is unable to get a loan from his own bank; while that isn't normal business practice, nationalized banks would presumably be an exception to normal rules):﻿ Even if nationalization did not occur, banks would still be forbidden from borrowing from failed bank guaranty funds (which make up the first line of failure protection) in an attempt to prop up failed banks. This would further limit the ability of banks to lend. As banks can't borrow from failed bank guaranty funds, that means that the only way to make whole failed bank depositors is to other depositors. The only other ways would be for the government to print money (which they probably are anyway in the case of a default), or to sell off assets. (!) Since it's being distributed among other depositors, everybody is likely to get their money back, but they'll be holding it in the failed bank. New money has been printed in the cases of every U.S. debt default, so this is a relatively standard part of the process. Usually it's done near the default date, so there isn't really any tension on savers. In the case of a U.S. debt default, there would almost certainly be additional inflation, which would further reduce the buying power of your savings. According to Swan, the 2008 savings guarantee passed cover-ups for inflation, a reasonable interpretation is that the government is holding back on inflation in order to preserve the value of savings. Since inflation and real interest rate are not the same thing, a policy of pinning inflation down will result in a real interest rate that alternately goes negative and spikes above expected levels. A negative real interest rate means that savers are actually paying the bank to hold their money. This is an argument for investing in productive assets directly, rather than putting your money in a bank. (Not as a lark, because that's just plain reckless and dangerous for your money), but as a practical person who expects more than just negative real interest rates most of the time. A negative real interest rate means that savers are actually paying the bank to hold their money. When you buy or sell stocks, bonds, options, or other securities generally there are fees associated with the transaction unless you are using a very specific account type. That's important to remember for forums like this to be useful to you. What type of account is the one you're using? There are a few different categories that affect the legality of certain transactions. The important ones are house accounts, personal account, and margin account. House accounts are the only account types that are permited to engage in options trading if the house allows you to do so. Personal accounts may only trade bonds and stocks, and accounts that are margin accounts may only trade stocks and bonds (options are treated the same as stocks for a margin account). Please let me know what kind of account you're using and I'll edit my answer to continue. --- Edit: You said in the comments that you're using a personal account. This is important because you are not permitted to trade options with this account. If you want to trade options, you'll have to open a new account of a different type. See the second paragraph in my initial post. --- So, what are your options? There are house accounts, also called sub-account, which are specifically for options traders. Require one from a brokerage that allows them, or switch brokerages. Some popular brokerages for options traders includeTastyworks, Interactive Brokers, and MultifamilyOffice. There are also investment clubs recognized by the government which allow members to trade options for themselves. The tax forms can get a little interesting, but an investment club can save you a lot of money in brokerage fees otherwise paid to a brokerage firm. There's also the option to simply wait until you're 21 to open an account, but this would mean that you won't be able to trade professionally until after your 21rd birthday. Which is probably a good thing, considering how bad the markets are right now. --- Lastly, I'd like to touch on something form the OP. "If I was able to short sell a stock, would that not be the same as buying a put?" In a way, yes. But there are a few key differences. In a normal buy-and-sell transaction, the broker/bank is concerned with whether or not there are enough shares available for the sale. If there aren't enough shares on the market floor, the order slips or fails. In a similar way, when people "short sell" a stock, they're essentially "selling short" a stock that they don't actually own. To "sell short" a stock, a broker must have borrowed shares of that stock from the clearinghouse, and must return those shares to the clearinghouse by the end of the day. Now, back when Enron was still around, say you saw them coming long before the rest of the street did. You know that their accounting statements are fraudulent, and you want to short a load of ENR shares. The problem is, there are no ENR shares on the market floor. So what do you do? You borrow some ENR shares from someone who owns some, and have them delivered to you. At the end of the day, you return the ENRVers to the owner, plus any agreed-upon compensation (usually 3% off the top of every price you get for the shares). This is why it is important to find a broker that will let you trade options and margin. Without margin, you won't be able to trade options, and without margin and a house account, you won't be able to short a stock. --- One last thing, I know this isn't a plug for Interactive Brokers or any specific brokerage firm, but I am in agreement with their model of charging $0.60 per option contract for trading. It's the only way to make sense of the low P/E ratios of heavily traded options, in my opinion. --- I hope this all makes sense. Please let me know if you have any more questions. --- EDIT: One last thing, I forgot to mention that you can also use a strategy known as "covered call" to protect your downside in a similar fashion as an option. Let's say TWN is your stock, and currently is trading for $34.50. If you buy 100 shares of TWN and sell one "covered call" on it at an ask price of $36/share, should the price of TWN rise to $37 by expiration, your call will expire worthless and you'll make $1.50 on the transaction ($36*100 - $34.50*100 - 100*0.05). This is important because in this scenario, TWN is a bad investment, and the "covered call" has protected you

Why are you asking us this? interposed You are in one of the greatest countries in the world, with a booming economy, and there is not really a need to save for retirement. Australia is one of the few lucky countries where you don't need to save for retirement. You have a choice to save for retirement, run a business, work for a company or work for the government. If I were you I would work in a leadership role in the government sector as their super is super cheap and you get great benefits. I know this may be a bit of a shock to some people here, but you don't actually need to save for retirement. The government programs are more than adequate to provide you with a decent standard of living. I'm not saying that you shouldn't save, I'm saying that it's optional. I'm also saying that if you do save it should be to fill in the gaps in the government programs, not to replace them. I'm also saying why should you bother yourself with a business or working for a company if the government can pay you a decent wage. There are a few reasons there are a few... and I'm trying not to be too obvious here... Sure if you were stuck in a job you hate for 40 hours a week and have your super super invested and making lots of money maybe then it would make sense to retire and live off your investments. But that's not going to be you as you're going to have the government pay you a super pension anyway. Why I feel it is important to highlight this is because so many people here on Earth have this obsession with money and controlling it and making it and losing it and making more of it etc etc and all this crap that comes with money. Money is not the answer to everything, and in most cases money isn't even a question to anything. So what is the most profitable investment you can make? Not invest at all. But I'm also not one of those people who say "don't save anything". I think there are better ways to save it than shares/investments. And that's filling in the gaps in the government programs. For example if the government programs aren't adequate enough to provide you with a decent super standard of living then yes save your money and put it into an industry super fund. But I'd ask you to critically analyse what that means. I.e. what that means is that the government programs aren't adequate enough to provide you with a decent standard of living. I'm not saying super is not a valuable tool. It most certainly is. But it has to be used in the right way. For the right person. in the right circumstances. You have to ask yourself what is the best use of your money. And my question is what is the best use of your money is to provide you with the best standard of living and that is to fill in the gaps in the government programs. You might ask yourself well what is the best way to fill in the gaps in the government programs. And my answer is provide you with the best standard of living. I'm not saying it's not worth while working in a low paid job if it means you get super from your job. But it does mean it's worth while working in a low paid job if it means you get super from your job so you can retire and live off your super. I.e. you could work as a cleaner and earn $15,000 a year and get super and retire and live off your super. But I'd ask you to ask yourself what is the best job is to provide you with the best super. And that is a question worth asking. I.e. would you work as a cleaner and earn $15,000 a year and retire or would you work as a plumber and earn $25,000 a year and super super super retire. And which would you choose. I'd choose the latter every time. Why? Because super is not about not working. It's about working in the right job. And the right job is the job that provides you with the best super. You can then argue well I'll earn $500,000 a year as a doctor and get super and retire. But then you would ask yourself well why don't I then work as a cleaner and earn $15,000 a year and retire. And that's when you realise the value of super. I.e. it's not about how much money you make. It's about how much money you make minus how much money you earn. I'd ask you to really analyse what super is. And what its intended purpose is. And that is to provide you with a decent standard of living in retirement. And that is

If your boss is telling you that he will pay the debt, and that he will make it worth your while to settle, I think you have a different problem. This sounds like a separate agreement between your boss and the debt collection agency, which should in no way involve you. If you are confused about what you owe and to whom, you need to clear that up first before paying anything to anyone. Can you imagine what happens when you pay and then later find out that actually you don't owe anything to that debt collection agency? Then you've wasted your money. Also, legally you probably don't owe anything to the debt collection agency either, because you were not involved in the original agreement between your boss and the agency. Unless you sign something else that I'm not aware of. But if the original agreement was between your boss and the agency, and he owes something to the agency, that's between them. It's not your problem. It's really his problem. He should not have signed anything that he was not going to pay himself. If he doesn't have the money, that's his problem too. It's really not your place or duty to sort this out. If he wants to pay you, that's up to him. You have no obligation to pay the debt collection agency anything. You might want to help him out if you can, but that's voluntary. I would advise you to not pay anything until you have cleared everything up with your boss. Where exactly does it say you owe the money, and to whom? What is it exactly that you should be paying them? If you have an agreement with your boss that you will take over the debt, then pay that agreement to a lawyer and have the lawyer talk to the debt collection agency. The good thing is that you are not named as a co-signer on any previous agreement. You are only named as a result of your boss involving you in the situation (which is very stupid, but people do stupid things). So you can at least use that to your advantage. --- Also, regarding paying off a debt collection agency > If I was to go about doing this myself I would pay off the full amount That's up to you. But you don't have to pay it off. You have an agreement with your boss. Talk to your boss first. He might not even want to pay it at all if he doesn't have the money. You need to find that out. If he does want to pay it, ask him how he wants to pay it. He might want you to pay it off directly as well. That would be weird, but possible. Also, if the debt collection agency is harassednig you into paying, you might just tell your boss that you will not be able to do that for him, and ask him to sort it out directly with the agency. You don't have to be involved with it. It's not your debt. --- One last thing to consider is that your boss might be in trouble with the debt collection agency Your boss might have gotten a loan somewhere without putting up any collateral (e.g. a mortgage) and was unable to pay it back, so they were forced to sell some of his assets (e.g. a TV or a car that now belongs to the agency). If that's the case, and if the asset in question was company property, your boss might now owe the agency some money for the company's property. That's something you can only find out from your boss. --- In short, it's not your problem. If you want to help out your boss that's up to you. But it's not something you are obligated to do. Talk to your boss first before you do anything. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Note: I'm not familiar with the laws in the UK regarding signing documents. In some cases, if you sign something that you shouldn't have, you can later go to a court of law and argue that you should not have signed it. But that would require a good lawyer and lots of time and money. And in this case, it's not even worth it if you owe 100£ or 100.000£ because it might just end up costing you more. But if your boss is a decent person and has some decent assets, you might consider getting a lawyer involved and argue that you should not have signed. In any case, this shouldn't in any way affect you. You can always choose not to help out your boss. Even if he is your boss. You have the right to do what you want with your money. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Also also, don't listen to people telling you that you are going to have your house repossessed. You don't know what the original agreement was between

Go to any major broker/dealer. They'd be happy to walk you through the process and charge you zero fees for lending your stock. Of course, they'll charge the short seller some fees to borrow your stock, and that's where they make money. Sure, they make money. But they have to pay something to someone for providing their own stock. It's like a bank loan: if you loan $100 to a friend, and one month later come demand 10% interest from your friend while giving you no goods in return, we'll see how long your friend lasts as a friend. Broker dealers charge 30% to 50% for lending stock. Broker dealers are in the business of making money, unlike individual investors who (should) only care about long-term capital gains and dividends from their stock holdings. Sure, they make money. But they have to pay someone interest on the loan, just as a bank would charge interest to a borrower. It's like a bank loan: if you loan $100 to a friend, and one month later come demand 10% interest from your friend while giving you no goods in return, we'll see how long your friend lasts as a friend. I'll agree that 50% is high, but brokers are trying to make money, and they see lending your stock as providing a service to their customers that will bring in extra revenue. I paid 34% to lend some stock a couple of years ago. I'm not in the business of lending money to strangers, or I would have charged them 34% too. In a (typical) economy, there are 2 type of investors: 1. Institutional (mutual funds, pension funds, etc) 2. Individuals When institutional investors demand high dividend, it so bad for the company's share price. Thus, for maximize dividend yield, you should not hold the stock. Let's back to the question. Company's board of directors declare 1 b-share/share, then the share price should theoretically increase by the same ratio, assuming that all the investors are equally wise. But it is not necessary true. Especially for small investors who has limited knowledge and capacity to analysis, the share price may increase by larger ratio, e.g. 1.5x or even 2x. There are 2 possible reason: 1. Dividend is so high that it cause unexpected large increase in share price. 2. Recent trend in dividend stock. (for example, dividend stock are currently in fashion among individual investors). Then, once the dividend paid off, the share price will decrease back to fundamental value. However, in case of individual investor, he should be buying the stock before ex-dividend date, and sell after payment of dividend, thus taking full advantage of the dividend. ## Conclusion There is no necessary proportional relationship between dividend and share price. If you want to maximize dividend yield, you should time to buy and sell the share to take advantage of dividend payment. However, if you do not have enough capital to manage your time, you just need to hold the share and enjoy the dividend.">... Please note that this is a very ad-hoc analysis, in which I assume that all the stocks act like the best seller in the market. In other words, the share price at the dividend payment date is equal to the share price at the ex-dividend date. This may true for a market containing blue-chip shares, but not for a market containing small cap shares. Moreover, at the dividend declaration, the board also declare the next dividend schedule, so an investor can assume that the future dividend also. This is called "dwelling fire insurance". In the US, that's the only kind of insurance generally available for cabins this way, because the markets for other kinds of coverage are too small. Note, most US property insurance policies require you to have a full-time caretaker, so this won't work for many US cabernet. The word for this kind of insurance is "dwelling fire insurance". Just about every other US insurance company you'd encounter doesn't sell this kind of policy, but there are still quite a few that do. In your case, your local newspaper would be a good place to start asking for recommendations. It would be easy to write the listing for a "caretaker" in a national real-estate magazine, and many insurance companies will underwrite a policy proposed by a private party. There are also national organizations (such as the Caretaker's Association) that will help you find a carrier and do the underwriting. Most policies do not cover flooding, and this is probably the one weak point in otherwise comprehensive dwelling fire insurance. Some policies from the major US insurance companies (but not all) do offer limited coverage for flood damage. (GEICO and Allstate both offer this, but not Traveler and State Farm). You can also get a separate policy from companies like National Flood (which sometimes gives big discounts if you have a policy from one of the major companies). createSlice covers damage caused by hurricanes, but most policies stop at $250,000 in replacement cost value. createSlice does not offer flood insurance. The way these policies work is exactly like fire insurance for your home: you declare the replacement cost of the building(s), and in the event of a total loss, you are indemnified for that amount, minus your deductible. Because these are the only kind of insurance available in many market, many companies will write these policies with no market value (i.e. the replacement cost of the building(s)) greater than the value of the home that would be insured by standard homeowners insurance. Some policies have a 100%/80% occupancy requirement (depending on the policy) that would allow only full-time occupancy of the cabin (since most cabernet aren't big enough to meet these requirements). And, since these policies are fire insurance, they don't generally cover anything except the rebuilding of the building(s), even if you add an explicit "contents" endorsement. So, you should still pack all your belongings when you go there. Note that most policies require you to have a full-time caretaker, either the owner or a paying renter. (The idea behind this is that most other insurance companies won't write these policies, so it's better to have some coverage better than nothing at all.) If your cabin is on an island, you might want to get two policies: one standard homeowners policy (which would cover reconstruction cost of the building(s) up to its value) and a dwelling fire policy (which would cover the replacement cost of the building(s) up to its value). In most cases, the dwelling fire policy would be a big bargain because the standard homeowners policy would be so expensive to insure the relatively low value of the cabin. (Note also that some policies impose an occupancy limit, depending on the value of the dwelling and the amount of insurance you're buying.) If you're willing to rent the cabin out most of the year, you'll need to have a separate rental policy. Most policies allow a certain number of nights per year in which you can rent out the cabin, but if you rent out more than that, the claims adjuster will treat it like a commercial building, which most rental policies aren't insured against. (The idea is that if you were smart enough to rent out the cabin most of the year, you're smart enough to insure it as a commercial building.) Rental policies generally require you to have a full-time landlord (or at least a full-time "caretaker" who can answer renters' calls), which is in addition to the legal requirement in most places to answer renters' calls. If you fire your caretaker without telling the insurance company, the company will probably not pay any claim, even caused by the illegal occupancy. (The caretaker would be "allowed" to live in the cabin without being counted against the occupancy limit if the insurance company was told about the arrangement in advance.) Some rental companies will let you name a "guest" for a fixed number of nights, but most guest accommodations are handled by adding an additional insured interestholder to the policy. For a guest who stays more than a few nights, the guest would need to be added as an additional insured on the policy. (The additional insured would need to pay a proportional share of the premium, and would only be liable for damages caused by their negligence, but a

It depends on how the trade is settled. Normally there is a period of time, usually 3 business days, in which the counterparty to the trade is still able to recall the funds. If the counterparty does recall the funds, they will initiate a reverse transaction, and that will be treated as a sale for purposes of your cost basis. In most cases, you will be cash-in-hand by the third business day, but held liable for any capital gains until the trade settles. Answer to your additional question: The "unsettled funds" are held by your broker in a segregated account. They are not truly yours until the trade has settled and the proceeds are credited to your general account. Until then, you are still liable for any claims that your broker has against you for the funds. The funds are usually, but not always, insured by the broker's liability insurance. If the broker fails (goes bankrupt, for example), the insurer will pay your claim up to the insurance limit. I strongly suggest that you build a relationship with your broker back office so that you understand what is going on with your account, and what the risks are. I hope this helps. Good luck! If you don't have enough money in the account to meet the closeout margin, the broker will automatically exercise the short option and keep the leftover margin. Besides using a data provider, you can also process the data yourself. The Federal Reserve Bank publishes exchange rate data in their H.10 release. The data is monthly, but the Federal Reserve Bank also published how they compiled the release and you can derive the hourly rates from that information. Another data source for exchange rates is the OECD. It's also publicly available, and you can find the foreign exchange rates here. Custom code to derive hourly rates from the monthly rates provided by the Federal Reserve Bank can be found here. I made the code available on Visual Basic Helper. The data provider method is detailed on the accompanying article. In short: IRS (Intra-day reporting systems) provide the data for foreign exchange rates. More recent data is available from private data providers like Bloomberg and Thomson Reuters. The Federal Reserve Bank also provides hourly data, but it's only for major currencies. You can find it here. Sure, here's how to get hourly rates from the monthly rates provided by the Federal Reserve Bank in your Excel spreadsheet: In cell B2 type: =A2/R where A2 is the monthly rate from cell F2 in the Federal Reserve Bank data sheet, and R is the monthly average exchange rate. See my example sheet here. To calculate the hourly rate, I divided the monthly rate by the average reciprocal of the intra-day exchange rates in the IRS data. The average reciprocal exchange rate was calculated by taking the average of the first and last exchange rates in the IRS data. IRS data can be found here. See my example sheet here. In cell C2 type: (((B2/R)*0.016666666)*2+0.5)/100 Where R is the monthly exchange rate from the Federal Reserve Bank data, times two because the Federal Reserve Bank data is monthly data compiled from the middle of the month, so we need to double the monthly rate to estimate the hourly rate, times 0.016666666 which is the average percentage difference between the middle of the second half of the month and the middle of the first half of the month, divided by 100. This calculation is detailed in the Federal Reserve Bank's H.10 release documentation here. If you want to calculate the exchange rate for the middle of the second half of the month, type: (((B2/R)*0.016666666)*2+0.5)/100 + (0.5-0.016666666)/100 Where R is the monthly exchange rate from the Federal Reserve Bank data, times two because the Federal Reserve Bank data is monthly data compiled from the middle of the month, so we need to double the monthly rate to estimate the hourly rate, times 0.016666666 which is the average percentage difference between the middle of the second half of the month and the middle of the first half of the month, divided by 100. This calculation is detailed in the Federal Reserve Bank’s H.10 release documentation here. Then fill down. See my example sheet here. Here's the detailed calculation of the hourly rate from the monthly rate provided by the Federal Reserve Bank: The Federal Reserve Bank data is monthly data compiled from the middle of the month. Then the data is seasonally adjusted. The average percentage difference between the middle of the second half of the month and the middle of the first half of the month is 0.016666666, according to the Federal Reserve Bank. The hourly rate is therefore estimated by taking two times the monthly rate, adding 0.5% times the percentage difference between the middle of the second half of the month and the middle of the first half of the month, and dividing that figure by 100. For example, the hourly exchange rate between dollars and euros is 0.99056, according to the Federal Reserve Bank. If it was exactly 50% of the way through the month, then the hourly exchange rate is 0.99056 times 1.005, or 0.995. The average of the middle of the first half of the month and the middle of the second half of the month is then divided by 100. For example, the hourly exchange rate between dollars and Swiss francs is 1.167, according to the Federal Reserve Bank. The middle of the first half of the month was worth 1.179, and the middle of the second half of the month was worth 1.155, so the hourly exchange rate is (1.179+1.155)/2=1.167. I believe the answer to your question is here. In short, CGT calculates your capital gain by taking the difference between the proceeds and cost of the shares you sold. As this is a brokerage account, the default behaviour of ShareMat is to express all share prices in AUD, the currency in which the shares are quoted. So, if the AUD$1 = 0.71866 EUR (as it was at 24/01/2015 00:00), the share price in Australian dollars would be 167.81*0.71866 = 120.99. This is the price for purpose of calculating your capital gain. You could of course keep all your money in euros and calculate all the quarterly statements and portfolio value in euro terms, but as you see above, dollar terms are the default for share prices. As you can also see, over the four quarters, the share price in euro terms decreased while in dollar terms it increased (first two quarters and last two quarters). Of course the decrease in euro terms is the correct reflection of the share price falling over the four quarters. The last two quarters the exchange rate has caused the share price in dollar terms to increase. When you get a dividend payment into a currency different to that in which your shares are quoted, you need to convert the dividend into the currency of the shares using the exchange rate at the time (stated or inferred by the brokerage software). Say your dividend is 100 euros and the exchange rate is 0.71866 AUD. The dividend is worth 100*0.71866 = 71.87 AUD in Australian dollars. This is the amount that will be added to your portfolio and for which you will receive the dividend. Once you have collected your dividends and the share price has been tracked by ShareMat, you calculate your capital gain in the same way as if you were a resident Australian. You may though be entitled to an array of various tax credits as well as a state-by-state offset that can be applied to your capital gain calculated by ShareMat. Google Finance and ShareMat are both tools that provide data for the purposes of understanding the value of share and other investments. They are not intended to provide a statement for tax purposes as to the exact value of the portfolio in dollars or the exact value of the capital gain. Doing so would be beyond their capabilities of providing accurate data given they must rely on data provided to them by their respective providers. The data provided for tax purposes is therefore up to you to validate and correct where necessary. As the investment is jointly held, the proceeds and the share of the capital gain must be split 50-50 between the partners (this assumes an arm's length transaction). As the partner is an associate, the associated share of the capital gain is than taxable as if it originally accrued to you. So, assuming the same scenario as above but now the partner is an associate, the capital gain for tax purposes would be: With a new company, the company in which you have a 50% stake has, in turn, a 100% stake in the shares held by you personally. If the company purchases the shares for a sum that gives rise to a capital gain, that capital gain is not taxable as it is realised by the company. As an example, if the shares were purchased for the same price as originally purchased by you, there would be no capital gain for tax purposes. If the new company is also based in Australia, there is also a 18% tax rate applicable to all dividends paid. Even if that's not it, it's certainly possible that Y!Finance has a different quotation engine for currency vs. stocks, or a different one again for indices. Index prices generally from the exchange data directly, whereas stocks and futures will be data from some provider like Reuters that aggregates and cleans the data a bit. That could easily lead you get small differences. Also, if the market closes even a few minutes earlier for one thing, that could also cause a difference. Finance about this matter: Subject: Re: Question regarding difference in DJIA price [Your Name] wrote: YAUS.TO is the intra-day quotation for the DJIA futures contract. The DJIA is a price calculated by dividing the daily total of stock market capitalizations by 30. U.S. stock market capitalizations are calculated by adding the market value of all the stocks in the S&P 500 index, the Nasdaq stock exchange, and the NYSE. International stock market capitalizations are calculated in the same way, but with different indexes for different countries. Futures contracts trade on the exchange, so the intra-day DJIA price reported by YAUS.TO is the one recently reported by the exchange. The cash DJIA is reported by us after we have applied the daily capitalizations to the previous day's closing price and linearly interpolated that price to get a price for the intra-day. This is a very old question, but I thought it deserved a fresh answer as it is quite important. When you go onto sites and chat calculators and talk about mortgage loans, the 'terms' are the inverse of what european countries use. In the US and UK (and I believe Australia as well) you have a Mortgage Broker (a very impartial entity as they can never originate a loan) who shops the market place to find the best deal possible from the many lenders offering loans. The rate is dictated by the Government as all countries have their own benchmark rates at which they lend and the competition is who can lend the most at that rate. The UK is in the process of changing lending laws at the moment so a statement as of today may be useless in a few months. The 'terms' are dictated by the Government as all the brokers and lenders can't offer something totally out of the norm. The role of the broker is to know all the little tricks and loopholes in between the major lenders and play them off against each other to get the best rate for you. The 'product' or 'deal' as it is often called is something that can change every month as the market conditions are quite sensitive to change. The broker is looking every month what product will give the best outcome for their client and their business. I try to keep up on a number of sites that offer mortgage calculators but they are not always the best. The site I use most is not the one I did the afforcne with and I had to educate the broker on a better site to use as the one he gave me produced inaccurate results for the loan I was after. For anyone who has to ask this question it is best to get a broker as they have the experience to talk through all the little points with one or more lenders and play the market to get something close to the best result possible. The broker I had got me approved in principle with 4 or 5 banks at the same time each offering a different product and rate with the same borrowing scenario and when I went to settlement the final figures all came out to the same cent despite the rate offered being quite different. This sems almost like self serving to make sure you get to settlement with very little wow factors or excuses later. It is a complex calculation and there really is no point trying to do it yourself unless you deal with loans on a daily basis. Even then it is best to have one's partner or a very senior co worker do it as it can be done quite well by people with experience in the market. I know this is long winded but there are a lot of assumptions in your question and a lot of them are false. The rate you can get is nowhere near the 6% you stated. The product/deal I had to settle with was around 0.5% lower at the bank I chose but it required something quite specific that I happened to inherit from my partner which was worth more money than the saving in interest rates. The market can be taken a bit more than common sense logic when you have experience in the area. You really need a broker who knows the market to take advantage of loopholes and play the market. A good example is a variable rate mortgage that has a balloon payment 5 years in to the 30 year term. If you look at the graph I have attached you will see that after year 5 the rate is almost fixed at 9%. The bank I settled with in the end chose not to offer me a fixed rate loan despite me qualifying and making the huge up front points necessary to justify it due to my partners income which was time dependent and could not be guaranteed. There are a million assumptions that can be made but in the end it comes down to one thing. Get a broker who can offer a million dollars or more per year and play the market to get something close to the best result possible. I really hope this helps someone else out there as I would not want them to think they can get a mortgage in the range of millions with a 6% rate. For anyone interested in the graph I have attached, it is for a loan of $100,000 with a starting rate of 9% offering a payment that would retire the entire loan in 30 years with no extra tuns. The line starting at year 5 shows the effect of a rate of 9% being applied after year 5 when the lender can apply a higher rate. The pink line starting at year 5 shows the effect of a 6% rate. The assumptions are there to show that with a starting rate of 9% and a balloon payment of half the loan at year 5, that you can retire the entire loan in 30 years with a constant rate of 9%. The pink line shows the effect of a loan where the rate is kept at 6% the entire way. The pink line serves to show that with a starting rate of 9% and a balloon payment

Think of your credit utilization ratio (CU) as telling a story about your ability to pay your debts. In the United States, we have a fairly sophisticated credit scoring system that is designed to evaluate that ability rather strictly. In general, a high CU is bad; a low CU is better; and zero CU is best of all. So, having credit available but not using it, actually hurts your credit score. For most people, a good credit score is important; for businesses, it's essential. They can't take a chance on Uncle Al because they have no way of knowing if he's really a responsible borrower or not. (Actually, there are ways, but I've simplified the process here.) So, bottom line: using even a small amount of credit available is actually better for your credit score than not using it at all. (Of course, using it "just in case" is not solving the underlying problem.) When I design a credit scoring system, the first thing I do is define the variables. In addition to income, assets, and liabilities (i.e. debts), I include these: I give each variable a weight for its importance in the scoring scheme, and then compose the score from linear combinations of the values for each variable. The weighting depends on the individual for whom you're designing the system, but for most people with regular income and routine payments, having zero liabiliites actually hurts them most because of low asset values and low liquidity. In other words, a rich person can afford to take on more debts than he or she owns, but without actually data regarding income and liabilities, we have to make assumptions. In your case, let's assume for simplicity's sake that CU = 10% is equal to a score of 5 (on a scale that I made up for this example), then your score without liabilities is 7.28 (28/40). With $50 of liabilities, it drops to 6.92 (80/120). Further details matter; that's why I ask more questions about design than I do in this answer. I understand your point about liabilities showing that you can borrow and do things with money that's not yours. But in your example above, the $50 is obviously not "not yours". It is money that you have borrowed. The bank or other lender does not care that it must eventually be paid; what they care about is that it can be paid. If you were to not pay, the bank would sue you. If you lose the suit, they could place a lien on any assets that you have. If you still fail to pay, they could seize your assets. In some cases, a garnishment of your income could be ordered by the court. In any payment society, there are debts and liabilities that are settled through borrowing and lending. It's like a river; it flows through society constantly, people deposit money and withdraw money they need. Your system would be like a dam; the money can't flow through because there's no way to borrow and then repay. In any given transaction, I have a need for someone to pay me what they owe me; the bank satisfies that need. Without the bank, we have to find another way to settle the debt, e.g. through a favor from a friend or by producing goods that the debtor needs as payment. Most people don't have favors to sell and goods to produce. So, the bank satisfies a very real need. I understand your concern about liability showing that you can do things with money that's not yours. That's why it's called "liabilities" rather than "assets". =) But in this case, the liabilities are money that you owe to banks and other creditors. You don't seem to have any assets that would be valuable enough to cover the liabilities; if you did, I'd suggest that you get a line of credit instead; your credit score would go up. The trouble is that your liabilities are gaining; if you ever lost your job or for some other reason stopped generating income, you would be in real trouble; you could end up in one of those "g garnishment" situations I mentioned before. In short, your trouble would start when your liabilities start increasing; and if you stop increasing liabilities, your score will start decreasing. No scoring model tries to take into account the situation where you stop increasing liabilities; thus, I suggest you forgo using credit even if it means improving your score. If you can make the money increase every year, the scoring model isn't a reliable predictor anyway. I understand your concern about liability showing that you can do things with money that's not yours. That's why it's called "liabilities" rather

As an employee, you may deduct travel expenses while operating between your employer designated location and your home. So if Plant #1 is 50 miles from your home and Plant #2 is 25 miles from your home, then the total distance and associated expense is additive. So if Plant #1 is 50 miles from your home and Plant #2 is 50 miles from your home, then the total distance and associated expense is not additive. However, in this case, the closer plant, Plant #2, should be your designated location of employment for purposes of the job-related travel deduction. This is because, to be eligible for the job-related travel deduction, the farther plant #1 would have to be at least 15 miles greater than Plant #2 for the 50 miles to be considered eligible. See Publication 463 for more detail. Employers reimbursement for travel, etc. of this type of expense is considered income to you. Schedule C income, to be exact. As to whether it is "reasonable", that is a judgement call made during the audit. If the number of miles seems obviously in excess of what would be expected for your position, it will be questioned. If it is borderline, you have a better chance of it being upheld if Plant #1 is further than 15 miles greater than Plant #2. If you are making multiple short trips during the day between Plant #1 and Plant #2, it is likely that your actual number of miles driven is higher than the straight line distance between them. If you are claiming the entire differential, you will need to be prepared to justify how your normal route and the fact that you are making multiple trips between Plant #1 and Plant #2 affects the number of miles driven. Finally, if you are self-employed, the normal job-related travel expenses are deductible directly on Schedule C, whether performed between your home and an employer location, between two employers or between an employer and a client. However, if you are a contractor, independent contractor, or self-employed person paid by 1099, then the employer is not "required" to validate the expense as reasonable and no job-related expense exists. In this case, you will need to prove the expense is both reasonable and incurred as a self-employed contractor. Note that this is a summary of the major points. There are a number of other inputs that may affect things, and specific situations may exist that alter the outcome. Either keep good records and be prepared to provide validation, or better consult a CPA. One last thing; most CPAs (and certainly me), will recommend keeping track of job-related miles between your home and employer or client, but will also say that most employers won't reimburse unless you acquiesce that they can under-reimburate you. Thus, it is a pretty infrequent occurrence that we see mileage logs being submitted successfully for reimbursement to an employer. So, in your case, if you are claiming 200 miles per week, I presume that means that you are making multiple trips between Plant #1 and Plant #2 during the week. If you are keeping track of miles for tax purposes, you will need to keep track of the specific reason for each trip, among other things. If you are not keeping track of this, this will likely be questioned during an audit. If you are keeping track of it and your normal route is through Plant #2, then the fact that you are making multiple trips between Plant #1 and Plant #2 should be substantiated by the number of miles you are logging between your home and Plant #2. If you are not keeping track of this and get audited, it will likely be questioned. If you are claiming 200 miles per week and making multiple trips between Plant #1 and Plant #2, then your actual number of miles driven will likely be questioned in an audit. If it is borderline, then it will likely be asked to be justified; if it is too high, it is borderline; if it is reasonable, it is unlikely to be an issue. If you are keeping track of the reasons for your trips between your home and Plant #2, then you will be able to demonstrate that the fact that you are making multiple trips between Plant #1 and Plant #2, is reasonable. If not, it will be questioned. Note that "demonstrate" and "justify" mean different things. Demonstrate is more of a showing as opposed to telling, but in this case, telling will suffice. Showing would be keeping track of why you are making the trips and proving it is a valid reason. The fact that you are claiming 200 miles per week should also make your employer(s) wonder why they should be paying you to commute. This is a borderline case for a company car

I think the rule of thumb should be the same as for insurance purposes - if it is in your house, in your car, or with you, it is probably not a good investment. To add to Dillip's points, if you do sustain a loss, how will you raise the money to replace this asset? It is one thing to say you will liquidate other assets, but how do your creditors prevent you from doing so until such time as you replace the asset? I had to deal with this with a relatively small asset (motorcycle) when I declared bankruptcy, and it was a real hassle to convince the trustee and the court that my paying off a loan for a motorcycle I didn't have, meant that I had to go and get a loan for a car, which meant that I had to provide evidence of a job, proof of income, etc. It would not be easy to do this with a large asset. I personally would feel more comfortable either putting the asset in a company that you own, or insuring it under a separate policy that you take out, than I would keeping it in my own name, but that's just me. But I also only have one home, and it is a relatively small one. The issue is not so much where you keep the asset, so long as it is safe and secure and recoverable in the event you need to make claim on the insurance. Keeping it in a large, unsecured storage facility may not be so wise, but keeping it in your house may not be the best solution either. The main issue is that if you total your car, or your house catches on fire and burns down, your asset is probably gone (unless you have very comprehensive insurance). Thus, you will have to raise money from elsewhere to replace it. If you have to liquidate other assets to raise the money, how do your creditors prevent you from doing that until such time as you replace the asset? It is not easy for someone with no resources to raise money to pay creditors, more difficult than it is to do so when you have resources to begin with, and it would be harder still if you owe money to (for example) brokerages you do business with, plus any professional licenses or union memberships that are legally required to be insured against loss. I don't have any experience with the UK laws, but in the US, you may have to take a drivers' education course and pass with a certain grade, before you can get your license back. You would not want to be unable to pass that test, or drive a car, for a significant period post-bankruptcy. That's not to say that you shouldn't insure a valuable asset, just that you should make sure that you have a very watertight plan on how you will replace it in the event that it is lost. I would also assume that if you are in your 20's, you probably don't have that much in the way of valuables anyway, without wife and family to insure. That's not to say you should start, just that if you do, you should make sure that the insurance covers everything you have, including any assets that are not in your name but are legally recoverable by you as if they were yours (such as a spouse, for example). I realise this has drifted considerably from the question of where to keep the asset, but hopefully you can see that there are valid reasons why keeping it in your house may not be the best idea. And perhaps you will forgive me for offering suggestions which you may find distasteful. But like I say, just my 2c from within the penny. And I am not a financial advisor, nor is this advice. And if you do decide to insure an asset and you can afford to take a few blows, I would strongly suggest getting comprehensive insurance on it too. And if you are worried about your house burning down, there is nothing I can say; you should consult a professional insurance salesman (I have no affiliation with any, I swear!). Good luck. :) --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- As a postscript, I will suggest another solution which may work for high value assets, but which may not be wise if the asset is your only possession of value. If you have multiple assets (which I had at the time I got insured, and still have today but no longer use) put them in a company that you own. Thus they will be recoverable in the event you sustain an insurance loss, but at least they are now an asset of the company, not yours personally. That way, you can insure the company, or the risk is spread across the assets (although I suppose if the house went up in flames the furniture would not be replaced by insurance money, would it?) I did this with a number of high-value items, including a motorbike, a

Unless you negotiate a salary advance, your paycheck will be spaced out over the year, so in some months you'll have $4000 extra in your account over other months. That will average out to $1000 per month, which is a pretty big chunk of money. I'll assume that your company will treat you like a part-time employee in terms of benefits, since you'll only be working 25 hours a week. (Note: This is a definite assumption, and it's not clear from your question if this is true or not. If your hours will be treated like those of a full-time employee even though you'll only be paid as if you worked part-time, that will significantly change things.) That, combined with the fact that you'll get 10% vacation, means that you'll end up with $110,000, which will be paid to you over 26 paychecks, so each paycheck will be $4230.31. That's your gross pay. The employer's share of FICA will cost the company $25,525, and its's going to pay in overtime ($2083.37), so in all, the $135,533.37 is going to cost the company $28,408.76. That means that your net pay is $61,114.61, which will be paid over 26 paychecks, so each paycheck will be $2304. That's what you'll have after taxes, assuming that your company treats you like a part-time employee in terms of benefits. If instead your hours will be treated like those of a full-time employee in terms of benefits, here's what you'll have to work with. Your salary will be $50,000 instead of $40,000, and your 10% vacation pay will be $5,000 instead of $4,500, for a total of $55,000, which will be paid to you over 26 paychecks, so each paycheck will be $2153.38. Your employer's cost is going to be $135,533.37, which is being reported as income to you, so in all, you're going to have $190,533.37 divided by 26 paychecks, so each paycheck will be $7,300. Note: This isn't what it would have been if you'd been hired as a contractor, in which case you would have gotten $135,533.37 in 13 paychecks, each paycheck being $10,400 (the two paycheck amounts sum to the total, assuming that you understand how to read summary of an IRS 1040 form). The difference is that an employee, as opposed to a contractor, is entitled to certain benefits in addition to pay. See https://www.dol.gov/general/topic/employment/laborstandards for more on that. I'm not sure what the state laws are, as the information above is based on Federal (IRS & DoL) inputs. The 21 hours a week plus 4 hours a week for training, if counted as hours worked, would have resulted in the situation where your salary, vacation pay, and employer costs altogether would have added up to $135,533.37, so your net pay would have been $135,533.37 divided by 26 paychecks, resulting in $526.58 per paycheck. I'll assume that your state is like California, in that it imposes an income tax on only the base $40,000, and you'll net $29928.29 a year after taxes, which will be paid over 26 paychecks, so each paycheck will be $115.59 after taxes. (Note: This is a definite assumption. Check your state website for income tax rates and deductions. Since it's almost certain that your 4 hours a week of training will be counted as hours worked, even if your state treats that as time worked, that changes things significantly.) For comparison, let's assume that instead you worked for Amazon, or some other company that treats part-time employees the same as full-time employees in terms of hours worked but not pay. I feel this makes a good discussion, but I believe this is off topic. I'm saying it is off topic because what you are looking for is a replacement for a liquid emergency fund. The purpose of an emergency fund is to smooth out short term income fluctuations. Keeping expensive items on hand that can be sold for cash isn't really what an emergency fund should be for. It sounds like you are looking for a liquid emergency fund. I feel like this is off topic because we aren't meant to answer questions about maintaining wealth. We are meant to help with personal finance. Maintaining wealth is different than personal finance. Personal finance is how to organize, deploy, and track your money on a day to day basis. Maintaining wealth is something different. Maintaining wealth includes saving for retirement, saving for college, saving for a downpayment on a house, wealth behavior that fits a discussion about an emergency fund, and probably a bunch of other stuff I don't know about. The downside is that you may be forced to sell items at a discounted price when you need them the most. In other words, you may not be able to sell them for their true value. This isn't really a problem if you have items that you know you can sell for fair value, such as through an online marketplace or in a local flea market, but it may be a problem for items that are more obscure. For example, say you have a rare Japanese teacup that you would like to sell for its true value. Unfortunately, most people who would buy Japanese teacups at your asking price probably wouldn't know what a rare Japanese teacup is worth. As a result, you may have to sell it for a much lower price than you would like. This is just one of the reasons why keeping expensive items on hand isn't a good idea as an emergency fund. There are better ways to save money and have it be easily accessible when you need it. marginLeft The upside is that you will earn some interest on your savings, which will help you to earn back some of the money you set aside. However, most savings accounts and money market accounts offer very low interest rates, and it may not be enough to make up for the money you are giving up by not being able to access your money when you need it most. For most people, the interest they will earn on their emergency fund probably won't matter much. For people who live very close to the edge day in and day out, the inability to access their money may matter a lot more. Overall, keeping expensive items on hand to sell is not a good idea as an emergency fund. There are better ways to save your money that is easily accessible when you need it. Regardless of what anyone else says, keeping expensive items on hand to sell is not a good idea as an emergency fund because it doesn't align with the goals of an emergency fund. An emergency fund should smooth out short term income fluctuations. Keeping expensive items on hand to sell doesn't do that. An emergency fund shouldn't include saving money for retirement, saving for college, saving for a downpayment on a house, or maintaining wealth. Keeping expensive items on hand to sell isn't really what an emergency fund should be for. I realize a lot of people will chime in and say they did that, or people they know did that, or it's a good idea... All well and good, but from a theoretical perspective keeping expensive items on hand to sell is not a good idea as an emergency fund because of the drawbacks it trades off against. If you have $10,000 set aside in easily accessible money, and you need $2,000, you should be able to access the $2,000 in 24 hours at most. After that, you can use the remaining $8,000 to pay off debt, or invest in income generating assets, or save for another specific goal. If I have to sell items to get that $2,000 back, it may take me much longer to get it back, potentially extending the time frame out weeks or even months. If I have to pay for storage, it may also reduce the amount of time I can't access the money, but it also reduces the amount of money I can make back on, generating income from, the money I've set aside. This is why keeping expensive items on hand isn't a good idea as an emergency fund. I hope this helps. I understand this is a sensitive topic, but I feel like from a theoretical perspective, this is the correct answer. If people have items they need to move to get debt under control, that's great, and I'm sure there are plenty of success stories out there. However, from a personal finance perspective, keeping expensive items on hand to move towards a financial goal isn't really what a personal finance forum should be encouraging people to do. No offense intended.

There is no "mini Google" stock. Google is a complex company with many different business units, some of which are quite profitable. They get their revenue from advertising just like other ad-ware companies, so buying stock in Google is equivalent of betting on the success of the entire company, including all its subsidiaries, not just the advertising part of the business. What is the ticker symbol of the mini Google stock? There isn't one. There aren't different stocks for different parts of Google's business, at least not publicly-traded ones. Google is a publicly-traded company with ticker GOOGL on the NASDAQ. What is the ticker symbol of the mini Google stock? There isn't one. There aren't different stocks for different parts of Google's business, at least not publicly-traded ones. Google is a publicly-traded company with ticker GOOGL on the NASDAQ. What if some people just take a part of the Google brand and set up a business that provides similar service? Won't that be another "mini Google"? There are thousands of different websites that are similar to Google's, but there not affiliated with it in any way. They all use the Google API to show results from search operations, and they all rely on adware model of revenue collection. However, they all have their own features and advantages, and some people may prefer one over Google because of better performance on certain types of searches or because of different criteria Google uses for ranking results. Google has a patent monopoly on the search results ranking algorithm, but that only applies to websites. Results from other kinds of searches, like video results or results from other kinds of queries, aren't covered by Google's patent monopoly. There are thousands of different companies that provide video rental services, and there isn't a "mini Netflix" stock that you can buy. There are thousands of different companies that rent videos, but they all use different criteria for ranking results of their searches, and none of them has a monopoly on the search results ranking algorithm. Sure, but how would you know which ones are knock-offs and which ones aren't? There are many ways of telling whether a website is a knock-off, and here are a few to start: If the URL starts http:// then it's not a knock-off. There are knock-offs that use https:// but most people aren't savvy enough to notice the difference. If the URL isn't http:// then it is a knock-off. The site may or may not use HTTPS. If the site is in the format of something.website1.com/something/something.html and something.website1.com isn't owned by you, it's a knock-off even if the site uses HTTP. If the site is in the format of something.website1.com/something/something.html and something.website1.com is owned by you, it probably is a knock-off if the site isn't also hosted on your servers. If the site is in the format of something.website1.com/something/something.html and something.website1.com isn't owned by you but something.website1.com is being hosted on your servers, you might want to check whether something.website1.com is one of your subsites by checking DNS records. If the site is at an IP address you don't own, it is a knock-off even if the URL starts http:// and you otherwise recognize the site. If the text letters in the website URL are in ALL UPPERCASE, it is a knock-off even if everything else about the URL is correct. If the website has insecure connections, i.e. doesn't use HTTPS when it should, it is a knock-off even if everything else about the URL is correct. If the website is perfectly identical to Google's in every way but the results of searches and the links on the results page don't go to Google's servers but instead go to servers someplace else and this despite everything else about the page being identical to Google's, it is a knock-off. If the website is perfectly identical to Google's in every way but the results of searches are shown in a different order, it is a knock-off. If the website is perfectly identical to Google's in every way but the ads are different, it is a knock-off. If the website is perfectly identical to Google's in every way but the logo has a different shape to it, it is a knock-off. If the website is perfectly identical to Google's in every way but the logo is missing, it is a knock-off. If the website is perfectly identical to Google's in every way but the colors of the page are different, it is a knock-off. If the website is perfectly identical to Google's in every

Don't try to time the market. It is impossible for an individual to beat the market and consistently do well. Instead, be a buy-and-hold investor. The best time to buy is when there is fear in the market. This usually occurs during recessions. You can use the News to determine when this is happening, but it is hard to do this consistently. A better way is to buy when the trailing S-and-P 500 yield is greater than the bond yield. The bond yield is a safe and easy investment that you can switch into when you are afraid the market is going to drop. The S-and-P 500 is a composite of the 500 largest companies in the United States. Its performance is the benchmark against which other stock market indices are often compared. When investing in stocks, higher risk usually equals higher yield. If you put all your money in one stock, you either gain or lose your entire investment when the stock's value changes. To spread risk, buy stocks from many different companies. You can do this by buying an index fund. An index fund is a type of mutual fund that attempts to follow the movements of an index. There are index funds that follow the S-and-P 500, the Dow Jones Industrial Average, and many other stock market indices. When you buy an index fund, you own the entire market minus the fees charged by the fund. There are many different index funds to choose from. Two of the best known are Vanguard and Fidelity. There are fees associated with owning index funds. These fees reduce the rate of return you get on your investment. The lower the fees, the better. Vanguard is known for its low fees. You can often pay zero commission on your transactions. Check out this article by Kyleуза at Investor's Place. It sounds like you are interested in angel investing which is very risky and therefore needs careful evaluation. The article provides some good references to get started. Also, consider skipping the stock market altogether and investing in real estate. There are many good pre-edited videos on YouTube that showcase various real estate deals. Some examples are here and here. You can do this without having to buy a property outright which can be risky if the deal turns out to be a dog. Then again, real estate can also be a wonderful investment. You can also use the equity in your house to leverage up and invest with your
If you can find a good deal, you can charge 10% above the going rate, rest your 20% down payment, and have a mortgage payment that is 25% lower than your new mortgage, and rent the place for 15% more than your new mortgage payment, and have more than 40% equity every month. That's with a 5% mortgage rate, adjust the magic numbers upward if your rates are higher.
From personal experience, I can say that if you make a 20% down payment and mortgage a house for less than its value, you can rent it for 1.5x the mortgage payment and charge 10% more than the going rate and you'll make out very nicely. In my case, I was paying $900/mo for an apartment that I could've charged $1340/mo to rent had I wanted, but I got burned a few times and didn't. But over the 10 years I owned that place, I collected over $70,000 dollars in rent money, and never had a vacancy greater than 10 days. The people that rented for $900 moved to Texas in search of cheaper living. Sure, it was fun to be flush with cash each month, but the equity built up over those 10 years was the real value. In the 10 years I owned that place, I built up $120,000 of equity, and collected $70,000 in rent, both tax free since it was rented out. I put $12,000 down, but that included the cost of moving expenses, appraisal fees, attorney fees, etc. Adjust for that, and my total gains were $80,000. The house rose in value, but not significantly. I did have one year where the value of the house increased by $10,000, but rented $29,000 more that year. So that year I had $39,000 of gains. It can happen. The thing about real estate is that there are so many different possibilities. The judgment provides for collection through the sale of the property. Your father's share of the judgment (2/3) may be enforced against his estate. Your mother and yourself would not have any liability for your father's share of the debt. Your mother does not owe your father's share of the debt. If your father owed his ex-wife a third of the judgment, that obligation died with your father. You, on the other hand, inherited your father's 1/3 of the judgment. You may be forced to sell your share (1/3) of the judgment if you try to collect on the judgment through the sale of the house. But you have the option to collect on your share (1/3) of the judgment through a variety of means, including foreclosure. If your mother wants to buy you out, that is certainly an option. If you have equity in the house, you could probably refinance the house and use the proceeds to buy out your mother. You don't have to sell the house if you don't want to.ferous This is an interesting twist on a common situation. As you have discovered, when spouses have claims against each other's estates, there is a judicial forum in which to sort things out. Assuming arms length dealing, your mother could now demand that your father's share of the house be sold and the proceeds divided three ways. Unfortunately for your mother, the house is probably not worth pursuing. If your father intended to leave his share of the house to you, that intent, expressed during his lifetime, should pass to your share of the judgment. If the intent wasn't expressed explicitly, then the value of your father's share of the house at the time of his death, coupled with your own claims on the house, should be enough to trigger a judicial finding that your share of the judgment is likewise worth the value of the house. If the house were put up for sale, I suppose your mother could try to claim encumbrances against it, but I doubt a court would go along with that. Rather, I think your mother would be better off trying to get a third of your share of the judgment, should you choose to sell the house and put those proceeds into play. The judgment provides for an equitable distribution of the judgment. If your father intended to leave his share of the house to you, that intent, expressed during his lifetime, passes to your share of the judgment. If your father didn't express that intent explicitly, then the value of your father's share of the house at the time of his death, coupled with your own claims on the house, should be enough to trigger a judicial finding that your share of the judgment is likewise worth the value of the house. In either case, a judicial finding on the value of your share of the judgment would provide an equitable basis for distribution. If you decide to sell the house and use the proceeds to buy out your mother, that would certainly be an equitable result. If you decide to refinance the house and use the proceeds to buy out your mother, that would certainly be an equitable result. If you decide to collect the equity from the house through foreclosure, or some other means which allows you to collect on your share (1/3) of the judgment through a variety of means, including foreclosure, that would certainly be an equitable result. If your mother simply tries to assert claims of encumbrance against your share of the judgment, that too would appear to be equitable results. Your share of the judgment is yours to do with as you please. If you decide to sell the house and buy out your mother that is certainly a reasonable thing to do. If you decide to refinance the house and buy out your mother that is also a reasonable thing to do. If you decide to maintain possession of the house and demand your mother pay you rent, that too would appear reasonable. If you decide to commit environmental hazards, or other public health and safety hazards, or commit acts which tend to devalue the house, your mother has the judicial forum to make claims against your share of the house. However, if you simply maintain occupancy of the house without putting people at environmental or public health hazards, or commit no harmful acts, your mother would appear to have no judicial recourse. Note that these results are predicated upon your not having any claims on the house yourself. If you have claims on the house of any description, such as a mechanic's lien, or a security interest, then you will need to make sure that these claims are protected before you sell the house. However, in that case you are entitled to judicial relief in order to protect your claims, just as your mother is. Finally, I would strongly advise against refinancing the house.
