There are two separate aspects to HFTs that you should understand before you worry about this. 1) Pace of Trade. You are suggesting that ordinary traders pace the settlement of their trades to the speed at which they conducted them. This would be very inefficient. Instead, the speed at which trades settle is dictated by the market, not the trader. Just as examples, theSOLVED stock on the NYSE traded 23+ million times in the last hour, and settled as a single lot about 90 minutes later. Or, take a look at the trading of NATX, a company in the news a lot lately. Over the last week, the stock traded more than 30 million times, with more than 20 million of those trades having settlement date more than 3 business days out. 2) Buy and Sell Arrow. You are confusing the settlement of your trade with the trade itself. When you sell a stock, what you actually do is enter into an agreement to buy the stock back at some point in the future. This is why you need to have money available now, even though the person you're selling the stock to won't have to pay you for it for days. For example, let's say on Thursday you sell 100 shares of GOOG at 130. What actually happens is on some day in the future you agree to buy 100 shares of GOOG from yourself for 130. The system doesn't let you do that unless you have the funds available now. (There are other reasons you might not want to do this; if you actually need the money sooner to pay an unexpected bill, for example). The way HFTs work is they have computers calculate the probability of a given trade settling at a given time. They also have computers calculating the opposite side of the trade, for example, if a given position they have could be profitably be sold short. If the two probabilities are great enough to make the transaction worthwhile, the computers will execute the transaction. This is basically how short selling works. The difference is that the computers are making thousands of these decisions every second, and they're looking at thousands of different positions. Because the settlement of trades is dictated by the market and not the individual trader, computers can take advantage of settling trades on the behalf of investors. I hope that helps. Good luck! --- EDIT FOR QUESTIONS ASKED IN COMMENTS: 1) The systems aren't "set up that way" - they're programmed that way. The pace of trade is dictated by the market, not the individual trader. The individual trader can certainly trade at the pace at which settlement occurs, but most ordinary traders don't have the systems set up to do so profitably because they don't have the resources to take advantage of the market timing available. 2) When I say the computers are "buying and selling" a position, what I mean is they are setting up a "to buy" order for a position that isn't available to them yet (because the previous seller hasn't settled) and a "sell" order for a position that they currently have (but which in some cases might have to be re-bought from the original seller). The chances of these orders matching are pretty low, but if they do, the position would be settled and the two trades would be executed. 3) Yes, this is basically how short selling works. The difference is in the orders set up by the HFT. Whereas the orders are to sell a position that doesn't exist to someone who doesn't exist yet. (The existence of the position and the trader is of course verified by the broker.) Also, I should mention that there are restrictions on how many such orders you can set up, and need to maintain throughout the life of the order a net short position less than 10% of the total shares outstanding. This is an aspect of the SEC short sale rules. 4) I don't know how I "misleadingly" answered any questions. I stated the way I understood HFTs to work, was asked questions about how they worked, and answered them to the best of my ability. If that is misleading, please specify which part and why. 5) Yes, I know settlement can take more than 2 business days. I was talking mainly about the difference between the speed at which trades are executed and the speed at which settlement occurs. If you want to know about the timelines involved in different types of settlements, ask another question. 6) I don't know how I "exaggerated." I don't know how to calculate the number of trades executed per second on NATX, but I know it was a lot. I don't have personal experience

Forbes has an article that discusses how Yahoo! Finance tracks dividends: How To Read The Yahoo! Finance Tax-Equity Data And Where To Start From the article: Your first stop will be the page(s) showing the tax-equity data for each symbol. These data have components for dividends, splits, stock-related events and earnings updates. The dropdown menu above the table lets you choose which type of event you want to see. If you select "Dividends", you'll see a list of ex-div dates, the dividend amount (in regular and dividend symbols), the dividend status (whether it's confirmed, estimated or just a payout ratio), and the date for which data including the dividend has been incorporated into the price. In other words, the dividend is reflected in the stock price as of the last day before ex-div. This is the date for which you need to exclude the dividend from calculations, because it isn't really "income" as the saying goes. (Dividends are not capital gains, and this is important - if you're looking at the "Tax-Equity" link under "My Finance".) As I mentioned in the lead-up, this page also includes information about splits and other events, but I'll just touch on those briefly. The split information shows the split ratio and ex-split date; the other information is outside my personal scope. As an example of the scope issue, I don't regard "EPS" as an important indicator for events like splits and dividends because they can be estimated and become irrelevant as the stock price moves around. I do like their inclusion but I just ignore these fields. On the page with your specific symbol selected, click on the little arrow next to the dividend table and select "Tax-Equity Events". Here's a portion of the resulting page showing the data for TXE: As you can see, this page shows the date for which the next dividend (estimated) will be incorporated into the stock price, and it also shows the dividend amount (estimated). On this page, you can also see whether the dividend has changed over time and see the estimated payout ratio. I gleaned a lot of important information about dividends on this page, but it doesn't include the actual dates for ex-dividend and incorporation of the dividend. That information is on the previous page, above. The "Estimated Payout Ratio" is useful because it can be compared with the actual ratio to see how accurately the estimate was, and also to estimate the likely dividend for a given stock. I have this data for dozens of stocks and have found this to be very useful for the long-term investor. For the short-term investor, it's not as useful since it's just reflected in the price and incorporated into the stock value anyway. (I regard estimated information with a disclaimer such as "estimated payout ratio and earnings date" as important information for long-term investing but not so much for short-term investing.) Sure, it does. But it's not always perfect. In particular, as noted in the article, the earnings date is estimated. Mint.com is a great tool, but like all personal finance tools, they rely on you to input your data accurately. It's easy to miscategorize something as a expense when it's actually income, or to do vice versa. The math still works out to your point of wanting to improve your finances, but it's not going to give you an accurate employeation of "this is how much money I have" at this point. I agree that it's funny that we have a whole host of personal finance websites and forums here to discuss forums and websites, but cannot do the same for health forums and websites. The principle is the same though. It's still math, it's still a potential distraction, and it's still a potential source of anxiety. That being said, I agree that it's great that there are tools available to help people get a better financial picture. I think it's great that people are trying to help each other become better with money. I'm not sure what the technical mechanism would be, but I suspect that Mint might share some data with other Intuit properties. I know that Quicken (a family member has that, I use Honeyfi to manage our joint account) sends data over to TurboTax, for example. The sharing of data between Intuit properties might be one-way, or it might be a two-way street. I don't know. I think it's worth knowing these practices in advance, is all. Sure, here is my standard disclaimer: This response does not constitute financial advice. Talk to a registered investment advisor in your state for financial advice. You are responsible for choosing what advice you accept. The information you get from credit reports (both personal and corporate) can be used to commit fraud. There was an article not too long ago about how businesses accepting credit reports have a higher risk of fraud from those reports. This is why I say that you should talk to a registered investment advisor in your state. They can help you decide what is best in your situation. You can get blacklisted from using credit if you miss a payment or two. This is another thing that is worth having a good relationship with your banker for. They can help you get back on track if you run into hard times. They can also help you in regards to co-signing something for your spouse. There are better ways to do that. There are better ways to help your spouse learn to manage money. Mint.com might help, but it's not clear. Sometimes there is a temptation to reach for a quick fix solution. My suggestion is that you make a plan to solve the underlying problem, and then execute on that plan. Using Mint to just track your finances might not be the best solution, but it might be a good starting point. I agree with all of the above posts. Mint is a great tool, but it assasslates my available-cash wrong. It includes stuff that is not liquid as well as not-quite-mine. For example, it lists 40K of "Shared Assets", of which 30K is "Stocks"; I don't see how that is mine to claim as available funds. It lists 10K of "Other Assets"; I presume this is our house, which is great except it is not liquid and I can't access it to pay bills. So I'm not using it to pay bills, and it doesn't really help me all that much. (I pay most of my bills via automatic deduction from my checking account, which is reflected as "Other Current Assets" to the penny.) So I use it as a general ledger but not as a cash statement. (And I still have to teach it how to correctly parse my transactions.) I like mint for the reminders to pay bills and to see the due dates. And it's fun to see the little spending competitions between rooms in the house. (Looks like the office is the biggest spender, woo hoo!) But I think it would be misleading for me to rate it as a personal finance management tool. It would be better to rate it as a tool for non-bill-payers. So in the end, it depends on what you need it for. If you pay your bills via check from a bank account, then it might be a great tool for you. If you want a complete personal financial statement, maybe keep at it and ignore the miscalculations, or use it as a tool to gather data for your own personalized financial statements. The best thing about saving money in the bank is quick access to your funds. I have a savings account with just over $100,000 on it which I use for everyday things - apart from the fact that I have a checking account on the same balance, I also use it to pay into because I can just transfer funds from my checking to my saving account. The main issue with this is overdraft charges. I quite like having a fixed amount of money in each account - my checking account has $0.01 in it so I can never overdraw it, and my saving account has $100,000 in it so I will never have an issue with needing more money in my checking account. Overall all I use my saving account for is daily transactions, so I quite like the idea of fixing the balance in each account and never having overdraft charges apply. Everyone is different though. If you have a lot of fluctuating funds in your checking account and you don't have a separate saving account, I would recommend getting one. I also recommend getting a separate loan from your saving account balance - that way if you get more business or make more money, your saving account balance won't go up and start getting interest charged on. I don't know how old you are or what your financial situation is exactly, but I got a student loan when I started working full-time after university which lets me get a loan with 9.32% APR on my salary (I'm in New Zealand if that makes a difference). The nice thing about this is that the balance on my loan doesn't affect my saving account, and I can pay it off over 7 years interest free (I'll probably end up paying interest as I'll probably forget to pay it off haha). This is an example of a type of saving your money can be used for to an extent. In my country (New Zealand) banks are really big on saving money, and everyone and their dog has a saving account. It's part of the culture now. When I go to the shops with my partner, both of us get a $100 gift card for presenting our student loan card (it counts as using the card for a minimum amount). This is also a good sign to retailers - you get more concessions etc. The government also provides a free saving account for school kids which grows to $50 by the time they turn 18. I remember when I was 10 getting my first deposit bank account and getting a stuffed animal toy with it too. It's part of teaching kids financial responsibility which I think is important. I hope this helps. The post has been voted to be closed as it turns out the topic is restricted to a specific country. The OP is welcome to create a new question with specifics about what they want to save for, and what kind of account would be best for that. I'm closing as well. That said, there aren't really any kinds of fixed-rate savings accounts that are interesting to adult savers. The APY for this kind of account would be so low it would be pointless. Basically what you have are choices between (1) putting your money in something that might give a decent rate and have some restrictions on it, or (2) putting your money in something that doesn't have restrictions but might give some interest. The amount of money you're talking about is small enough that even the terrible APYs we have in the US would make decent money so I suspect the restrictions on higher levels of savings are going to be a show-stopper. My suggestion is to just keep your money in your checking account and skip the savings altogether. If you were saving a lot of money, or planning to have a lot of money, then at some point it would make sense to move at least a portion of it into something that pays interest. But for someone who only has $2000 it makes the most sense to keep it safe and easy to spend every day. I'm closing this again. If you are looking for a simple rule of thumb, stocks that pay dividends will tend to follow the dividend paid times the number of shares held. It's a fairly straight forward calculation and one that is relatively easy to understand. For example, if a company declares a $1.00 dividend per share and you hold 1000 shares, you can expect your account to be credited $1000 the day of the dividend distribution. This is a simplified example and usually there are more than a few days of activity around the declaration of the dividend and the distribution of the dividend. Just search for the company symbol + div. For example if it were Apple you would type Apple div and you would get the details of Apple's dividends. Check out https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_companies_by_P/E_ratio#High_P.2F.2E_and_P.2F.2ED_ratios for a list of companies that pay a dividend and their current dividend yield. For a company that doesn't pay a dividend you will have to calculate the dividend, times the number of shares held, to get the capital gain or loss over the transaction. For example if you bought 1000 shares of Apple for $95 per share, sold 1000 shares of Apple for $125 per share and at the time of sale Apple had not declared a dividend, you would have a $30,000 capital gain ($125k*1000 shares - $95k*1000 shares) If you paid $3 per share in commission the total transaction cost was $30,003 (~$3 per share). You could calculate the P/E ratio at the time of sale using the price paid for the shares $125k*1000 shares / $30k = 41.68 or in each individual share $41.68 / 1000 = 0.042 (read more about P/E here http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Price_to_earnings_ratio) You would also calculate the P/E ratio of the current price of the stock. For Apple it would be (320.85*1000 - 30k) / 0.042 = $136,604 However this isn't quite right, you would need to average the P/E ratio over the shares owned. So if you had averaged over the whole population of Apple shares this is how you would calculate it. ((0.042*320.85*1000 - 0.042*30k + 95.87*1000 - 30k) / 2005) = $93.27 This would apply to any number of stocks. You would calculate the average P/E or yield or other attribute. Here is an example from Google The Google example shows the current price of GOOGL is $1053.81 and the dividend paid was 1.15 which would result in an annualized dividend yield of 1.15/1054 = 0.109 or 10.9%. The P/E ratio is calculated as the price of the stock divided by the intrinsic value. In this case the P/E is 27.6. However, in most cases you will use an average because you probably own different shares than I do. For example if you owned 1000 shares of GOOGL and Google declared a dividend of 1.25 there would be $1250 credited to your account followed by a commission charge of say $30 or 3% whichever is greater. The stock price may have gone up or down with the recent trades providing you with more or fewer shares depending on your purchase and sale transactions. However, for the average you can calculate the average shares owned and calculate the dividend yield and P/E on that average. When using the term "dividend stock" you are indicating to other investors that your stock pays a dividend and expect other Apple, Electric, or Adobe investors the same. This is why in the case of Apple's dividend investors are "surprised and delighted" according to the WSJ. They probably weren't expecting it and when they get their quarterly statement they will be very pleased. This will not have lasting effect on the stock price because the dividend is variable from quarter to quarter. It could rise it could lower or be equal to or greater than the previous quarter. Apple could have paid $0.3

If his tactics are "value investing" then they are imposters. Value investing is done by individual investors every day. Anyone can do value investing. What makes a business a "value trap" is when it is difficult for you to sell it or find a buyer for it. This is not the same as being a "hidden value" or "hidden risk." Any business is a better investment if you can sell it than it is if you cannot. There is an entire market for selling businesses, so it is relatively easy to find a buyer for any business. The difficulty is making sure that you get top dollar for the business. But even if you don't, you can still sell the business and use the proceeds to buy another business that is a better investment than the one you already have. I (and most investors) would rather have a business that I can sell for a lot than one that I can't sell at all.//...Or one that I can sell for a lot and then buy another business with the proceeds.!):---! The main benefit is that you are contributing pre-tax income to (401(k), (or (IRA) so the growth does not count as income. In a sense it keeps you from chasing after inflating income. Of course when you withdraw it (assumedly at your retirement) it will be taxed as income again, but that is far down the road when markets are expected to have higher gains. You are also supposed to stop contributing when you reach a certain income level (20k+ in most cases) so it is not like you can just choose to have this income later. The contribution limits are low enough that you could contribute all your pre-tax earnings and have all of it be deducted: in 2010 the limit is $5500 for individual savers. If you earn $11,000 or so you would have $5500 in taxesaved "income" that would be taxed at your marginal rate when you withdraw it after 5 years (or when you retire). It will be taxed as though it were interest on a savings account you never opened. For (401(k) savers the employer gets to set up the plan, decide how much they will match, and can choose to offer to contribute for their employees. You would have to ask what the current situation is. With 401(k) some but not all employers will also make matching contributions to their employees' accounts. You would have to ask your employer what, if any, contributions they will make. IRA savers do not have an employer so no such question makes sense. The growth of a 401(k) IRA account will not be taxed for the owner. At some point, when you stop working you will no longer be eligible to contribute to a (401(k) account but you can continue to contribute to an IRA. The growth of an IRA account will be used for current income taxes. You can withdraw money from both the (401(k) and IRA accounts tax free until you reach a certain age (65 in most states but 59.5 in particular reference to 401(k) accounts). After that age you will have to take the distribution (also known as liquidation) of the accounts and will have to pay taxes on the growth (but not the contribution; this is called "penalty free" money). The amount of the distribution you must take is called the required distribution or RD. The amounts are specified by the IRS (the 401(k) creator) and are enforced by the IRS. If you work for yourself or are not employed at all, you will be required to start taking distributions in earnest after that age. If you work for an employer and continue to work after retirement age, your employer will decide to stop contributing to your 401(k) in favor of claiming your superannuation guarantee obligations are met through superannuation. You will then be sent a notice that says you are now required to start providing superannuation for your staff. This is where the superannuation industry gets its foot in the door. If you continue working for an employer after retirement age you will also be obliged to contribute superannuation for your staff. You will not be making super contributions for yourself because you will no longer be receiving superannuation guarantees, but you must provide super for your staff. The super must be contributed to an account in the staff member's name and is an amount that would have otherwise been paid to that staff member in salary had they not elected super. A common super guarantee is 9% of salary. This money is in addition to superannuation guarantee super, which would have been paid to the staff member's super fund instead of being paid to the staff member. Some employers will choose to superate their staff, in which case the super is paid to the staff member's super fund instead of being paid to the staff member. This is called super guarantee super. If an employer superates staff, the employee no longer has a super guarantee super obligation. This is why super is called super sexy - super sexy super. If you are making super contributions and then working for a new employer that superates you into super, you can be seen to be paying super tax on money that was not in fact income tax deductible. This is why it is super sexy super. You can get tax advice on whether or not you should contribute super for yourself, but if you are working for an employer it is very likely that super is in fact a tax deductible expense. If you are making super contributions and then make contributions to an SMSF, then the growth of the SMSF is also super tax free until you withdraw it (withdrawals are meant to be for super purposes, like buying a house but if you just spend

There are many ways you can invest in emerging markets. One way is to use an index fund or ETF that tracks an index of emerging market stocks, bonds, or currencies. Some popular examples include: You can also invest in emerging markets by buying the stocks of companies that are based there. Some well-known companies that are based in emerging markets include: There are many other examples. You can find a list of well-designed emerging markets funds here: http://www.hedgefundarchive.org/Funds/Emerging+Markets+Funds If you want to invest in an emerging market that is not on this list, you will need to make sure that the fund is well-designed before you invest your money in it. There are also a number of investment banks and brokerages that specialize in emerging markets. Some well-known examples include:==""){ You can also find a list of well-designed emerging markets brokerages and banks here: http://www.hedgefundarchive.org/Banks/Investment+Banks+and+Brokerages+in+Emerging+Markets If you want to invest in an emerging market that is not on this list, you will need to make sure that the firm is well-designed before you invest your money. What is important to remember when investing in emerging markets is that these markets are generally more volatile than developed markets. The risk of losing money in an emerging market investment is higher than in a developed market investment. You should make sure that you are comfortable with the risk before you invest your money. Also, beware of load funds. For emerging markets, it is rare that a load is justified. The classes of shares with loads often have very low net asset values because the class has fees that reduce the NAV. You will get much better value with the non-load share class. Some examples of good, low-cost, non-loaded emerging market funds are: The problem with investing in emerging markets is that it is hard to find good, low-cost funds. Too many investors means high fees. Good luck! Also, I reccomend that you not try to time the market or emerging market equities. Instead, you should set a long-term goal, such 10 or 20 years, and make regular contributions, so that you can take advantage of compounding interest. Putting $5,000 in today and forgetting about it for 10 years is a lot better than putting $1,000 in every month for 10 years. For emerging market equities, I reccomend a contribution of $1,000 every 6 months or so. The reason for making the infrequent contributions is to reduce the effect of volatility. If you make regular contributions, you need to be more mindful of market volatility. If you make infrequent contributions, you only need to be mindful for the 2 or 3 months per year that you are investing. If you are not making enough money to cover your opportunity cost, then you should reconsider investing in emerging market equities. It is likely that over the long term, emerging market equities will provide better returns than risk-free investments, but that is not the same as saying that they will provide returns great enough to justify the risk involved, especially if you need a high probability of getting your money back quickly. I reccomend keeping your opportunities open by not investing in anything, and spending money on things that will give you a high probability of getting your money back quickly. If you want to help your community, you can volunteer your time (you can also do this with your friends and family without spending any money). If you want to help your family, you can spend time with them (you can also do this with your friends and family without spending any money). If you want to help yourself, you can spend some time every day developing your abilities and post-crisis career prospects (you can also do this with your friends and family without spending any money). If you want to help yourself and your community, you can spend some time every day developing your abilities and post-crisis career prospects and you can spend money helping your community with the goal of helping people to lead better lives. I reccomend not investing in emerging market equities, and instead keeping your money liquid so that you have the freedom to help your friends, family, and yourself in the best interests of all. I understand that you do not want to simply "throw your money away", and that is why it is important to think about what you are getting for your money. If someone is asking you to give away money without any expectation of recompense, it is reasonable to conclude that you are not getting anything for your money in return. This does not mean that everything that is not a charity is a scam. There are many reasonable things that you can do with your money that do not involve an expectation of monetary return. You can definitely refinance a line of credit at a bank. Wells Fargo is one that provides this service. I don't know if it's easy or not, or if one bank would be better than another. Sure, they're different in that you don't have to pay back a specific amount every month, you only pay back what you owe. However, you do have a maximum balance that you can carry, and after 12 months, you need to bring the balance to 0 or pay interest on the entire amount. Yes, you can re-finance the line of credit just like you can re-finance a mortgage. There are typically no fees associated with reducing the credit line, but check. You'll want to make sure that you'll be able to stay within the new limit in the time frame that works for you. You can't stop using the line of credit just because you've borrowed a certain amount, per se. You can stop using it altogether, in which case it functions like a certificate with access similar to a credit card. You can redraw, up to the new line size, in the time frame allowed. After that time frame (usually 12 months, but check), you'll have to bring the balance to 0, or pay interest on the entire amount outstanding. You don't have a set term, per se. You can definitely pay off a line of credit earlier, and most will let you do so without penalty. (Some have a penalty for paying off early if you do so within a certain time frame, check.) You can definitely have no payments for a set time, just like you can with a credit card. (For a line of credit that advertises "no payments for 18 months", what they usually mean is that there will be no payment required for the first 18 months, provided you borrow at least x dollars and keep the balance below y dollars. After the time frame when there is a balance, you'll have only a minimum payment, which may be $1.) If you're considering getting a line of credit, you should definitely shop around. The rates are definitely better elsewhere than at Chase. I have one, from Vancover, and the APR is 18%. The fee was $50 for any size line of credit. I've used the line for almost 3 years, and have never had any problems with it. I hope this helps. Good luck! The idea seems reasonable enough, though I'd definitely shop around first. I have one line of credit left, and it's with Vancover, a Canadian bank. The APR is 18%, and I've paid a one-time fee of $50 for any size line of credit. I've used it for almost 3 years, and am quite happy with it. I got it when interest rates were a lot higher, or I'd have shopped around longer. The rate is high by today's standards, but my credit's not what it used to be (it went down when I stopped working full-time). I'm paying it off soonish, and will probably get one from my bank next, for probably a better rate. I'll update my profile if I do. I'd definitely go get one if you need one, even if you have to pay a little more for it. Good luck. Awesome, good to hear you're getting one. Good luck! I've never had a mortgage, so I can't compare, but it's probably worth it to wait and get one from your bank if you can. The interest rate will probably be better. I got stuck with Vancover because I could get one elsewhere with worse terms, and I was between moving and starting a new job at the time I wanted one, so I just grabbed the best terms I could find. In retrospect, it wasn't worth it to save a few percent of the APR. With hindsight, I would have waited until my credit was better to get one. I'm going to do that again with a mortgage, once I can get one for less than, say, 4.5 percent. Probably not relevant to you, since you're probably going to get one with an APR of 3.5 anyway. Best of luck! No problem. I'm glad I could help somebody somewhere. :) Looks like I've become "the guy with the credit line" ;) Next question: will I be able to get one at 2.5% :)? You're very welcome! :) I'm glad I could help somebody somewhere. Next question:

For government bonds, the price is primarily determined by the bond's yield matching the risk-free rate investors can obtain in other risk-free investments like T-bills. Bond prices move in opposite direction of Yields. So if a bond price goes down, its yield goes up. Since T-bills are the risk-free rate, if investors are satified with the risk-free rate, they don't see a reason to seek out the risk in bonds (and potentially obtain higher returns), so supply and demand determines the price of T-bills and corresponding ETFs. All other bonds not matching the yield of T-bills will be less desirable and have lower prices (higher yields). Some bond ETFs may try to follow the yield of an index, and if that index has higher yields than the T-bill, investors may prefer that bond ETF over the T-bill vehicle, and the price of that bond ETF will be higher than the T-bill ETF. The following mutual funds have similar objectives and purview, though not all have the same mandate: 1. Fidelity Quality Bond Fund (FDIC)--a mutual fund that tracks the Total Return of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index which consists of U.S. dollar-denominated bonds and Secured, Rated, and Unsecured Serial and Term Loans and Creditors' Certificates publicly traded in the over-the-counter markets and the major stock exchanges of the United States. This essentially means that it tries to get the total return of all U.S. bond markets. 2. Vanguard Total Bond Index Fund (VTB)--a mutual fund that aims for the total return of all U.S. investable issues in the USD. Investable means issuances by state and local governments, though the fund doesn't have to provide specific holdings. 3. Proshares VBM Total Bond Market ETF (TBND)...it tracks the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index which is described above. It has the same objectives and holdings (or intended holdings) as the mutual fund Fidelity Quality Bond Fund above. The bond market is not very deep, especially in the short end. The U.S. Treasury can issue 3 and 6 month T-bills at yields to maturity of .25% and 1.25% respectively, but there will not be enough trading in these bonds (or ETFs holding them) to make accurate prices for maturity out beyond 3 months since the bond market is not very deep. As such, these instruments will tend toward the Treasury's prevailing rates fairly quickly, so prices will move in concert. The reason Treasuries can get away with short yields to maturity and sell short maturity bonds at zero or very low yields is because of the convention in the U.S. market to express yields as a simple percentage, not compounded daily as is used in Europe and Japan. The U.S. market is fairly convention-driven, price for bonds is firm, yields are negotiated, and the converse is true for yields, price is negotiated. A relatively low yield to maturity on a short end security means the security is essentially at its par value, meaning the market price will move fairly quickly to match the next coupon and then the security will appreciate to reach its redemption value at maturity. Since the security is not offering any capital appreciation, it will not be very attractive to many investors and therefore the volume of trading will be low which makes it difficult to get an accurate price for a maturity greater than 3 months. The price of VBM and TBND at 3 months might both be impacted by the 3 month T-bill yield, but at 5 years, the yield will have little impact since the security will have appreciated to its redemption value and its price will move in concert with the 5 year T-note. This answer is long because there are no other aspects to U.S. T-bill and bond ETFsasmuch much of the answer to this question is based on a fundamental understanding of U.S. bond markets. If you are not familiar with bond markets, you should first read the "Bond Adventure" thread in the bogleheads.com forums. It will give you a better idea of what is going on in the bond markets and why. Also, this article by Paul McGrattan describes a bond ETF that is backed by actual bonds sitting in the portfolio rather than being fully derivative like VBM and TBND. The author and I have differing opinions on this practice, but it is an interesting idea. Finally, it is my understanding that the ProShares VBM Total Bond Market ETF (TBND) is essentially a derivative-based ETF similar to the Vanguard Total Bond Index Fund (VTB). TBND also tries to track the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. VBM is not backed by actual bonds sitting in the portfolio (it is my understanding that it is fully derivative like VTB), but

Cash in hand and a known development roadmap are valuable assets. An entity holding those would typically demand a premium over its current price to acquire it. It's similar to buying a house - once you know the price of the house, you'd typically want to negotiate a price below that because you know very much what you are willing to offer. If the other side doesn't agree, there are many other houses to choose from. In similar fashion, if the original company accepts an offer, there will be other willing sellers - so the acquisition price will be neogiatiated. 0% financing up front may be a part of the deal - an arm around the neck tactic where Company B directly finances the purchase from its own assets, thus removing the cash from the market itself. This of course means that Company B is foregoing the interest that it would receive from lending the money to the purchaser, so the purchase price will need to be even higher to make up for this opportunity cost. Companies don't typically go into debt to purchase assets - they either use cash on hand, or they issue stock, or they raise funds through a stock offering. A 100% debt-financing would therefore be an unusual part of an acquisition plan. Taip The above-listed reasons are all correct, and further illustrate how cut-throat business dealings can be. It is worth considering the possibility of these points being manipulated even further. When you are as large as an IBM, the market capitalization of smaller competitors is often too minuscule to be buying directly - you would setup shop in the same country, often directly across the street, and slowly suck the market share away, forcing them into a negotiation. This is why the term "competitive marketplace" holds so much truth - it is literally a place where ideas (and products) compete. If the bank failed in the 80's, make sure you have all the documentation that proves that the funds were deposited and should have been in your account. If the amount was over $100,000 then you will probably have to go to court to get it back. I would at least make an effort to seek recourse within the next 5 years. The government seizes funds from failed banks Yes, that is what seizure means. The government takes over assets and liabilities of the bank. They will attempt to sell the assets (in this case the funds that were in Escrow) and use the money to pay off the liabilities (in this case it would be putting the money back into your account). Depending on how much money there is and how many failed banks there are, it could take awhile. It could be 5 years or more. But the government doesn't care how inconvenienced you are, they are going to make whole whatever they can. In the event that the government doesn't have/can't find enough money to make whole all the liabile people, then they will prioritize based on how well organized and loud your group is. If you are a business organization, the government will treat you like a person. If you are a corporation they will treat you like a person too, even if you are as large as a state. If the funds aren't there to be distributed to depositors? In the case of Enron (and any other company that the government bailed out), didn't the government just print money to cover the liabilities? That is actually just how it works. Sometimes. Enron wasn't bank, but played the same game with accounting practices, and the same result was the outcome you are conjecturing. If the government didn't seize funds, would the bank still be open today? I don't know the answer to this, but I'd guess there would be laws passed since the late 80's regarding seizures. Also, on this site, we had a good thread on how to locate funds held by a defunct company called "Enron". It may be worth locating that if you can. Thank you for all the responses. I can't believe the government actually seizes funds! That's just insane! But I'll keep all of these responses in mind if I ever find out that my savings are in a failed bank. Also, I'll be closing my account at the other bank that also is(!) a member of FDIC. If the government seizes funds in the United States is there any point in having a bank account? Yes, to the tune of thousands of dollars every year. The government doesn't always seize funds. FDIC charges a dime a transaction limit $100 four times a day, per account. If you put in less than $440 a day, the FDIC won't cost you more than $16 a month. If you are worried about the government seizing funds, then you should probably limit your contact with the financial system. There are a lot of paperless transactions that occur these days, that involve multiple banks. You might get hit twice in the same day for the same transaction if you are not careful. FDIC won't count more than four transactions per day, but will count any transaction that occurs more than once a day. So if you put in $1,044, you will pay $16, not $44, because the 44 dollars from the two extra transactions crossed the threshold. Some banks count transactions and some count usage of the account, so be careful. FDIC counts $100 as being "usage". This account isn't designed for someone who might make a lot of transactions, but it is convenient for paying bills. The reason I mention all this is because you might decide to keep lots of funds in an account if the government seizes funds from accounts with less than $100,000 dollars in them. If you make a lot of transactions, be aware that your funds are at more risk if you keep a large amount of funds in an account. Everyone is insured for $250,000, but the government hasn't paid depositors what they were owed in decades. I don't know of any victims besides free money seekers at the government offices though. The free money seeker at the government offices probably got relatives that voted to seize funds though... The government hasn't paid depositors what they were owed in decades That's not quite true. After Enron, the FDIC did pay some depositors what they were owed. It wasn't the entire amount, and it wasn't made whole, but it did involve some funds

It's not necessary to understand the duration of a bond to understand the situation, but it's helpful. Imagine two identical bonds with the following parameters: The first bond is selling at par, so at 100 cents on the dollar. The second bond is selling at 108.25 cents on the dollar. The yield to maturity on the first bond is 3%. The coupon on the second bond is 5%. The duration of the first bond is 5 years. The duration of the second bond is 4.77 years. The duration of a bond is a measure of its sensitivity to interest rates. As interest rates fall, bonds with longer durations rise in price more than bonds with shorter durations. The converse is true when interest rates rise. The price of the first bond is going to move much more dramatically than the price of the second bond in response to changes in interest rates. At the moment, interest rates are rising. As a result, the first bond is falling in price, and the second bond is rising in price. If the interest rates stop rising, or even fall, the first bond will start rising in price while the second bond starts falling in price. This is why the first bond is currently selling at a higher yield to maturity than the second bond. The upside for the buyer is that if interest rates continue to rise, the yield on the first bond will continue to fall. The downside for the buyer is that if interest rates stop rising, or fall, the first bond will stop rising, or fall, and the investor will be stuck holding a bond that is worth less than the face value. felipe Very nice explanation Mr.Wizard. I have one more question. What would happened if the bond holder did not sell the bond at the rising rate, instead wait until the rate started to fall? I understand this is holding the bond to maturity but what is the tax implication, and is there still a better yield than the coupon yield by holding the bond to maturity? Sure, here's what would happened. The prices of bonds are inverse to interest rates. If interest rates go up, prices go down. And vice versa. With this in mind, if you bought a bond with a fixed $100 coupon, and you paid $108 for it, and interest rates fall, you will be upside down on your investment. Your profit will be the $100 - $108 = $8. If you sell the bond at a time when rates are falling, you'll have to sell it for less than you bought it, unless rates fall all the way to where you originally bought the bond. As a rule of thumb, you should only hold bonds to maturity if you are investing in bonds with par value of $10,000 or more. And even then, you should never hold a bond to maturity. If you cannot afford to tie up your money for the entire term of the bond. Because holding a bond to maturity is equivalent to taking a loss on your investment. And a loss will offset some or all of your gains in other investments for tax purposes. So if it's 20 years out, and you originally paid $108 for the bond, you would have to sell the bond for less than $108 to make a profit. If you sell in 21 years, you will have to pay capital gains tax on the $8 profit, which may be more than if you just waited 21 years for maturity. Note: if you are investing in bonds that aren't at par value, you should still never hold the bond to maturity, even if it means taking a loss, because it's likely that you won't be able to hold the bond to maturity for a number of other reasons. See the "bond math" article above. Very clear and comprehensive explanation MrWizard. I have one more question regarding this: Say the initial yield of the bond is Y1% and after X years the yield is Y2%. If I hold the bond for X years, what is the simple return / year end over yield for this period? And is there a formula for this? There is something called weighted average cost of capital, but it is accumulate after-tax profit. I want to calculate it without taxes. Also this question is open to both equity and debt.==""){ There is a simple measure, the holding period return. It's reported as an annualized simple rate, but it's not weighted. The drawback is that it doesn't take par value into account. If you're investing in bonds with par value of $10, that may not matter. If you're investing in bonds with par value of $10,000 or more, you should calculate it two ways. And a weighted average cost of capital is a good derivative, where par value is taken into account.

The dividend is whatever the board of directors of the company decides it to be. They can decide that all earnings are to be reinvested; they can decide to pay a dividend of, say, 10% of the share-price; they can decide that every share pays a dividend of so much cents per year; or they can use some complex criteria based on who owns what kind of shares, when, and from what proceeds the company can afford to pay. You (or we) cannot possibly answer that question. It would be possible if the company were to announce the dividend per share, but we don't have that. For that matter, we don't have even the identity of the company.ArrowToggle is what we call a type of cloud coverage, in this place. Please check the signature on the check. If it is that of the original authorizer of the plan account, then the check should be valid to cash. If the signature does not match, or if it is missing a needed endorsement, then you will not be able to cash it. A check is simply a negotiable instrument. It is retired by retirement of the plan, and you have no rights as to its value or validity. If you are interested in the assets of a dead co-worker, you need to follow the instructions for how to make claim to them. That will cost you some money, and may require that you take a vocational training course or two, but becoming familiar with how to make claims of this sort is a useful career skill. What does the back of the check say? There may be some information there about how to make claim to the funds. Also, if the check is issued by a company that still exists, you might try cashing it at a bank or other financial institution that also is a subsidiary or part of the same company. If the back of the check has nothing useful, and you try to cash it and the bank turns it back down, then all that you have lost is your time and some ink. I found this site which should give you some useful pointers if you do decide to seek cashing a dead person's profit sharing cheque. Good luck! If the check is made out to "beneficiaries", then it is unlikely that you can cash it, even if you are a beneficiary. To cash an individual retirement account, you would need to make sure that the check is valid and then follow the instructions for how to make claim to the funds. It may be that to make claim to the funds, you need to take certain training courses, have certain documents issued by the appropriate agency as evidence of your claim, and then you would probably have to make a claim with the appropriate agency that you are entitled to the funds. This could cost you some money, and may require that you take additional vocational training courses, but you would then be able to make claim to the funds and hopefully obtain some much needed money. If the check is not made out to one of the specific beneficiaries, then you may have to have some legal proceedings to determine who gets the funds. The costs and time involved would depend on the laws of your state, and if you are representing a company for which the original owner intended the funds, you may be able to make a case that you should receive the funds directly. If the check is for a certain amount of dollars and cents, and the check is not current, you may also have to take steps to determine who gets the funds as well. This site provides some good secure instructions on how to proceed if you can't cash the check directly. Good luck! I would advise against cashing the check. If there are formal procedures to follow in claiming the funds, and if you fail to follow them correctly, you may have to give up your claim to the funds anyway. It may be better to talk to your supervisor, or a representative of the appropriate labor union if you are in a union. If you can convince them what happened, they may be able put you through the necessary training (if any) as well as any required steps needed to make claim to the funds, and then they can write a check to you. If they are unwilling to help, a friend or significant other may be able to do the same. Note that if they write a check to you, they will likely want a copy of a recent paycheck as well as some evidence of your identity. If you don't have a friend or significant other who can do that for you, you may want to talk to a minister or religious leader; most people who have religious beliefs have some sort of affiliation with a church or religious organization, and it may be possible to get a paycheck and some evidence of your identity from them. Do not give your paycheck to anyone who calls itself a "church" or religious organization unless you are absolutely sure that it is in fact affiliated with your church or religious organization. If you are unable to find someone who can issue you a check or money order, you may want to consider setting up a small business, selling goods you produce, and ask for donations on a website. You will have to get a Tax ID, but you can then make claims of expenses and income, and as long as your income minus expenses are greater than zero, you will make a profit. If your business fails, you will still make a profit in the eyes of the IRS in that particular year, and you can cash the check and use the money to buy supplies to sell the next year. The FRA is the historic trading symbol for Frankfurt. The ETR is the symbol for the European market place. The ETR is really used in OTC markets as well, so the FRA should not be used in this context. The ETR is used in almost any market that is traded internationally. The Frankfurt stock exchange is one of the many many market participants that make up the European market place. You could argue that the OTC markets are the core of the ETR and the listed markets like the FRA are the sideshow. The listed markets are definitely less important, because most of the listed companies are large and liquid and have a lot of listed securities already. The OTC on the other hand is for small caps and illiquid securities. So the FRA and ETR are two different things. The ETR is more the market place and the FRA is the exchange. You can trade on the FRA if you only want to trade Frankfurt in general. Or you can only trade ETR securities with other market participants that have the same trading equipment and licences. In the end it does not matter much. Just use the appropriate one. If you always trade listed securities, use the ETR because almost all listed securities also trade on the ETR. Only if you also deal in OTC securities you have to switch between the FRA and ETR trading platforms. I hope this helps you and if you have further questions feel free to ask. :) Also, welcome to GTP! :) How to use ETR in your trading platform and not FRA? I mean I have seen on some forums people suggesting to always use ETR instead of FRA. One response I got when I asked this question is that ETR requires more licences to trade than the FRA. This is kind of strange as the FRA requires you to have a licence from the German authority while the ETR states that anyone is allowed to trade it (provided it is a recognised legal entity). Maybe this is outdated information though. I don't know if this is true or not, but if it is, it would mean that the ETR is not really more compliant than the FRA. If you have any insights on this please share them here. If you read the resources that I provided you will see that the ETR actually requires more compliance than the FRA. The FRA is supervised by an authority. The ETR states that it works according to the "principals of transparency, fairness and equal treatment" but does not state that it is supervised. This means that you have no recourse if you suffer losses due to the ETR. The FRA has some level of due process and you can appeal infringements of your rights. The ETR does not. I have found this site which explains some more differences between the FRA and ETR. It is in Spanish but I am sure you can translate it yourself. The site is about a Spanish broker called ABBA. The important points are: The site also says that the ETR is more expensive. I do not know if this is true or not. I also do not know if the FRA and ETR treat large and small traders differently. I have contacted the support of my trading platform (a well known retail provider in the US) and they said that they automatically trade on the ETR. They said that they are allowed to trade on the listed markets only. I find this hard to believe as I cannot find any listed securities on the Frankfurt exchange that are not also listed on the ETR. I also find it hard to believe that large market participants would only trade listed securities that are listed on the ETR only. I think my broker are just using the term ETR incorrectly. They also said that they cannot charge me more for trading on the ETR than they would charge for trading on the FRA. I find this hard to believe as the ETR should be more expensive due to the additional compliance requirements. I think my broker are just guessing in this respect. In the end I am going to contact the ETR and FRA directly and find out what they are allowed to provide to retail traders. I will post the results here once I have. I contacted the stock exchange in Frankfurt and they stated that they charge 10 € per transaction if you are an individual. They also stated that they have a "sub-broker" program. If you become a sub-broker you can charge your customers 5 € per transaction instead (apparently this is a price reduction for "large customers"). They also said that if you become a "Institutional Customer" the transactions are free. To become an institutional customer you need to have an institutional investor rating from S&P, Moody's of Fitch. You can also become an institutional customer if you have an "equivalent status" to an institutional customer as determined by the exchange. The ETR also has a "Trader's Trader" service which costs 35 € per month and allows you to make unlimited transactions for a fixed price. You can also become an "

I believe student loans are specifically for tuition, so if you're studying a course that includes a mandatory work placement, make sure it won't affect your loan eligibility as it could be seen as you're not actually studying the course. It's worth calling the Student Loans Company directly and asking them. They're pretty good at explaining whether you are eligible for a loan or not and what the criteria is like. Sure, here's what I've been able to discover: The Student Loan Program specifically excludes living expenses and other associated costs because the Student Loan Program is intended to cover only tuition costs. Furthermore, the Student Loan Program specifically does not provide financing for expenses that are covered by other forms of financial aid, such as scholarships, grants, or private education tax credits. However, if the work experience component of your course includes earning money in a separate job (for example, a student who is enrolled in a "study and work" program), this will not be covered by student loan funds. The work experience component of your course will also need to be reviewed by the Student Loans Company to make sure that it is an integral part of your studies and not simply a way to extend the length of your course. The P.C. is right in its statement that you may not deduct as an itemized deduction on Schedule A any expenses that were paid for the benefit of your business, including the home office and the improvements. The P.C. is wrong in its statement that you may not deduct as an ordinary and necessary business expense the cost of improvements made to your home. (The P.C. may be thinking of the home improvement as being an improvement to your home rather than as an improvement to an asset used in your business.) The improvements, including the floors, may be deducted as an expense when they are purchased and installed; they are assets that increase the value of the asset used in your business, the residence. Because the P.C. is wrong on one point and mostly correct on the home office deduction, I conclude that it is wrong again when it says that the home office deduction is limited to 10% of the base. The home office deduction is generally limited to 10% of the base, but there is a simplified method which requires no documentation and no percentage calculation; it is available to persons who use a separate room, or rooms, in their home exclusively for business purposes. If one room is used exclusively for business purposes, the deduction is 18% of the base. (The 18% is a carryover from the old 25% home office deduction.)NSYLVANIA Sure, but how many of us can claim a home office? Everybody we knows who reads these messages has one, but very few of us actually claim them. I can claim a home office, but I live in an apartment with no separate room that is used exclusively for my business. I have one small office upstairs which I use exclusively for my business, but it is not in my apartment. I also have one room in my apartment that I use partly for my business. I can't claim that one, of course. So I can't take the home office deduction. Would you like to know more about the home office deduction? There is more to it than meets the eye. I am not claiming the 18% as a carryover from the 25% deduction. I have never claimed the 25% deduction for my home office. I have only claimed the simplified method home office deduction. And I know this is true because I have written here on this site how to claim it every time I claimed it. (And it is rare.) When the home office deduction was changed to the current 10% deduction plus the documentation and the 25% calculation, I never wrote here how to claim it because I had never changed to using that method. I don't know how to do it. I never claimed a home office deduction before I changed my mind about home office deductions. I don't know how to document the 18% calculation from the simplified home office deduction. I have never documented the 10% deduction either. (And I never will.) When I can't claim the home office deduction, I keep track of the expenses and leave them on the books as expenses that will be deducted when I finally claim the home office deduction. They can't be deducted more than once, of course. But that's okay. It's better to wait and deduct them than to wait and not deduct them. I don't know how to document the 18% calculation from the simplified home office deduction. But that's not a problem because I never used that method. And I don't ever expect to use that method again. The office was in another building on the same property as my apartment. So I now use the simplified method again. And I will continue to use the simplified method until I move again. And after that, I don't know how I will do it. But I will bother some people on this site with it. I never claimed the 25% deduction for my home office because I never used the method to calculate it. And I won't bother documenting the 10% deduction either. It's not worth the trouble. It's better to keep the money than to waste it on accounting services. (And my accountant won't mind that I didn't deduct the expense either. He's glad that I kept good records and can deduct the expense when I finally do claim it.) Sure, I believe you. But why didn't you just wait until you moved again to set up your office in your new apartment? And claim the home office deduction for three years? Why did you move only to reconfigure your office? This does not make sense to me. I don't understand why you went through all that trouble.

When you sell something, you earn income from that. You could create a separate account for sales, but for simplicity's sake, we'll just use a general income account. When you sell something, you have an asset (the inventory) that becomes an income (you earned $100) and a liability (you owe to (or you should give to) the supplier $60). When you collect the $100 payment, you have a cash asset that grows; the income account (sales) is then totaled and reconciled later. Note that if the supplier wanted to see if anyone would pay more than $60 for the item, they would have to be given the proceeds of the sale. If they decided to keep the $100 and give the supplier $60, they would have to find someone to buy the item from them for $60. Note also that accounting does not try to maximize profits or revenues; it tries to accurately and completely record what has happened. Any vagueness here is due to ambiguity in the real world. (I've tried to keep this as simple as possible; I'm skipping over the lengthier topics of chart of accounts, asset vs liabilities, assets vs equity, income vs expenses, and the difference between financial accounting and accounting.) The entry is: disagrevate

This is oversimplified... there are thousands of companies in each S&P index component list, not just the namesake commodity. The S&P 500 is equal weighted, so each company in the SP500 index is given the same voting power as every other company in the index, regardless of its market cap or revenue or profit margin. The S&P 100 and the Mid-Cap and Small-Cap indices are also equal-weighted. The Russell 2000 is market-cap weighted. In a quiescent (low-volatility) market, the index futures tend to follow the underlying index itself when the futures are held for a short period of time. This can be observed in the grapics at [Real Money Analytics](http://realmoney.glitchedgate.com/

I don't know of any specific names for the processes that happen after expiration, but all outstanding option positions are settled by the time the stock market opens, as you mention. It's made clear in the Contracts section that on the day after expiration, the opening price of the underlying stock is the opening price, not the price at 2:59 PM on the day of expiration. The term "expired" isn't used in the sense that a stock or option goes out of style. I'd assume the vast majority of outstanding options are settled by the day's end, but maybe there's some quirk that prevents some settlement and someone would still be holding an option past expiration. I don't think there's a specific name for the process that occurs when options are settled. I don't think it's a process exactly, since it's literally what happens. Regularity of deposits into a savings account is the main point of a savings account. You can use a corkscrew or a format a few other ways. One way to format: For a corkscrew: (Year-Month-Day) dd/mm/yyyy Next Deposit: (Date) Previous Deposit: (Date) Description: (Description) Amount: (Amount) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- P.E.C. Utile Oggi Muitos Acest Yes, it is a good idea to invest in stocks. The problem is that to get decent returns, you have to be willing to assume some risk. If you put your money in low risk investments, you will get a very low return. That money will not keep up with inflation. You should at least be investing in index funds, or similar, with some modest amount of money behind each index fund. There is an entire academic study called "behavioral finance" that studies the behavior of investors as a whole. Regular investors who buy at the low points and sell at the high points and the like are referred to as "behavioral investors." They do things very differently than buy-and-hold, or buy-and-hold-forever investors. The behavioral finance studies show that most regular, non-professional investors underperform the market as a whole. They also become increasingly apathetic and emotional about their investing as they learn more about the stock market. This is very pessimism, however it does serve to illustrate that regular, non-professional investors cannot outsmart the market as a whole. "Buy low, sell high" is great, but who can do that? A lot of people think they can, and a lot of people are very good at it. But the buy-and-hold, or buy-and-hold-forever methods do not require you to make these judgements. You simply buy when there is the least emotional impulse behavior involved (January) and sell when it is least emotional (before you know what the year-end return is). Keep in mind, these methods do not require you to make buy or sell judgements, you simply buy a stock or index fund at its inception, and nobody including the professionals, including the wise-guys, can time the market. It is however possible to hold what you invest in for the long term with these methods, without letting your emotions color your judgement. The "efficient market hypothesis" is the basis for the last statement above. It states that the stock market is a "ГЛАC", which means "completely irrational." This means that it is unpredictable, and that it is not possible to gain any sort of an edge in the market, whether through emotion, religion, or otherwise. The corkscrew method and the last method above do not rely on the efficient market hypothesis, however. Sure, it is a good idea for you to invest in stocks now, even if it is just a few dollars a month. It is good for you to get in the game now so that you can build up some momentum, and so that you can see what the market does and learn to not let your emotional reactions to the market dictate your investing decisions. You can use the last method (buy-and-hold, or buy-and-hold-forever) without relying on the efficient market hypothesis, however it does not hurt to do the things listed below, even if you are using the last method. (This means you don't have to worry about timing your investments, which is impossible, but you do have to worry about making a solid plan and sticking to it.) - Create a corkscrew or a format that works for you and your family. This means: - Create a spreadsheet so that you can easily keep track of what you have invested in. - Decide in advance the format for your deposits, and stick to it. This means you don't have to think about it every time you make a deposit. - Decide in advance the end goal(s) of your savings, and every milestone between there and there, and track your savings against those goals. - Decide in advance the minimum amount of money you will have coming into the fund each month, and every year, until the fund is created. - Decide in advance what the first goal is of your fund is, and the goals thereafter. This will help you to track your success. - Decide in advance what you are going to save the money for (and make sure it is something that will exist when you get there, i.e. don't save money for a college that is not there yet). - Decide in advance what the "exit criteria" are for each goal. This means: - How much money do we need to have in place to replace whatever thing that the savings are for? - How much money do we need to have in place to run our business? - At what

To suit all property types, including commercial, the following is a well-diversified portfolio: 80% - invested in shares via ETFs or similar 10% - Cash 10% - Property Rather than having a direct holding in the shares, I would recommend a minimum of 50% via index funds. The 50% minimum via index funds allows for the greatest possible diversification. Even 70% via index funds is achievable, however I would not recommend going below 50%. Sure, here is what a well diversified portfolio would look like: 4% - Cash 6% - Share Funds 50% - Share Index Funds 35% - Property I recommend a minimum of 35% property because property can make up a significant portion of a portfolio, and if you have a high risk tolerance, this is too much exposure to one asset class. By having a mix of shares and property, you can reduce your risk by 45% by having either shares or property suffering a catastrophic loss, but this is where the share index funds really shine, as they reduce risk by 70% by itself. Even with a catastrophic year for shares and property, your portfolio will not suffer a loss (providing you buy at the lower of the share funds or property in each respective case). Note: I have a higher than usual recommended allocation for cash due to the extreme volatility of share funds. I would expect that if you have a low risk tolerance, you would also have a low liquidity requirement, so why take the risk of share funds if you are likely to panic at the first sign of a downturn? I have also included a small amount of direct property investment, which is an alternative to being invested in shares or share funds. There are many reasons why you may want to own a direct property investment, including: 1. You have a higher liquidity requirement than others 2. You have a higher risk tolerance 3. You feel that a downturn in shares and/or property is imminent and you do not trust the investment management industry to appropriately allocate funds in a downturn 4. You simply feel uncomfortable with a high level of diversificationたたみ Note: This is just one example of a well-diversified portfolio. There are many other combinations that are equally well-diversified. The important thing is to ensure that you have a low enough risk tolerance for you personally. For more information on risk tolerance see here. The problem is that you are looking at it from an investment-type-perspective rather than a personal-financial-planning type-perspective. For most people, the best investment is their home. They own it, they rent it, or they are paying it off. Renting your home has been proven time and again to be the best investment strategy. Any examples that you read that suggest it is better to invest in shares or bonds have not taken the tax implications, transaction costs, and changing circumstances of a changing housing market into account. From here: "Homeowners pay off their mortgages slowly, each year they pay thousands more into their mortgages than they would if they rented, and they get nothing in return for this money beyond a sense of satisfaction." While this money is being "wasted" on the mortgage, it is nonetheless making you richer. You have more net worth because of it. You are not building equity in your home because you are paying it off, but you are still getting the tax benefits of a mortgage. Two points here: 1. You still get the tax benefits of a mortgage even if you are not building equity because you are paying off your mortgage. 2. This is a rather subjective question, but let's give some ideas; in the US, you could check out the Fidelity Freedom Funds http://www.fidelity.com/fdrs/gestion/ppr/ gug_freedom_funds.html These funds charge a 3% trail commission, with a 0.35% expense ratio. The S&P 500 index fund at Fidelity costs 0.25% and doesn't have a trail commission. I'd park your money in Vanguard's S&P 500 index fund if you want a low-cost way to track the market. Freedom funds, or ones like Fidelity Freedom 1, 3 & 5 Year funds, add a tarnished-bell-shaped bump to the market returns if you hold the fund for the recommended time period. Check out "The ETF Answer Book" by Jason Bobbin. It's a pretty comprehensive resource on ETFs and index funds. According to Kip Anderson of Abnormal Returns fame, Fidelity's 5-Year Freedom fund family has "proved to be some of the worst investment vehicles one could choose." Apparently they have "significantly underperformed pre-tax (net of trail commissions) a broad array of index funds..." Anderson is talking about the 5-Year Freedom fund, which charges a 1% trail commission, and has a 0.55% expense ratio. Taking leverage, or buying the 5 Freedom funds with the 10- and 20-Year Freedom funds, doesn't make much difference, according to Mr. Anderson. I don't know of any index funds that duplicate the returns of the Freedom funds, but you could ask your broker for a free prospectus and a hard copy of the portfolio of any index funds with similar expense ratios. You'd be shocked at how many index funds follow a blended approach, and have significant holdings in international stocks, and non-U.S. dollar currencies, and derivatives. You'd also be shocked at how many U.S. equity index funds are blended, with "value" or "small-cap" strategies, for example. You'd also probably be shocked at the quality of the management teams, and how they don't match the descriptions of "fundamental" stock funds with "passive" index fund names. Thanks for the responses. I know this thread is old but I wanted to ask one more question. Would the blended approach and non-US holdings be more properly handled in a separate account, such as a brokerage account, rather than as a 401(k) investment? The reason I ask is because my 401(k) has a menu of index funds, blended funds, and stock/bond funds, but no separate listings of "passive" index funds. It does, however, have some rather expensive blended funds (0.75% expense ratio, yikes!) but no blended funds with a "small cap" or "value" designation, or a "mixed" designation, etc. So a blended fund with a 0.75% expense ratio but no small cap or value designation would not be a good buy, but a 0.55% blended fund with a small-cap or value designation would also be a bad buy, so it must be that the blended small-cap funds are being used to offset the 401(k)'s lack of a small-cap index fund, or something along those lines, I would think. But I digress... Anyway, my 401(k) does not have any "passive" index funds, so if I want to hold an S&P 500 index fund, I would have to buy one of the expensive blended funds, or I would have to move my 401(k) assets to a brokerage account. Is it better to buy one of the expensive blended funds, or to move one's 401(k) assets to a brokerage account? The brokerage account would surely have lower fees but the convenience of the 401(k) enrollment, automatic payroll deduction, etc. I have some large automatic deductions set up that would switch to a different fund if I changed to a brokerage account, and I don't really want to mess with that. But perhaps it's worth it to save money to be able to choose from the full range of index and blended funds offered by one's 401(k)?
