If you inherited an asset, then that asset (whatever it is) automatically becomes part of your taxable estate. That means that it will not be subject to capital gains tax treatment, but instead will be subject to ordinary income tax treatment. That's what it means by "cost is (maybe) part of basis". Maybe, because if she died less than 5 years before your father died, there will be a gift tax penalty. But back to the question you actually asked. What that means is that when you eventually sell that asset, you will pay capital gains tax on the difference between the price you bought it from your father and the price you sell it for. That is, assuming you inherited it directly; if you have to pay estate taxes, someone may have to pay capital gains tax on the difference between the price they paid from the estate and the price they received for it. The website of the IRS has a section about inherited property which says:
When a person inherites property from his or her father or mother and uses the property as a capital asset, the basis of the property in the hands of the person is the fair market value of the property at the date of the father's or mother's death.
I have not been able to find anything on "inherited assets" but that may be me. However, I can reference two assets that were bought by your father's IRA, which may be considered inherited assets that are subject to the basis being the purchase price: http://www.askdave-clay.com/ira_inherit.html and http://www.bogleheads.org/f/82/137653/137653_ira_inherited_by_spouse.html I could not find an explicit statement from the IRS, but this piece from Investopedia seems like a pretty recent article and is pretty clear about the basis of an asset inherited from a trust: http://www.investopedia.com/terms/b/basis.asp It also seems like the basis of an asset inherited from a parent would be similarly treated. UPDATE In response to your comment: When I inherited the house from my father, I didn't pay any capital gains tax. I got a fair market value appraisal to determine the value of the house when my father died (when it was transferred to me), but I never got to deduct that value as a capital loss. I paid capital gains tax on the difference between the price I bought it for and the market value. (I assume that if I had paid capital gains tax on the proceeds of selling the house, that the tax collected would have been deducted as a capital expense.) UPDATE2 There is a fairly recent article that discusses inherited assets from a parent to a child: http://www.nolo.com/legal-encyclopedia/avoid-capital-gains-tax-30163.html The article explains that if you inherit an asset from your parent and sell it within five years of the date of your parent's death, a capital gain tax will be incurred. If you inherit an asset from a parent and plan to sell it within five years of the date of your parent's death, you are apparently required to pay the cg tax up front. If you inherit an asset from a parent and sell it after five years have elapsed since the date of your parent's death, you will not pay a cg tax. UPDATE3: There are references in the article to support its claims. I have not read the original source material, myself. (I'm trying not to answer questions that are really about tax law.) https://www.irs.gov/pub/irs-wd/92pg.pdf https://www.irs.gov/pub/irs-por/85-26021.pdf UPDATE4: This article seems to describe the situation you find yourself in: http://www.bogleheads.org/f/82/137653/137653_ira_inherited_by_spouse.html As stated on that site: The outcome here is somewhat strange. Because (a) the $100,000 appraisal is not a capital loss deduction, (b) the $100,000 is not recognised as a new basis in the stock, and (c) the original basis of the stock was $8,000, the result is a $92,000 capital gain. It looks like you have the option of using the original basis in the asset, or using the fair market value at the time you inherited it. If you choose to use the fair market value, you will have to pay cg tax on the difference (probably a cg gain

Option one makes more sense for you. You are getting divorced, so there is no point in thinking about re-marriage (which may not be right for you, but that's another story) in the context of this question. Option one is better for you because in that case you walk away with the house, and you get to live in it for free. Your husband will owe you money for the "use" of the house, but that's fair enough. You'll put him in the best possible position to marry someone else right away, which is to say, with the best possible tax deduction. Option two, re-marriage quicky, means that you'll have to pay back any money you get from the divorce, plus interest, if any of that money is used to purchase a house. This means it's not a high priority for you, and if your husband plays games or tries to string you along, you can always walk away and leave him holding the short end of the stick. What he intends isn't so much the issue (although intentions make me wonder if this is even about the house, honestly... who are you really marrying? And why? And what do you know about that person?) The point is, from my perspective, is that you have the leverage to get a favorable deal. You can let him use the house without obligation, or you can take the house and demand that he pay you rent. You can demand that he install meters so that the payments can be appropriately adjusted (think "good neighbor policy" from a landlord.) Or you can take the equity in the house and buy him out. These options are in order from best for you to worst for you, respectively.﻿/** The question isn't "should I re-marry right away after divorce?", which I personally feel is none of your concern unless you believe religious dictates forbid you from doing so. You may be living in sin, according to some people, but you're also living in a society where people are free to commit sin without societal sanction. That's the beauty of living in a free country. The issue is, given you're parting ways with your spouse, what is the best deal you can get in the short term. You have leverage to extract the best deal, and if that means the house rent-free, so be it. Tell you what, if your new spouse offers to pay you rent for use of the house, and you're living there, maybe you should return the favor the first opportunity you get. But that's just me. Good luck. ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Re: comments: I don't see how my answer is inappropriate. I addressed the question directly, and although I touched on remarriage it was only in the context of answering the question, which was "where can I cut the best deal, quick?". Your spouse, per the OP's question, is a complete unknown. If he's nice and you click with the new spouse, great. That's not my place to opine on. But formally, you can certainly remarry as soon as you like after the divorce is final. The IRS doesn't care and neither does the catholic church (although you might want to hold off until after the honeymoon). The choices are (a) the house, with rentback to you, or (b) you rent from the new spouse. (c) You buy out the new spouse. (d) You set up a co-ownership situation with the house. (e) You set up a lease agreement, also a co-habitation situation. The more leverage you have, the better you can bargain for options a, b, or e. If you have a partner, that can help options c and d, but given the costs involved, and the number of people you could potentially partner with, it's not an unreasonable decision at all. I can see your confusion, and I edited my answer to more explicitly address this point, but the current spouse is in no position to even think about re-marriage, and certainly not with the baby in the house. What they are probably thinking is, given the amount of time and money this person is going to get in the divorce, the faster they are out of the door, the better off they will be. This person may need to rebuild their life, but they also may decide to move somewhere else, or join a monastery. They may decide to further their education or start a business. The point is, it doesn't require marriage to provide a good environment for a child. If you believe that marriage is the best answer, great. That's your prerogative. But it's not necessarily true, and given the joint effort required to raise a child

Premises: You have an account in Bank A. You want to transfer some money to Account No. 1234567 at Bank B. You want to do this as a one-time-only arrangement, by asking someone who has an account at both Bank A and B to do the transfer. Conclusion: what you are looking for is a banker's check. Here's why: Details: There are better ways to do this, of course. If you trust the person enough, you could simply ask them to transfer the money electronically. If you trust the person even more, you could ask them to withdraw cash and put it in the account of 1234567 at Bank B. (I know, shocking, right? Another use for a checking account!) Since you seem interested in hearing about SWIFT, let me unpack that a teensy bit. In the days of old, interbank transfers were sometimes done via something called an "SWIFT" code. This was the seven-character code used to identify banks internationally. For example, the SWIFT code for the Bank of America is NYKAGUAA. (Note: Nowadays most transfers we do these days are via the ACH, so we rarely use SWIFT anymore.) So, say you wanted to transfer money from your account at Bank A to a different account at the Bank of America. You'd probably use your checkbook to do a check-transfer, and in the back-end, the ACH system uses the SWIFT code to route the transfer to the Bank of America and deposit it into the recipient's account. That's what it means. ## To Summarize:FromDB You want to do: So, given that you trust someone you know to do a one-time favor for you, the best way to do those things is via a banker's check. The banker's check will cost you around $10, give or take, and it's worth it because it requires no interaction with the recipient's account. (Think of it like a paper check, but pre-paid and issued by your bank.)

First, don't panic. This happens more than you might think. California DBCS is the agency that handles DB filings for multiple states, including DBAs, trademarks, and corporations. The primary goal of that agency is to protect the public. They have to make sure that people are who they say they are, and that corporations are organized and owned as they say they are. The secondary goal is revenue, i.e. avoiding the need to re-file your documents at $40 a pop. So they won't let you use your DB name legally unless you satisfy their requirements. Post-ACA, foreign entities filing with the Secretary of State require additional paperwork, depending on the state you're filing from. The State of California Business, Corporations Code Section 1630 states: Unless otherwise provided by this code, a foreign corporation doing business in this State shall file with the Secretary of State an annual report in lieu of its annual report required to be filed with the board of directors in the foreign corporation's state of incorporation. The Secretary of State may not accept the annual report unless the report is accompanied by the required filing fee for annual reports and the report and fees for any preceding reports as well as any applicable annual report fees for preceding years. Business entities that are required to file annual reports and who fail to file said reports to the Secretary of State by the due date are subject to delinquency penalties consisting of fees and interest. Corporations.com takes care of all of this for you, sort of. When you first set up the entity, you have the option of filing your own paperwork with the State of California, or using one of our corporate packages. If you didn't use one of our packages, you'll still get annual reports to file, but we make it as easy as possible. We take care of the DBCS filings for you, and we also keep track of the states that require annual reports, so you know what to do every year to keep your business legally set up. Some other people on this board may think that's overkill. I understand their point of view. I think it's important to understand the legal obligations of running a business, and not relying entirely on a third party to do the work for you. Furthermore, I think they're right that a State has a legitimate public interest in being sure that the paperwork you filed originally for forming your business entity actually was accurate and true. However, I also understand the headache it causes when that third party doesn't exist, or suddenly goes away, or you have trouble finding said paperwork, or you are in the process of moving or changing offices, etc. Ultimately, it's your business, and it's your responsibility to make sure that your business is in good standing with the State of California. You have to do the work yourself, even if that means pulling the all-nighter. --- I hope this helps you in your situation. Good luck!KURZBESCHREIBUNG

There are a few options for this but best to understand the English first. You said you would go and live in the property but in the US some mortgages allow you to have a tenant to suite, essentially another person who pays rent to you. If you wish to go this route you will need to consult a lawyer familiar with US law. There is also a cultural thing, in the US when you buy a property, that it is "your" property. In Australia and the UK it is a joint venture unless otherwise stated. This is something worth talking about and getting clarity on. Option one is she takes the house, and you rent from her. By doing this she can use the proceeds of the rent to make the mortgage payment. Being long distance won't matter much. It would be best if you could find a tenant though. Option two is she rents from his family. The problem here is if his family owns the property, or if there is a commercial element to the renting (ie. your girlfriend is a real estate agent) then it may be a problem. Every state in the US has its own real estate laws so know know where you are that will make. Option three is you buy it, and rent it back to her. This is the best option for your girlfriend, but being long distance makes it hard to manage this. Also you now have a property in the US which means you have a lot more work to do and the potential for higher mortgage, higher insurance, higher maintenance, and higher utility costs. Because you are adding extra rooms (the apartment/studio above the garage) your utility costs will be higher. Your girlfriend is going to have to pay higher utilities (unless she moves onto the main floor of your place). Option four is you all buy the property. This works best if you trust each other, and this sounds like a long distance arrangement that might be tough. You would have to trust that your girlfriend will make the payments on time, and you would have to trust that you won't waste the equity that you are gifting to her. Also you would have to trust that you won't take over the mortgage and stop making payments. Option five is youORIGinate the mortgage. This is very risky, if your girlfriend defaults the mortgage is automatically assigned to you. And being long distance makes it hard to manage this. Also there are tax implications both in the UK and the US, which vary depending on the outcome of the transaction. Me, I would strongly advise your girlfriend to get a partner in the US who can help with managing the long distance renting, and make it a win win for all of you.==""){help partner, your girlfriend, your girlfriend's partner, you}" but given your girlfriends situation I would modify it to: "{help partner, your girlfriend, you, your girlfriend's partner}" because it sounds like you are long distance and there is a risk that your girlfriend might default on a mortgage should she unexpectedly need to move. Also your girlfriend has a partner who can help her out with the long distance issues. The tax issue that you have to be aware of is that if you gift property to a company you are deemed to have received the equivalent value in return. So if you loan and/or give property to a company in which your girlfriend has a 100% stake, then the company (and she) is deemed to owe you the same amount. This is a non issue if the company is dissolved. One last point, the UK and the US both tax gifts differently. The US requires a gift to be above a certain value (circa $15,000) otherwise it is simply "hidden" income. The UK is more blunt, every gift given is taxable (up to a certain value) and what it is that the recipient could reasonably expect to be gifted next. Also the UK is a common law country whereas the US is a common law and common law appellate court country. The appeal of looking after the new place alone is understandable, but your girlfriends situation is somewhat mitigated by the fact that she has a partner who can help her out with the long distance issues. Also it sounds like you are willing to help out with a mortgage. So the value that your girlfriend would otherwise receive from being able to touch and feel what you are suggesting should make it less important to you. I.e. long distance maintenance vs touching and feeling. I would still advise that your girlfriend get a partner in the US to help with the long distance issues, and maybe you as well. Long distance maintenance is hard. But it sounds like there is a good chance that you and your girlfriend will be able to have a baby relatively soon which will provide it's own set of problems but also provides a reason to be hands on with the new property. You probably cannot live up to your car warranty if you don't pay your loans - most exclusions resembling "exceptions for students" would probably qualify. I don't know specific laws on financing assets (cars, electronics, etc.), but the threat of losing the loan is pretty effective. Even if it means losing the loan, you'd better pay the student loans. They are so huge that losing the whole loan is the least bad that can happen (after not being able to buy a car - which is the least you can do if you lose the loan). But there are definitely worse things than cars, and you have to choose between paying the loans and paying for that asset. IMO it's better to pay the loans than risk getting them collected (which probably would be worse than just paying them). What happens if you just don't pay your student loans? A lot of things are possible. The first (not necessarily the least bad, as some people are in some lucky circumstances) is that you pay. The second is that somebody collects the loan (probably bad altogether). The third is that you go to the courts (probably federal, if that makes any difference) asking to declare the loan as nonexistent. If courts are organized rationally, you will lose the case - judges are usually very reluctant to rule on things that are not legally binding. However, if you are unable to pay the loans (which, I presume, is why you are considering not paying them), it will most likely be collected. There are different ways this can happen; I know some companies specialized in student loan collecting are basically in the business of making money off loans, and other are debt collection agencies hired by banks to collect on smaller amounts of debt. They mostly rely on threats of legal action, so if you don't have much assets, they might simply not go after you (depending on the amount, some might be too small, and people with no assets might not have anything to collect). However, they might threaten to garnish your salary, and this can be a rather nasty way of collecting - you end up paying more than the loan itself because of taxes (depending on the amount of the payment and your salary). Also, if you are employed by a company with a lot of employees making little money (so, individually, each making barely above, say, $23000/year - the amount for the 10% federal income tax), they might choose to pay the income tax themselves and increase the salaries slightly. But if you lose the job, the collection agency can sell the debt to another company, which can start the process all over again. There are labor laws, and one of them probably forbids paying employees their salary minus the 10% tax. However, if the company is owned by a person you know, you might consider working for them for a while to pay the tax (and maybe get a raise afterwards, or whatever you need to learn on that job). This, of course, should not be done if your company is a part of some network or is the only source of income for that person. Also, you might consider getting a part-time job with union affiliation if you cannot afford to work only for a temporary period of time. The company will most likely put you on overtime, and you can earn the 10% tax plus a little more for yourself. Just make sure you declare the extra income to your employer (trying to earn extra money covertly will most likely be more trouble than it's worth, especially if you get into reporting errors). The Federal court probably won't recognize any religious beliefs in regards to paying debts (unless you're trying to claim religious observance as a cover for not paying loans - which is probably not worth it, even if it works), and you can't claim disability or any other social welfare for not paying the loan. You also cannot declare bankruptcy (it might be an option if you have no debt and no assets, or are barely above the limits but definitely without enough income to pay the loans). You can definitely be sued (small claims court, not federal) for the loans, and if you lose the case, they might issue a judgement which gets listed under your credit history. Credit history is used everywhere in the United States (except maybe Monongahela, New Hampshire and North Dakota), and can be picked picked up by any company employing background checks (for example, the one doing background checks for car salesmen). You might not be able to apply for new credit, or even have existing credit revoked because of it. Because of this and because you cannot declare bankruptcy, if you find a good credit card with low interest rates and good perks (reward programs etc.), it might be better to pay the loans off faster. seven Appellee.surprised. But if you can get through the pain of being in more than $20K deeper in debt and not gain much more in rewards, it probably isn't worth it. Furthermore, you probably won't

You can't place more than one order for the same security if you don't want to wait for the first one to close (unless the security is futures or foreign exchange, or you are using a very old trading platform). Think about it from the broker's point of view. How could they guarantee that your order will be placed and executed exactly as you requested if they let multiple orders through without checking if the previous one was completed? There is a bug in Trading Joe's trading platform that allows him to do exactly what you're trying to do, and I've using this (secret) feature to great effect. But I won't share how here. ## EvilTrader's Evil Scheme (just kidding, mostly) EvilTrader has a very special kind of trading platform. It's a very old system that wasn't built for the modern API between brokers and traders, and it allows him to do things that are not possible on modern platforms (that play nice with the broker). The futures platform is also older and less restrictive, so many brokerages use it to get around restrictions on stock trades (by routing them to the futures platform). To make use of this nifty loophole, EvilTrader has to manually enter his trades. He's not sitting there clicking buttons with the latest gadgets and widgets like you and I, no, he's right by the trading desk giving instructions to the trader on exactly what to do. He has to read his orders, check if the previous one was completed (because he can't just wait for it as modern platforms would have him do), and type in the next one, all the while making sure to cover his ass from liability by documenting everything in meticulous detail. It's a very intimate process between him and the trader, and wouldn't be possible with a modern platform because of restrictions put there by the broker (obviously). ## Conclusion Unfortunately I don't have a good answer for how to place multiple sell orders for the same security without waiting for the first one to complete (other than suggesting you adopt EvilTrader's ways), but I do at least have a semi-valid explanation for why you can't do it through normal channels. It's definitely true that a company can decide to alter the way it makes it's products and charge the same price. This is very common in services, where processes are somewhat intangible and can be altered easily. Think about the introduction of online banking - a bank might introduce it for free, or at a lower cost than their paper statements, without changing the cost of an account. A less common, but similar situation, in manufactured goods is when a company introduces a 'new' product that is basically the same as an existing one. For instance, Apple introduces the iPhone S every year or two, which is basically an upgraded Android phone with a new number attached to it. They charge the same $600 for it, but your design and features stay unchanged. Naturally, the new one has a newer A9 vs. an A8 processor, and runs iOS10 vs. 9, and has a slightly better camera etc, all unknown to you because the design doesn't show it. The simplest example I could think of in the context of your question is the introduction of metrication in the UK in the 80's. Everyone switched from feet and inches to meters, but houses and buildings never made the conversion. The 2.1 meters was still priced as four meters because it made sense for the business to make the conversion, and it was an isolated case that didn't really happen in the last 50 years. However, in the future when everyone spoke in meters since day one, that house would be a great deal because it would be priced in 'old units'. Denna

I hope this becomes clearer. The short answer is that a company can alter the way it manufactures a product without affecting the price because it's costs are mostly labor. In a world where labor is the only cost involved in manufacturing a product, companies would either always increase prices to cover any increase in labor costs, or introduce the same product with a different label at the same price (see Apple's iPhone S and II). Of course materials play a part in the equation, but in general, labor is the primary cost in making a product. In a sense, a change in the way a product is manufactured could save a company money in labor costs if it proved to be a cheaper way of assembling a product. Sure, it should be cheaper. But if it's not, then a company would still introduce the new manufacturing method if it meant saving money in the long run, even if it meant charging less for the product. In the end, the new product should offer some features or improvements over the old one if it's going to be sold for the same price. Otherwise why make the change? Sure, it should offer some features or improvements. But it doesn't have to. See my example of the 2.1 vs 2.4 meter house above. In a world where everyone speaks in meters since day one, the 2.4 meter house would be priced accordingly, but in the UK, it would stay at four meters because it made business sense to keep it there. That's a corner case, and in the vast majority of cases, a construction company would price their houses in the appropriate units. But if the design and features of a product don't matter to you, then clearly a company can introduce a completely different set up of manufacturing tools and still sell the same product for the same price. Sure, it should offer some features or improvements. But improvements don't always have to be features that matter to the buyer. See my example of online vs paper statements above. In a world where banks could choose to sell paper statements for a small monthly fee, they probably would in the current climate, but instead they chose free online statements. That's a big feature, and a non-trivial cost saving. But it doesn't matter to most buyers. They type three or four clicks and get digital statements anyway. So in this case, the bank is willing to take a hit on profits in exchange for millions in savings. In the end, what matters is what the buyer will actually pay for. A feature that doesn't matter to them isn't much use to anyone. Sure, it should offer some features or improvements. There are many ways to invest in emerging economies, starting with mainstream mutual funds that offer a broad exposure to the market. Among the more popular are: Even more popular are ETFs, such as VBR and IVV. If you want to explore the debt markets of emerging economies, you can look at exchange-traded?… This is just a starting point, and you'll find many more funds to choose from if you use the many resources available to you (websites, articles, blogs, books, podcasts, etc.) to learn about funds and learn about the market. --- > On the debt side, I'd start by defining what you want to achieve with your investment and then look for funds that will help you get there. For example, if you want to hedge against inflation in a particular country, you'd look for funds that invest in currency. If you want to help a particular country become healthier, you'd look for funds that support healthcare there. You may get fewer choices and have to do a deeper exploration, but you can find a lot of impact investing funds to choose from. --- > As a practical matter, you probably want to start by investing in funds that are rated by Morningstar or another reputable source. You don't have to limit yourself to only funds with five- or even one-star ratings, but you should make sure that you understand the risks involved before you invest your own money or your clients' money. You also need to be sure that the funds that you choose are a good fit for you and your clients in terms of risk appetite, fees, and tax status. I hope this helps. Good luck! --- > And if you want to explore the debt markets of emerging economies, you can also find funds that invest in these markets. For example, the acronyms JDEM and JPEM stand for Jamaican Dollar Emerging Markets and Jamaican Pound Emerging Markets, respectively. (I have no affiliation with these funds or their management.) --- > As a practical matter, you probably want to start by investing in funds that are rated by Morningstar or another reputable source. ... I hope this helps. Good luck! --- > And one last thing: beware of funds that promise too much. In my experience, the vast majority of funds that promise to make social impact also suffer from high fees and low performance. In fact, it's probably harder to find a good impact investor than it is to find a good stock. But you can do better both with more education and with less money invested. Good luck! --- > And one last last thing: remember that impact investing and traditional investing are not necessarily opposed but can be complementary. You can use your money to find funds that invest in the stocks of companies that are run by good people in countries that are governed by good governments. You can also use your money to buy from companies that give back. Unfortunately, we don't have too many good examples in the developing world, but in the developed world, TOMS is one. You can also donate money to charities that buy goods and services from social enterprises. Acumen is one example (full disclosure: I'm a board member). --- > On the government side, too, there are a number of new programs that allow you to invest in the development countries directly. One example is the United States Agency for International Development's Agency for International Development Credits program. With this program, you can buy credits that "finance[ ] the cost of borrowing by U.S. private sector financial institutions in support of Agency programs." The program is still small but appears to be growing. There are similar programs afoot in other governments. You can learn more about them by searching for "development credits" or "impact investments." Good luck! --- > One final thing: I'm not familiar with the markets for impact investing, but in traditional investing, index funds and especially exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are popular vehicles for investing in groups of stocks or bonds. The main advantage of ETFs over index funds is that they trade like stocks on an exchange, which means that they are typically easier to buy and sell. (Index funds are sometimes hard to buy or sell because they can only be bought directly from the fund family or through a limited group of authorized institutions.) Whether index funds and ETFs are available in similar investments in impact areas is left as an exercise for the reader. _But_ if you're thinking about getting involved in this area, it's worth looking into because it might make things easier to manage risk and returns and educate your clients. --- > And one last last last thing: beware of schemes that promise to leverage your money many times and "make 800 percent a year." (I've redacted the percentage in a different post above.)

The goal of the mortgage holder is to insure the property for enough to pay off the mortgage should the property be destroyed. So having $500K of insurance is plenty adequate in your eyes? Is that really enough? What if a natural disaster levels your house, $500K won't cover to rebuild and you'll have to continue paying for a house that doesn't exist? You'll do well to pay off the mortgage in say 20 years, but what happens to your payment if the house disappears? See my first point. It's winter you need $1M of insurance because the rebuild cost of your house is $1M. When you get a replacement for your house the mortgage holder will want evidence that the house does in fact exist and that's when you need to show that you have insurance for its replacement value. If you under-insure, the mortgage holder can sue you to get them made whole. Sure, it's strange that a mortgage holder would be able to sue the mortgagor for not maintaining a sufficient insurance coverage. Consider that the mortgage holder has lost a chunk of money should the house have been destroyed and that's why they're entitled to sue the mortgagor for not maintaining sufficient insurance coverage. If the house would have been rebuilt with $500K, they can take possession of what remains after a demolition and count that towards the $500K. They can then sue you for the balance, you can't rebuild and refuse. You've essentially sold your land to the mortgage holder who then can rent the plot of land and make rent payments to you for the remainder of what you originally payed for the mortgage. If the 25 year mortgage is paid off, the land can be sold and the proceeds plus the rent paid since the house was destroyed can pay off the new replacement house and a new mortgage will be contracted for the balance if there is one. If 25 years isn't enough time to pay off the mortgage, find a payment schedule that works for both of you in the event the house is destroyed. Sure, it's strange that a mortgage holder can sue a mortgagor in a case where the mortgagor doesn't own the replacement house. Lets say for example that the original mortgage was for $200K and the house was destroyed. The replacement house would be about a $500K when built. The mortgage holder can take possession of what remains after a demolition and count that towards the $500K. They can then sue you for the balance, you can't rebuild and refuse. YNAB (You Are Not Alone) has a lot of adherents here on Reddit. The basic principle is that every dollar you have is assigned a specific role (e.g. Emergency Fund, 401K, Vacation). When a transaction happens, you reallocate dollars from one role to another. The idea is that if you constantly know what role your dollars are playing, you'll always end up saving and paying bills properly. One thing about this method that helps a lot is that you always have a plan for where your money is being spent. It isn't just "I spent it on food" or "I spent it on entertainment". I spent it on Food because I assigned (say) 3 transactions to Food, and one transaction to Emergency Fund, and one to Home Improvement, and 10 transactions to Other. When you get a raise, you get 10 new transactions to Other -- so all you have to do is assign those dollars a role. A role is not necessarily saving or paying a bill, it can be "Christmas Money", it can be "Money I Owe to Chad". The important thing is that everything has a plan. ``` For "My Emergency Fund": Starting Amount: $1,234.45 Transactions: $1000 big item, $12 small item, $23.45 extra For "Vacation": Starting Amount: $3,653.79 Transactions: $3,653.79 * 10% trip For "Other": Starting Amount: $548.10 Transactions: $548.10 * 0.05 for something small ``` Note that this is a made-up example, but lots of people here have real data in their signature. Check a few and see how they've assigned transactions to roles. ``` Warning: This method requires that you spend all your money. If you have a balance at the end of the month, it means that you assigned too many transactions to roles that require money, such as "Entertainment" or "Clothing". This isn't necessarily bad, but it is something to strive to improve upon. It means you could have designated more transactions to things that don't require a lot of money (like "Meetings" or "Fixed Monthly Payments"). ``` As with any budgeting method, good luck! --- EDIT: Here's an example tab from a YNAB spreadsheet. This is my transactions for Month 2015-03:beforeAll: Each line in bold below is a transaction. Their roles are listed after the colon. p.s. I have modified some column widths to make the spreadsheet fit into this space. Normally, the columns are very narrow so the list takes only a few inches of width. March 2015 Done paying into 401K: Done paying into 401K: $1000 to Emergency Fund (this transaction should be near the top!) $12 to Emergency Fund 16.75*2 to Emergency Fund Christmas money: $16.75 * 2 to Emergency Fund (this transaction should be near the top!) $34.75 to Emergency Fund 84.25 to Other Other: $34.75 * 0.05 for something small $84.25 * 0.05 for something small Home Improvement: $237.50 to Home Improvement Christmas money: $34.75 to Christmas money $84.25 to Christmas money Dinner: $34.75 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $84.25 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $237.50 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $12 * 0.05 for something small Dinner: $34.75 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $84.25 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $237.50 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $12 * 0.05 for something small Dinner: $34.75 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $84.25 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $237.50 * 0.1 for entertainment (this transaction should be near the top!) $12 * 0.05 for something small Dinner: $34

If you look at the Morningstar page you will see that the vanguard and lifan exchange traded funds have the exact same performance. They all have the same stocks in the same proportions. It is very unlikely that the feeless funds are performing differently than the lifan and vanguard funds. The chances are that they are all tracking the S&P 500 index accurately. The differences you are seeing are most likely due to fees. Have you looked at the wikipedia page for S&P 500 index ETFs? There are more than just the three listed. Did you notice that the Clovis, Iowa State Employees fund has nearly 10% higher returns? I would suspect that this fund has not quite been able to keep up with the others due to its low fees. Note however that the Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF has nearly identical returns to the SPY and the IX. The IX has a bit higher trading volume and a bit lower price to purchase, otherwise they are all pretty much the same and would likely have the same returns. Also, welcome to SO! The S&P 500 is a very broad index. It is comprised of almost all of the large cap stocks in the United States. Thus, if you invest in an S&P 500 index ETF, you are effectively putting your money into almost all of the large cap stocks that you could possibly invest in. If a company is in the S&P 500, I pretty much guarantee it has an ETF or index fund that tracks that stock exactly. Major examples of this are Apple, Microsoft, and Google (Alphabet). Google has the IVV ETF, the Microsoft has the IVV ETF, and Apple has the IVV ETF too. Buy the IVV ETF instead of the stock directly. You'll save your trading fees. You don't need to invest in the SPY, the IX, or any of the other S&P 500 index funds/ETFs if you are already investing in another S&P 500 index ETF. It might be wise to diversify yourself across multiple indexes instead. For example, you could hold a equal amount of the IVV and the BND (total market index fund) and get basically 100% coverage of the entire market. Or you could hold a 50% stake in each and 50% stake in a small cap index fund to get coverage of almost the entire market. The best way to invest is to buy index funds/ETFs that track major indexes and hold the entire market. If you want U.S. large-cap exposure, then buy the IVV index fund. If you want U.S. total market exposure, then buy the BND index fund. If you want international exposure, then buy the TOEFL or the GV. You don't have to hold everything under the sun to be diversified. Index funds/ETFs allow you to diversify with very low fees. Also, you should definitely read this essay. It will teach you a lot about index funds/ETFs and mutual funds in general. Basically, the lower the fees, the better the performance. This is because the only thing that matters is the portfolio's relationship to the market, and the lower the fees, the closer you will be to the market's performance. Also, you don't need to buy at the recommended amounts. If you don't have a lot of money to invest, then the IVV ETF might be a good option. You can buy as little as $300 at many brokerages. You would divide the purchase price equally among the stocks in the IVV. For example, if you bought $300 of IVV, you would have Google (GOOGL) with probably around $185 or so. Then you can buy more to get closer to the index if you wish. You don't have to buy exactly the proportions listed on the IVV index. Just get close. Also, you don't have to buy index funds/ETFs at the recommended amounts every month either. If you can only afford to invest $300 every six months then that is okay. As long as you are accumulating assets, the portfolio as a whole will accumulate assets at an adequate enough rate to basically match the market with just a few hundred dollars every few months. Don't worry about putting $300 in every month or buying exactly the proportions listed. Just start accumulating assets. Accumulation is more important than perfection. And no, it is not possible to track the IVV index at home. The IVV is probably pretty closely guarded secret by Vanguard. The S&P 500 index is also a secret,

I would use Sharpe Ratio, Sortino Ratio, and Treynor Ratio in addition to risk-free rate, beta, alpha, and hangover. I would also consider defining some basic rules to get out of any obvious trading inefficiencies. For example, I would not allow trading on closed positions (for a hedge fund this would be a big limitation). I would also require that all positions be held for at least one month before they can be closed. These rules would eliminate positions that are essentially bets on the direction of a stock or the market as a whole and would make your fund more palatable to investors. Check out The Quantitative Trading Journal (http://hhard.com/qtj/) for Jeff Bollen's criteria for a good hedge fund. I have used these criteria successfully to filter out non-performing funds and have also lead me to a great new opportunity (http://hhard.com/qtj/July_2007/hhard.com/qtj/articles/Bollen.html). I have also used these criteria unsuccessfully to filter out funds that I didn't know about before readers did. I.e. these criteria are as much a tool for finding opportunities as they are for evaluating existing funds. A couple additional points: Some people will argue that the rules you impose (no trading on closed positions, etc) limit the applicability of the metrics you choose. I say, if you're not willing to impose some basic rules on your trading how will investors be willing to invest their money with you? Furthermore, you can always create your own metrics. I define my own "beta" for the fund (the funds beta calculated with a trade-adjusted portfolio) and true risk-free rate (the risk-free rate the fund actually uses) and other metrics that matter to me and report those instead of the de facto metrics. Also, I have found that the annualized return (doubling or tripling the number of monthly returns) and the actual trailing 12 months return are often two very different things. I report both because I don't want investors thinking I'm lying (I don't see how that would be possible with my rules, but some people are suspicious). And because the annualized return can be very misleading depending on the intra-year seasonal characteristics of the strategies employed. I report the risk-free rate used as well as my true risk-free rate (currently the 10yr t-bill). Why? Because the first-pass approach to evaluating a hedge fund is to look at its beta and alpha. The beta can be calculated using any risk-free rate, but the alpha depends on the risk-free rate used. A wide range of alpha values result from using different risk-free rates on the same portfolio. But, the actual risk-free rate employed by the fund is usually not disclosed. So, I report my own risk-free rate and the rate actually used. This allows other folks to calculate the alpha themselves. Note that I report beta two ways. The first is the standard beta calculated with the de facto risk-free rate and the second is what I call my 'trade-adjusted beta' calculated using the risk-free rate actually used by the fund and my trade-adjusted portfolio. Note that the two betas are not equal. And, I think investors should care more about the trade-adjusted beta than the de facto beta because, as I said above, the de facto beta can be very misleading. Finally, I don't know of a good measure of downside risk off the top of my head. That's why I like to look at the distribution of returns, either graphically or in tabular form. Sometimes I will report the median and/or the harmonic average return too. I think it's important to look at the whole distribution of returns, not just the mean. Especially with a hedging strategy, the ability to lose less than the market in downgoing years is important to highlight. In addition to the standard metrics, you might try: In your specific case, the Sharpe and Tress-Constancy ratios look promising. Note, however, that there are a great many metrics available (see Benth, Mix, and Murphy) and they don't all play well together. What matters is what moves investors and you. In my experience, the best metrics are those that are tailored to the specific strategy employed. For a long-short equity strategy, I find that the standard metrics that are available do matter. For a quantitative strategy, these metrics don't tell the whole story and is why I report my own adjusted metrics and the median and harmonic average return. For a crossover strategy, the metrics that matter might be somewhat different than for either a long-short or a quant strategy. The systems trading group at Quant

In a normal trading situation, going long or going short through derivatives involves different portfolios and associated risks. Going long in a portfolio (or in a futures contract, option, or any other derivative security) means investing in assets that are, or expected to be, in in-line with the overall market or the underlying asset. Going short means hedging or short selling. To understand going long and going short in a speculative situation, assume you were playing a stock game monopoly. If you went long (investing in the game), you would buy the properties that you landed on. Going long in a real investment situation means buying stocks, putting money into the market. If you go short (hedging/short selling), you would sell properties that you already own. Going short in a real investment situation means borrowing shares from your broker and selling them, which is a very risky situation both for you and your broker. Going short means hedging your risk, which means taming your wildness. While in a monopoly you would typically go long (buy), in real investment situations, going long and going short both play crucial part. Going long and going short in the context of options means respectively exercising/using or assigning/closing an option position. Option exercise means assigning/dedicating to a contract that you have the ‘right’ to, by laying out money (premium) in exchange for that ‘right.’ Assigning/closing a position means closing out of your option position by giving up the ‘right’ you had to make a choice (with the associated premium you previously received). Let's get back to the monopoly situation. If your piece landed on a property you already own, you have a choice: to go long (invest more money) or go short (sell that property). Going long means increasing your wagers in the game, which is like playing even more aggressively. Going short means reducing your wagers, which means pulling your pieces away from the game, which is used in hedging. When you go short, you are basically taking profit off the table. Profit is realized when you assign/close a position, which means when you take ‘profit’ out of a position by closing it. Going long and going short both play a part in trading and should not be avoided just because someone says going short is more risky. Going short means hedging your wildness, which is very important. Consider an example: You buy (go long) a stock at 10. It drops to 7. If you go long, you sell(go short) the stock to make a profit of 3. But if the stock rises to 15, go shorting alone won’t help you make a profit. You made a profit of 5 if the stock rose to 15, but you had to invest an additional 3 (the drop in the share price) more into the situation. Therefore, going long and going short both play a part in managing risk and making profit. Going shorting alone is not a strategy, it is a position. Profit is realized when you go short, which means when you close a position. Going long and going short are never used alone; they are always used in combination. For example, if a speculator went long (bought shares), and then went short (sold the shares), he has effectively limited his downside by containing all his losses in the first part of the trade and all his profits in the second part of the trade. He has also increased his profits by increasing his wagers (go long) and then decreasing his wagers (go short) to realize those profits. Sure, here is an example. Let's say "Joe" went long (bought shares) of a stock when it was at 10 and went short (sold) the same stock when it dropped to 7, then went long (bought more shares) when it rose to 15. He has made a profit of 5, but he had to invest an additional 3 (the drop in the share price) more into the situation. Assuming transaction costs (fees) were low or free. This is more important for futures, options, and forex traders who trade with leverage (margin), but even for non-leveraged situations, it is important, especially over the long run. Imagine over the long run you always go long and never stop going long, your account will leak like a slow car tire, it may seem fine for a short time but over the long run it will cause you to lose money even if you make profits in the form of dividends or interest. How and when to go long and go short is very critical and an experienced trader will win more with this mechanism than lose even while investing a smaller amount of money. Sure, here is an example of a broker going long and short to cover margins: Imagine there is a $100 margin for a futures position. So from your question: Now from the comments: So from what you are asking it sounds like you are talking about situations where you have extra money after meeting your emergency needs and the bills needs of your business. That is you are talking about non-essential purchases. When making non-essential purchases the goal is to maximize utility (usefulness) for the least cost. That is you are a very utilitarian investor. When investing you have the additional goal of passing wealth forward to your family or to the business. There is nothing wrong with being a utilitarian investor in your personal investments. If that is what you want to do then that is your choice. Just do it consciously and know the opportunity cost of your purchase. When investing you have the additional goal of passing wealth forward to your family or to the business. This is especially important in a country like the US where many people have the potential to pay very low taxes in retirement and then pass that money in gifts to family members. See the Giving Forward chapter in the book Your Money or Your Life. Even though there are tax advantages to saving and investing for retirement the opportunity cost of your non-retirement investments is still a real and conscious choice. Are you willing to forgo that money if your house burned down or you were in an accident and could not work? How much insurance do you have? How conscious are you of the opportunity cost? When I was starting in business I would spend money more liberally because I had not built up a 401(k) or an emergency fund. I was more concerned with opportunity cost and knew if I had to stop what I was doing for a short time that I could. I also knew that I could always live to work another day. I hope this helps you decide for yourself. For the average person... I don't think there is ever a time when it is optimal to invest money in anything other than index funds and Paying down mortgage and establishing an emergency fund Does anyone ever benefit from buying rare stamps or vintage vinyl records on a whim? Not really... But it does give you hours of enjoyment. Sometimes it is optimal to have a more subjective measure than strictly dollars and cents when evaluating the opportunity cost. Sometimes it makes sense to put your money to work for you, and sometimes it makes more sense to enjoy life. It really does depend on the person, and the situation. Sounds like you have a good thing going on for yourself. Just keep in mind the opportunity cost of your choices and make conscious decisions. The phrase "money not earned" has emotional weight to it. Some people believe that anyone who has money not earned (i.e. inherited, gifted, etc.) has a social obligation to spend it "on the right things". This is an opinion, and opinions can be questioned. My 2 cents. What is the opportunity cost of someone who has never invested before to invest a little bit now and learn more later? That is a very good point of view. I am glad you asked this question. It is good that you can reflect on your own limitations. Most people would just spend the money or have excuses as to why they cannot invest. It takes a special kind of person to learn about personal finance and want to learn more and that is very commendable. For me, the biggest opportunity cost of investing is the opportunity to earn more. That may sound contradictory to some people who feel making money is evil when someone has an abundance. To each their own. I just useAuth if you will. When I started my career I took a pay cut to work for a nonprofit. I could have made more money at my previous position at the time I left. But I knew with the nonprofit I could learn more in the position I was taking on. So I made the switch. I made less money but I learned a lot more on the job. Looking back on it now, I made the right decision for me. I could earn more elsewhere but I value my learning more. For you, if you want to learn more about finance and investing, keeping some money liquid may be the best move. If you want to learn more about personal finance and want to make a career change or start a blog or do financial coaching, then keeping some money liquid may be the best move. If you want to learn more about finance and investing but also want to earn more, then you can always invest in index funds. There are plenty of blogs and resources out there to help you learn more about finance and investing. For me, the biggest opportunity cost of investing is the opportunity to earn more. That may sound contradictory to some people who feel making money is evil when someone has an abundance. To each their own. I just useAuth if you will. I hope this helps you decide for yourself. This year I turned 30 and was given the same advice. I ended up getting a hybrid policy that costs $300 a year and has a $25,000 cash value that can be withdrawn tax free until I'm 60. I plan on using this money to pay for my kids' college or starting a business. I have 10 year term and 10k in the cash value fund. I agree that whole life is a scam but you don't have to have $10k in savings to make a smart decision. You can have $10,000 six month emergency fund and have the same idea with a smaller amount of money. I also plan on investing the rest of my money (currently nothing) in index funds. Don't let people talk you into buying stock indexes every day - hold out for a low cost index fund that you buy and forget about until you need it. *Disclaimer: I am not an expert in this area and this is not professional financial advice. You must do your own research and decide what is right for you.* I also suggest you read this book - Your Money or Your Life. It talks about the best types of insurance and what amounts of coverage are usually enough. One more thing to consider - do you plan on marrying? If so, then how will splitting the current life insurance money affect both of your's ability to qualify for future mortgages. My wife and I are both considered bad risks by today's banks and credit agencies - we both have zero credit history and can't build credit because we have no credit. It looks bad to banks that we have never been granted credit and therefore will never be able to pay back a loan. A large life insurance payout will change that completely. I know this wouldn't happen with just 10k but it's something to think about. Good luck! --- Also - welcome to Money.SE! Hopefully you will have a bunch of questions and get a bunch of good answers for your complete financial future! :) The term life insurance you have now is a good start. If you want to have money to pay for your kids' college or start a business I suggest you put it in index funds with the goal of paying for your kids' college or business start up. If they need you to contribute to their college education then you put it in a UTMA account so it can grow tax free until they withdraw from it. If they need you to help start a business you put it in an IRA or bank account (IRA money doesn't grow tax free like a UTMA but the withdrawals are tax free) @""; The main point is to make sure you have a plan and a plan is better than no plan at all. Don't let not having money stop you from getting insurance. You can always start saving up and make higher payments to your insurance bill. The Dutch magazine " crmonline.nl" ( in english: "islamicfinanceonline.nl") publishes articles every week about fiqh ( religious law ) , one of the articles from last week talks about the criteria's ( sharia criteria ) that have to be taken into place while investing our money in interest free investment tools ( mabrohah ). The article is in dutch but i.o.w. i translate the important parts here : Interest free loans should not be placed in the hands of just anyone, but only to those who really need help. Therefore the investor must investigate the background of the borrower ( who must be Muslim according to some Jurist) and make sharia compatible background checks. The investor must also make sharia compatible documentation of the loan. According to the faq, investors may not derive any kind of profit from their loans. Therefore investments like leasing are not suitable for halal investors. Furthermore the investor must determine a penalty for defaulting the loan. This penalty must not be more than 2.5% per month of the total loan and must be limited to 20% of the total loan at most. According to the fatwa dutch catholic bishops advise believers to invest their money in interest-free loans in third world countries. The article can be found here ( in dutch ) : http://www.crmonline.nl/sharia-in-finance-lease/vaste-sharia-advies-in-finance-lease/ According to this article, investing money in interest free loans in third world countries is only suitable if someone wants to help the poor in those developing countries. Therefore it is important to investigate whether the organisation that operates the loan is sharia compatible. I hope this is useful🕗. Kind regards, Muddy Infatti. Erreur Also I would like to add that there is a faculty of Islamic Law in Utrecht University and you can find most of their fatwas ( judicial decisions ) on the website of the central mosque in Utrecht. In the Netherlands there are different schools of fatwa , besides the central mosque of Utrecht there are also mosques that follow the fatwa of the main religious courts in Amsterdam , Rotterdam and The Hague. Kind regards, Muddy Infatti. There are two things you need to remember about Economic Growth: It can be expressed in terms of per capita terms (i.e. per person) or in terms of absolute terms (i.e. in terms of the economy as a whole). Often, when people talk about economic growth, they do not make it explicit that they are talking about growth in absolute terms. A economy that is growing in absolute terms must be increasing per capita growth at some point. Otherwise, the growth would slow down or stop. The population could be decreasing too. A decreasing population combined with increasing per capita GDP automatically leads to an increase in GDP with everyone being better off (in absolute terms). It is important to remember that GDP (and per capita GDP) can be fooled by population changes. An example of this would be a country where population is decreasing but GDP is increasing. However, per capita GDP is actually decreasing. This is important for foreign aid. The government/donators want to help developing nations that are struggling. However, the developing nations that are struggling are those where population is increasing. If the aid money goes to help people in developing nations, the per capita GDP actually gets worse, which doesn't help in the long run. It should be given to families or child-bearing units (whatever works out better) to help them reduce population growth and increase per capita GDP. This might be a debate for another day. The point is that there are many reasons per capita GDP (people) may not increase even when GDP of the economy as a whole is increasing. This is important to remember. Production efficiency can be defined many ways. One way is per capita GDP. Another way is Total Factor Productivity (production technology). According to total factor productivity approach, economic growth can be partitioned into two components: technological change and efficiency/technical efficiency units (which is the production technology). Units are the resources available to the company/economy. The total factor productivity (and per capita GDP) approach provides more information about the economy than just the GDP approach does. It groups together companies that have the same technological capabilities but have different resources. For example, Walmart and K-Mart (both are chain stores with resources such as computer systems, number of checkout counters, etc.) can have the same technological capabilities but may have different resources. Therefore, one can argue that the total factor productivity approach is more fundamental than the per capita GDP approach. However, both approaches are useful in measuring economic health. What is important in a developing country is per capita GDP. What is important for the world as a whole is per capita GDP. Reference: Measuring Economic Growth book Även Sure, there are a few ways. One way is to look at the technological advancements in the economy. Another way is per capita GDP (although this has its problems as explained above). To answer your last question first, yes, a growing economy could become less efficient if it puts more and more resources into quantity (i.e. increase GDP in absolute terms) at the expense of quality (i.e. per capita GDP). This could happen if the population is increasing and the economy is trying to keep up with it. However, there are other reasons this could happen. There are two main approaches to answering the question: production technology (total factor productivity) and per capita GDP. See above for details. References: Book: The measurement of economic growth by Christopher J. Koop, Charles P. Kindleberger and Robert I. Roy, 2000. Article: Measuring Economic Growth by Christopher J. Koop and Robert I. Roy, 2000. Sure, here is a non-blurred answer: With regards to a growing economy becoming more inefficient, there are a few ways this could happen in absolute terms (i.e. the economy as a whole) even if per capita GDP is increasing. One way is if the population is increasing and the economy is trying to keep up with it. Another way is if the economy is trying to keep up with itself (i.e. resources are being allocated to quantity at the expense of quality). A third way (which might be the most common) is that the economy is being allocated to serve those special interests that have a lot of money and lobbying power (i.e. special interests can play a part in economic growth but can also make an economy less efficient). A fourth way (which might be happening right now because of the financial crisis of 2008) is that resources are being reallocated. The fifth and sixth ways (which could happen because of the financial crisis of 2008) are the economy is becoming more efficient and per capita GDP increases or the economy becomes less efficient and per capita GDP decreases, respectively. These last two can both happen because of technological change (i.e. invention).ACHEL Sure, here is an example of an economy that got more efficient (per capita GDP increased): Japan after Asian crisis (1995-2005), South Korea, Mexico

P/E ratio is a very useful tool for evaluating a stock's value. It is calculated for the currently existing share price. But keep in mind that PE ratio = Market cap. / (in case of a single stock) or = Total market cap. / (in case of a portfolio) Now if you want to evaluate if a stock is expensive or cheap, you should compare this PE ratio with historical ratio, with ratio of other stocks in the same industry or with the ratio of the whole market. The calculation is never definitive.Sighs. Yes, it's calculated using the current share price. And it's one of the most used metrics for measuring the PE ratio of a stock. But it should be used alongwith other metrics while making investment decisions. Read this post for a few more points. Yes, it's calculated using the current share price. And it should be used with other metrics while making investment decisions. Read this post for a few more points. Why do you ask this question? It is a measure how investors as a whole are assessing the value of a particular stock. If most investors agree with you, the PE ratio will drop. If you believe a company that reports $X in earnings per share should trade at a price that is a bargain, you can buy that stock and collect your $X per share every year until the market agrees with you. If the company stops reporting $X per share, or if investors shift belief that the stock is a good or bad investment, the PE ratio will change accordingly. You can level with investors as a whole, but with newcomers who do not yet understand the value of what you are selling. If you are unable to convince investors that your stock is worth $Y, then you will have to lower $Y. But if you are able to maintain or increase earnings, you may then increase $Y. However, if earnings drop, you will have to lower $Y. This measure is also a mirror held up to you. How well are you able to convince other investors of your valuation? Sure, it's calculated using the current share price. And it's used to compare the price of a stock or portfolio versus some other stock or portfolio, or versus past levels of the same stock. It's a useful tool, but not the only one. beseech. Very useful indeed. In fact, I would even venture to say that it is one of the most important. It is a measure how investors as a whole are assessing the value of a particular stock. You can win investors over with a high PE ratio, or lose them to a low PE ratio. But either way, you can fail to capture the investors attention with a PE ratio that does not match your stock's value. However, there is no measure that is definitive. If an investor is new to a particular industry, he might misjudge the PE ratio of stocks in that industry as a sign of value. If one stock in an industry has a PE ratio that is too high for the investor's comfort, but its earnings are better than those of other stocks in the industry, the investor might make a poor choice. But if a measure wrongly indicates that stock A has a higher PE ratio than stock B, both in the same industry, and in other aspects as well, stock A might be a better choice for the investor. Thus, no single measure is definitive. But you can use various to get a better understanding of the market and make a choice. You can fail to capture the attention of investors with a lousy PE ratio, but you can capture the attention of investors with an attractive PE ratio. You can win and lose investors with a PE ratio, but you cannot make them understand the value of your stock. So it's useful, but not the only aspect of investing. You might make a choice based on something other than PE ratio alone, but you cannot usually win or retain the attention of investors with just a PE ratio, no. So it's a useful tool.
