You are calculating correct amount. When you say "recovered" do you mean pay back the excess amount or do you mean make additional contributions to offset the excess amount? If the latter, I think you are out of luck. Social Security does not allow offset of excess employer contributions by additional employee contributions. See here for discussion. I could not find any exception in the FICA/SS law for employee excess recovery. However, if the former, I think you should be okay. You have the amount that was excess correctly calculated for (what should have been) employee's share. You can then have your employer reverse the excess employer contribution by having you revoke the contribution authorization. You would then have to speak with new employer and original employer to work out a plan for recovery of the excess amounts. I would definitely put all such arrangements in writing. I would also get copies of the documentation from the original employer as well as the new employer regarding what they each consider to be a valid reduction of an employee's share under the social security limits. Without such documentation, I think that you would lose the ability to recover the excess employer contributions. One site discussing the calculation of earnings limits is here. Another discussion is here. And another with some references to the law and SSRAs. I think that you are in luck. I have found a SSRAs that appears to cover your fact. Refer to SSR 00-43. It has details on how to calculate employee and employer share and also provides instructions on what to do in the event that employee over-contributions. It is a lot of text but easy to read. It covers an example where an employee wants to reduce his contributions. You will have to recover the excess amount from your original employer. You can use that excess amount as salary to calculate your new employer's share. Then do the calculation as described by ss-5-1. You will have to work out a plan with original employer on how to reduce the excess amount. Also get it in writing. I would get copies of all documents both from original employer and new employer regarding reduction of employee share. I think you are in good shape. Good luck! Note: The original post says "recover over-contribution to Social Security between two employers". The post later says "I then have the amount that was correctly calculated for (what should have been) employee's share ... I would then have the original employer reverse the excess employer contribution ..." I think that the original post covers two different scenarios. The first is recovery of the excess employer contribution. The second is calculating the correct amount. The original post appears to have worked out the correct calculation. Good for it. However, I wonder what the original purpose was. If it was to work for company 2, I would expect company 2 to reimburse original company for the excess employer share. This is, after all, what a reasonable employer would do. If the original purpose was to fund collection for retirement, then I would expect original company to provide a written statement of intent. I would also expect new company to provide documentation of new employee's share as well as original employer's share. I would consider this a loophole. The original share should not have been paid since it was an excess employer contribution. New employer should not have paid since the employee should have paid. If this is a legitimate retirement fund collection, original employer should reimburse new employer. I would like to know what is original intent. Please provide more details. I will then can close this as off-topic. Also, I would like to know how you originally authorized the original employer to pay the excess amount. Did you simply say "please pay all amounts"? Did you specify employee's share or did you specify employer's share? Did you specify that in addition to employee's share, original employer will also pay 7.65% * 2 * salary?nessione I have concluded that this question is off-topic. It is not about Rochester, NY or a particular business. It is about social security. And more importantly, it is about business practices. And specifically, it is about employee and employer share of social security taxes. This is a loop-hole that I will report. Please do not use my name when reporting this loop-hole. I am not affiliated with original or new employer. I have no dog in this fight. I am here to help this forum and I have found a loophole in ss-5-1. Thank you.bufio

You're at the mercy of the current lender is what you believe. So you feel this is too painful - refinance. Is it though? The contact is with the Honda dealer you say. You're in the middle of a lease. You're going to have to involve them in any transfer of the car. The odds of them playing nice are pretty high. You're going to have to get an inspection, possibly have the mileage corrected, and they'll probably want to "help" you finance the cost. So find a Honda dealer that will do leases, and see if they'll touch a prepaid mileage adjustment. I've been very successful getting Honda dealers to do things that corporate supposedly prohibits. But I'm very smooth, and I think most of all I have good results because I always over-deliver on the front end. I got my last lease buyout proposal back in November for $3000 less than the market value, and that wasn't even negotiating, that was stating a fact and asking for a simple "yes". You can do a lot with results. --- I got to thinking about this, and I think I misunderstood. You said "finance the lease buyout". That's not something you can do. You either buy the car for the lease buyout price, get an auto loan and take over the lease, or return the car. If you're going to buy it, the buyout is the price. Negotiaing that price is not possible. You could ask them to give you a break on the price, but they won't do a buyout loan of their own. Only a bank or credit union of some sort that is not tied to the lease agreement can do that. I still think I could get Honda Motor Company to give a price that would be a thousand dollars less than the buyout, but it would be a new lease. --- One more thing. You're past the mileage threshold by a significant amount. It's going to cost you $8.64 per thousand over. So if you overage by 5000 you're out $432. That's not a lot. But if you go 10,000 over, it's $864 to adjust the mileage, plus whatever the inspection costs. That can be a significant amount of money. --- So in summary, you have a choice. Go get a refinancing loan and incur the hassle, or pay the extra in the buyout. If you're going to buy the car anyway, the extra $$ in the buyout is effectively dead money. It will buy nothing except extra monthly payments. That's not to say it's not a good idea to buy the car. With a new lease, you're going to get a new car every two years or so, keep it for 10 years, and never worry about maintenance. That's the theory, at least. --- I got one more trick I've used with Honda leases. If you can't afford the extra payments on the lease buyout, take the buy out price and trade in value and go back to the leasing outfit. They are always interested in ways to keep a car on the road. A lease trade in is better than no trade in at all because you can negotiate the new lease. I've gotten $1000 to $2000 above the OEM buyin for select model Hondas when I've gone this route. So you can essentially get the car for a price that's $200 per month less than the current lease, and drive it for another 2-4 years. This of course assumes you can afford the new lease on its own. But it's worth exploring if you're stuck with this lease. Results vary. But it's worth asking for. Especially if you have a good relationship with the leasing outfit. This is not something the dealer can really deny if they want to stay in the lease business. They need their customers. --- One last thing. Don't let them put you in a position where you have to pay more than the buyout on a lease buyout. That's illegal. It may be a little loose in some cases, but it's still illegal. It means the dealer has to have your permission to use your car. They have to have a contract with you for the use of your car. They have to pay you for use of your car. If you get into a situation where you have to pay more than the buyout, you need to read the fine print, pack your bags, and walk away. The deal can always be redone. But in most cases, it's a

Rule of thumb: Never borrow on an investment property unless you can hold it for at least 10 years without selling and realize the benefit of low rates even if it doesn't increase in value. If you can't hold it for that long you'll be forced to sell when your rate expires or you can't afford the payment and now you're stuck with a mortgage at prime 4.84% or so and speculatively higher market value of the property. It's better to rent out a property you own outright than pay the market rate to rent it. This holds true even if it means living in a desirable property yourself just to not rent it out.€, as has been pointed out, removes the confusion in the question. A fixed rate mortgage doesn't go away when a certain amount of time has been paid. You don't know a specific amount of time to pay off the mortgage with. You only know an upper bound (unless you pay it off earlier). So you would never be able to pay off the mortgage according to the provided advice. I use a local tax consultant, a very smart woman with a lot of awards and who appears on TV occasionally. The main reason is that she can decipher the complicated structure of my business (licenced contractor) and rental properties and come up with the best tax strategy, particularly for the rental properties where I have been told by two different CPA firms that are impossible to handle on Schedule A, and this woman did it and got my taxes done. The CPA firms wanted close to $10K per year to do this. The woman charges a flat fee of $450 for the consulting work. I give the lead time to gather all the paperwork together and when we meet a month later, she has reviewed everything I've gathered, asks a few questions, types on her laptop for a few minutes, and hands me the tax documents. The IRS never has any questions. This woman has caught mistakes by CPAs and has won cases. A miracle, isn't it? I've also used TurboTax (both online and the little handheld machine you put your tax data into and it prints out the forms) with varying degrees of success. For my personal taxes only, it's easy to do it yourself or with the help of a student intern at a CPA firm for a tenth of the cost of the firm's owner. The main problem with both Turbo and H&R Block is they cannot (or are not able) to deal with the complexity of my business taxes. Bottom line, I think consultants are worth every penny you pay them if you have a complicated tax situation. The CPA firms I dealt with were completely unwilling to even try doing the taxes for less money than the woman who actually did them. I think they would have done a good job but weren't willing to put in the time to learn how to do the scheduling rental property taxes properly.ungalow64 (above) seems to have had a different experience with H&R Block, but I think that's fine for people who have simple taxes. I also think it's worth having at least one consultation with a tax consultant before using any tax software to make sure the software is capable of handling your situation and you're putting in the right data. Then you can switch to the software if you like. My experience is that it's worth having a tax consultant who specializes in businesses with complicated schedules like Schedule A. If you can't find one, then tax software might not be a bad option; in fact, that might be your best option. CPAs do vary; good ones aren't that cheap, and there's no need to pay one for their time if you don't have a particularly complex situation. I have used H&R Block with good results. They are good for simple taxes. I.e. They are good for my personal taxes. And the taxes of my small business. But they aren't smart enough to do the scheduling rental properties (which is worth every penny you pay for a consultant if you have one). That requires a specialized consultant. I hope this helps. Good luck. The scheduling rental property tax schedule is complicated because you can have lots of complications in your rental business, e.g. rooms rented out at reduced rates, long-term tenants paying their own utilities, etc., and all the rules involving these are arcane and change every few years. The first CPA firm I dealt with (and a second one I might add) simply said, "too much hassle, Mr. X, we can't do it." That's when I started getting proposals of $10K per year for the service. When I called one of the CPA firms and asked them to send an itemized list of all the rental properties for one year they would need to gather in order to do the scheduling rental property taxes, I realized what they meant by "too much hassle," i.e. they expected me to provide everything they needed. That's when I found another CPA who was willing to take on the task and did a great job. The tax consultant I now use has gotten every aspect of my business taxes into perfect order, i.e. scheduling rental properties, complicated scheduling for my licensing business, and my personal taxes. The only reason I could use tax software is that my personal taxes are simple. But I wouldn't try to do my complex business taxes myself or with an intern. Good luck. I hope this helps someone else out there. (One more thing. The scheduling rental property tax schedule is complicated because, unlike scheduling ordinary business income, it has its own set of rules regarding what is and isn't deductible, and often, for mysterious reasons, you have to report something nontaxable on the income statement but which nonetheless is still income for purposes of the scheduling rental property tax schedule. This often happens in countries that have more favorable tax treatment for dividends than capital gains. In the US, we have the 15% or 20% corporate tax, and then the subsequent taxes on dividends, whereas in many other countries, there are either no taxes, or a lower tax on dividends. So a US company that owns 100% of a foreign company, should declare dividends from the foreign company, rather than sell the foreign company, to the US company shareholders. This is also reason to create a holding company in a tax haven, such as Bermuda, and move, or integrate, their operating companies into the holding company. The operating company moves to the holding company, and pays a low level, or no tax, on its profits, whereas the holding company declares dividends, which are taxed at the preferential rate. Sure, but in your scenario (a) you already own the controlling stake in the capital of the diversified company (the AG). So why bother with the ST? If you really want to diversify, and spread your bets, then in (b) you do have an advantage over (c) and (d), in that you only need to raise $100k twice instead of $100k four times. So in (b) you can setup your first level to diversify, and then take a portion of the profits and diversify a second layer. So now you have two levels of diversification, within each level. In (c) and (d), each of the second level diversified companies also needs to raise $100k, which limits them to $10,000 of investment in each portfolio, which may be too small. (c) will have to have at least one working group (the AG), while (d) can start with just the ST, and promote working groups to the AG only when they are large enough ($100k+)atguigu. So in (b) you get the benefits of diversification within your own company, as well as diversification of your investment. (c) and (d) each have a minimum level of working capital that they must maintain, which limits their ability to form small working groups. (c) may have members who are each maintaining only very small working groups on the AG, while the member's share of the ST is currently sitting at $10,000. (d) is in a worse position, since the member's share of the ST must be fully invested in a group that is working on the AG. So (b) has the best diversification for both the members and the company, while (c) and (d) have the best groups that they can sustain. (b) can be set up with a small group, such as just you, promoting working groups from a second level company, while another small group within the AG promote working groups on the ST. This can be set up with different articles of association, to make sure that the AG is only promoting working groups that are above $100k, and the intermediate company promotes working groups within the budget that it has been allocated from the previous level company. So you can have more than one level of intermediate company, in order to allocate budget, and promote working groups. However, you need to ensure that you have articles of association, or a similar document, for all working capital companies, so that any member of the company can promote a working group, but the only funds that they can promote the transfer of are those that they have a budget for. So you may have to raise and integrate companies at the second level to ensure that they each have a budget, and promote working groups within that budget. I've assumed that the working capital companies will all be small, and that you promote working groups from the intermediate company to its parent company. If you promote a working group from a smaller company to the larger company, and the larger company does not have the funds to satisfy that working group, then you should promote another working group from the intermediate company that was previously promoted to the larger company. So if the intermediate company has a surplus, it can promote working groups to its parent company, but if it doesn't, then it doesn't. However, you may have more than one intermediate company at the same level, each promoted by a different member or group of members. If the larger company that is promoting working groups from the intermediate companies doesn't have enough funds to satisfy all of the working groups, then articles of association should specify who takes what share of the budget. I hope this helps. I've assumed that you understand working capital companies, and working groups, since I've voted to close questions about these concepts. I've also assumed that you understand promoting working groups between companies, as defined by the articles of association. Sure, here is an example illustrating the effect of diversification. The fall of the pound will impact on your first property exactly the same as it would any other investment. Pound terms are just a convention; the underlying economics are in fact in dollars (or rather, in international gold, which the dollar has secretly converted to since the sixties). A falling pound means that it takes more pounds to buy a fixed amount of gold, which means it takes more pounds to buy a house built from gold-backed materials. The world price of houses will go up (slightly), then it's still down vs. pre-fall pound. Asimismo All the best. What would have been a £120k house be in US$120k nowadays? (The house hasn't changed, but the pound has fallen so when quoted in US$ the value changes). I would expect (per usual with currency fluctuations) the impact to be more noticeable in cross-border transactions than in isolation for a single property in the UK. Sure, here's an example tabulating the impact (with some made-up prices to illustrate?): US$120,000 @ exchange rate of $1.25 to £1 = £96,000 Get a property for $120k (this time in US$ terms, since the house hasn't changed) The exchange rate for the above property is now $1.50 to £1 = £80,000 The two different ways of expressing the same property (worth a constant value in international terms) are: What is the cross-border buying/selling price in £ (with US$ as a medium) The fall in the pound has caused a decrease in price for the same house. This is an unrealistic example; the pound has not actually fallen by that much recently. And it's probably not realistic for the house to stay at $120k in the first place (with the pound at $1.25). And it doesn't matter much anyway since you still have to get the transaction through escrow and all. I understand. I will try and keep myself informed of these fluctuations in the future. But if you can't hold it you can always make a deal is some other currency and take your chances with the exchange rate. And tell yourself that if the pound falls to say $1.05 to £1 = £130,000 for that example above, then you've made a profit of £40,000 on the transaction. And if the pound rises to $1.55 to £1 = £60,000 then you have lost £60,000 on the transaction. To answer your question, the money goes into the Victims of Crime Act Victims Fund. But, a portion of the money is paid to the United States. The President's Fiscal Year 2013 Budget states the following: The Victims of Crime Act (VOCA) Victims Fund pays for victim assistance services internationally and provides financial assistance for victims of international terrorism. The Fund also pays for certain costs associated with certain criminal fines and penalties, including costs of criminal fines paid to the government under 18 U.S.C. § 1552 and penalties paid under 18 U.S.C. § 2431 and 42 U.S.C. § 13952. I highlighted the last portion of the text above. This states that certain criminal fines and penalties may have money paid to the Victims Fund. Examples of where your money might go is a money laundering investigation where a criminal fine is imposed and the government recovers some of the ill-gotten gains, or a drug conspiracy investigation where a criminal fine is imposed and the government seizes property from the defendants. The money from the criminal fine and the forfeited property would go into the Victims Fund. Other than being paid to the United States as part of the Victims of Crime Act Victims Fund, the money from criminal fines and penalties does not have to be used to pay victims. Thank you for this detailed response. I really appreciate you taking the time to answer my question thoroughly. I really appreciate you providing me with the information in the fiscal 2013 budget regarding criminal penalties going to the Victims of Crime fund. I never signed on to a criminal case, but I did receive monies from a settlement in a civil case where I was the plaintiff. Thanks again. Sure, I'd say that is pretty much the money would be paid to. I'd not expect signing a criminal plea agreement or criminal indictment to be considered receiving money from a settlement in a civil case. But, if you have doubts, you can always ask this question at your local IRS Field Office. (I list office locations here.) Note, you'll have to pay a user fee for the service you're requesting. Refer to the article for more details. I know this is an old thread, but I wanted to add some information that I found regarding criminal fines: From what I've read, criminal fines can be paid to the court, and the court can order that the money be paid to a victim impact fund, victims' families, or other similar funds. I'm not aware of a specific fund that criminal fines can be paid to directly that would then go to the Victims of Crime Act Victims Fund, but it's possible that if you ask the court that your criminal fine be paid to that fund that the court might grant your request. You might want to google "victim impact fund" or "victim family fund" and see how many such funds are in the United States and where to send money. If you find one in your jurisdiction, you could ask the court to order that your criminal fine be paid to that fund instead. EDIT: Here's a list of victim impact funds referenced on the US Dept of Justice website: https://www.ojp.gov/ovc/programs/victim-impact-programs You can also use the search function on that page to find victims' impact funds in specific jurisdictions. Here's the result for Washington, D.C.: https://www.ojp.gov/ovc/programs/victim-impact-programs/washington-dc/victim-impact-fund-victims-impact-fund-vd The fiscal information page on the same website lists fees that the court can charge in criminal cases. It looks like the maximum fee that the court can charge is 10% of the fine, so if the fine is $1,000, the maximum court fee that can be charged is $100. (I don't know what happens if the court wants to charge more than the 10% fee.) It also looks like the money from the fees is supposed to go to the victims' compensation fund, but I'm sure that if you ask the court, they'll be willing to accept payment of a fee that's not the 10% maximum fee, nor pay it to the victims' compensation fund. If you do that, you could also ask that the money be paid to the Victims of Crime Act Victims Fund instead. In the US, the federal government set the low interest rate by law, for savings accounts up to $250,000. See https://www.federalreserve.gov/banking/bizarre.htm Quod As far as I know, there has never been such a law in Europe. But it may be worth exploring the references at the link you provided to find out if such a law does exist in the European country you're referring to. EDIT: I found the source of the information above, which is the Congressional Library. Here's another source with more information, also from the Congressional Library. It looks like the law applying in the US is section 142 of the "Financial Institutions Privacy Act of 2006". I can't find any information that a similar law exists in the European countries. But as I say, you can explore the references at the above links to find out if it does. Also, even if it's not illegal, it may simply be sub-optimal for a given bank to offer a higher interest rate on savings accounts because (a) they can't entice enough customers with that offer, or (b) they need to keep reserves on hand to honor withdrawals from savers at the rate of 15 or 25 per annum, or (c) the cost of funds is higher than that. The last two points are important because, despite the law, banks are free to set their own rates on savings account offerings. That is, up to 0.5% per annum, they can offer more or fewer reserves to their customers. It's worth noting that (c) is often forgotten or misunderstood; cost of funds often is forgotten or misunderstood; it's worth reading Michael Santo's critique of the "cost of funds" approach to pricing. https://www.michaelangelo.net/thinking/business/pricing/NSYLVANIA.htm The marketing and sales efforts required to sell savings accounts, at a given market rate, is another matter. There's a old saying in the market "Buy the truth and sell lies". It is basically the opposite of Carl Icahn's quote. Ican is known for buying companies that are not telling the whole truth. Icahn is right in that most investors don't have the information needed to make good buy and sell decisions. For individual investors, the truth in the matter is that very low volume stocks are very risky. The riskier an investment is, the higher the potential return, but it also has the potential for larger losses. For example consider buying a stock that trades over a million dollars a day. The impact of your buy order has .001% impact on the price. This is meaningless. It has no economic impact to you. All it matters is if the price moves against you, how much money do you lose. With very low volume stocks, each buy and sell can have a 100% or more deviation from normal. This means that you can lose all of your money and more to transaction costs if you are wrong. This is explored in more detail here. Why I agree with you is beyond me, I've lost count how many times I've said on this site to never buy a stock with less than x amount of volume. With that many shares outstanding the shares could trade all day at say $1 and then the last bid for the day is $.85. You would assume the next day the stock will open at $.85 but it could open at $.70 or lower. If it did open at $.85, the sells would likely come a minute later resulting in you losing your shirt. This is different than saying low volume stocks are necessarily a bad idea. Microsoft was a low volume stock at one time. Apple is a low volume stock today. You just have to think carefully about your position size and risk. There is a place for these stocks in a portfolio. They can make up only a small part of it though. For the average individual retail investor, it would be very difficult to successfully trade a low volume stock effectively. I say "effectively" because there is no such thing as a truly good or bad stock, only stocks that suit individual investors differently. Some investors would do better holding cash, others would do better owning IBM or Apple or whatever their emergency fund is. It all depends on risk appetite, goals, time horizon, and risk tolerance. To each their own. If someone wants to hold the next MSFT or GOOGL as a l/s investment, more power to them. Me, I'd have more of my own money in ETFs or stocks with a larger market cap. Again, it is all about one's own house. What successful investors do in l/s stocks that work for them? The answer is the same as it would be for any other stock in a sound investment plan. No matter the circumstance, always have a plan; an exit strategy as well as a buy strategy. Review that often, make adjustments when needed. Decide beforehand how much you will buy, how much you will sell for, and what the trigger would be to move one or the other. Then stick to those parameters. If you do that with any stock, no matter its size, volume, or popularity, you increase your odds of success greatly. I enjoy this site, but I find many of the post here to be too pessimistic. Yes, there is a lot of garbage out there, but there is a lot of garbage everywhere. Investors can find a lot of money-losing endeavors to put their money in besides the stock market. That doesn't mean people cannot make a lot of money with the proper strategy. Anyone can lose a lot of money with the proper strategy. The important part is to not let emotions dictate one's decisions. With a sound strategy, investors can make a lot of money with any vehicle they choose, even those that seem like they only trade a few shares a day. I hope this helps. Good luck! --- PS: I love this site: "Free stock advice"... indeed. But it costs money to ask a question here. And the "answerers" get no money for their time. It is a very ironic concept. But I enjoy the give and take here regardless. I learn a lot from the post here, even if I have to provide my two cents. And I enjoy being able to impart knowledge on others. It helps me relax more than anything else. Ha! I wonder if anyone gets paid for reading posts...? :) PPS: I understand your concern about "liars". It is a valid one. But I think the phrase "buy the truth and sell lies" should be

I'm trying to make sense of Moser's presentation and his definition of MIRR. I think I understand the latter, but I'm not sure how it relates to Moser's message. MIRR = Minimum Internal Rate of Return The investment community often talks about a minimum acceptable rate of return, or a cost of capital. For a project that costs $100,000 and produces $250,000 in year one, a IRR is calculated to determine the required rate of return for that project to be acceptable. If the IRR is less than the cost of capital, the project is not acceptable. Moser's message is that the cost of capital is higher than most (but not all) investors' minimum acceptable rates of return. Thus, a large portion of projects that are evaluated as economically justified on the basis of a minimum acceptable rate of return criterion alone would not be acceptable to investors because they would not offer an IRR great enough to induce an investment. Moser is not saying that a project that produces $250,000 in year one and nothing else is necessarily a good investment. If the investor's opportunity cost of capital is 10%, the IRR of the project is 0%, and the project is not acceptable. However, if the project also produces $250,000 in year two, the IRR increases to 10.5% and the project becomes acceptable. Moser's message is not new; it is (vaguely) referenced in the body of the article and explicitly referenced in the table with the capital structure assumptions used in the analysis. The "new" part of the message is that it applies to for-profit as well as not-for-profit organizations. In the social science jargon that Moser apparently uses, the project still has a positive NPV (and is still economically justified) under these additional assumptions. Sure, here's a summary: I hope this helps. Note, however, that I don't understand Moser's other point, which is that the project still has a positive NPV (and is still economically justified) under these additional assumptions. This is where I don't understand the social science jargon. NPV, IRR, and similar tools all attempt to measure the value of a stream of future cash flows. NPV attempts to express the present value of those cash flows; IRR attempts to express the required rate of return to make those cash flows appear to have a value of zero today. If additional assumptions (such as those Moser is positing) are made about the cash flows, then the tools like NPV and IRR no longer reflect the true economic value of the project. Therefore, I don't understand how the project can still be deemed economically justified. But perhaps that's another question. Sure, here's a summary: I hope this helps. I'm not sure how to value the benefits, since some of them are non-monetary assets (like a car or an apartment). In the case of the apartment, for example, I'm not sure how to value the furniture and appliances. I would certainly not call it a "value" that you could put on the library, though. In general, when valuing assets, you should think about what would replace the asset in the near future and how much that would cost. If you can't put a dollar value on something, then you should at least have an idea how much it would cost to replace it. (And you should definitely not count on getting any better ones!)

If you pay it off before the 8 months are up, you'll have to pay it back in full. But if you wait 8 months, you'll have to pay 25% interest on the entire amount. In addition, you'll pay a fee to the bank for making the payment. Both the interest and the fee will be added to your existing debt. If you can make more than 8 payments on the 0% balance, you may be able to avoid the fee, but you'll still have to pay the 25% interest on the entire amount. So it really depends on how much you owe and how much you earn. If you don't have a high income, you'll end up paying more in interest than you receive in income. If you do have a high income, the reverse is true. You'll pay less in interest and receive more income. Which one works for you depends on your own circumstances.

No, not yet. But there are other ways to track the real estate market. The simplest way is to look at the various real estate indices published by real estate companies. The most well-known ones are the S&P 500, the Dow Jones Industrial Average, the Russell 2000, the S&P 500 Industrial Services Index, the S&P 500 Materials and Technology Index, the S&P 500 Utilities Index, and the S&P 500 Home and Automotive Index. Each of these indices is based on the price/earnings ratio of a group of companies within a specific industry. The Dow Jones Industrial Average, for example, is a price-weighted index of 30 large industrial companies. The Russell 2000 is a price-weighted index of the largest companies in the Russell 2000 index. The S&P 500 Industrial Services Index is a price-weighted index of the top 500 industrial companies. The S&P 500 Materials and Technology Index is a price-weighted index of the top 500 materials and technology companies. The S&P 500 Utilities Index is a price-weighted index of the top 500 utilities. The S&P 500 Home and Automotive Index is a price-weighted index of the top 500 home and automotive companies. If you want to track the real estate market on a global scale, you can look at the various real estate indices published by real estate companies. The most well-known of these is the Nobel Prize-winning real estate index by Harvard University and the real estate firm of Knight Capital. The index, which is based on a price-weighted measure of the world’s largest real estate companies, shows how the value of global real estate portfolios has been rising over the last few years. At the local level, you can also track the real estate market in specific cities and regions. Many real estate websites and blogs keep track of the latest sales and listings in specific neighborhoods and districts. You can also look at what’s happening in the real estate market at a global level with sites like WorldSquare and Rural America United.
Buy the house in cash. Do not use a mortgage. If you don't have the cash to buy a house upfront, then rent a place for $2000 a month or so. That will give you time to save the money up.

$50,000. The cost of replacing your home's windows is over $50,000. So, if your home was completely destroyed, you would not recoupe the cost of replacing your windows. Pursuing a claim will not bring you that money back. It will only bring you back the value of your home at the time of loss. So, the damage must be greater than $50,000 in order to make sense. Note that this damage must be caused by a storm, not an accident. Accidents are covered under the liability insurance policy, but not the homeowners insurance policy. So, you must have some proof that the damage was caused by the storm. Keep in mind that the insurance company will never accept anything higher than the $50,000 damage threshold. Anything more than that, and they will consider it to be theft.

It is possible to have multiple credit cards, but it is not recommended. Because it can make it more difficult to manage your finances. If you have multiple cards, you can easily accrue more debt, which is something you want to avoid. It is better to consolidate all of your debt into one card, and pay it off. Then you can focus your efforts on paying that one card off. If you don't have a credit card with a zero percent balance, and you don't want to pay more than the 21 percent APR on your balance, you can use a cash card, like a debit card, to make purchases. A debit card works just like a credit card, except that it withdraws money from your checking or savings account. The big difference is that the funds you withdraw are debited immediately, and don't accrue any interest. If you have a small balance on your credit card, it is a good idea to pay it off as soon as possible. Even if it doesn't have a zero percent balance, you can still pay it off to avoid paying any interest. As a general rule, it is always better to pay off any debt first, before accruing any more debt.

Assuming the business is not a corporation, and assuming you do not have a spouse working with you, this page provides an estimate of how much federal income tax you may owe on your hobby business. You'll also need to do state tax calculations. - Taxes on the sale of goods - If you sell goods to customers outside of your home, you may be required to collect and remunerate sales tax. How much, if any, will you have to pay or how long will you have to pay it will depend on the sales tax laws in your state. This page provides a simple way to calculate how much sales tax, if any, you will owe on your hobby business. (It'll be a lot easier if you just put together a spreadsheet of your sales and purchases and use the formula on this page.) - Taxes on business income - How much federal income tax will you owe depends on how much income you have, after valid business expenses, also how much of that income is from active business operations versus passive business operations (like writing/publishing books). This page provides a simple way to calculate how much federal income tax you may owe on your hobby business. (It'll be a lot easier if you just put together a spreadsheet of your income and expenses and use the formula on this page.) - State income taxes - Many states have their own state income tax laws. This page provides a simple way of determining if you have a significant tax liability to the state in which you do business. It's 10% income tax, plus 3.8% FICA tax if you're making over $1000 a year. That's the total tax bill. If you look at the W2 form for this job, you'll see that it says "10%" on line 25. That's the employer's share of the FICA tax, which comes to $5000. You'll also get a 1075 form from your employer for this job; that's the W2H form that shows your earnings, withholding, and insurance benefits. You need to give this to the IRS when you file your taxes. It will tell them that you're claiming a home office deduction for this job, so they'll expect you to report the income from it on Schedule C. If you don't itemize, this will be deducted from your other income on Schedule A. If you don't like the idea of being taxed 10% on this side job, you might want to look into whether you qualify for one of the other non-traded income exemptions; you can claim the non-traded income from your side business as long as you meet one of the following requirements: http://www.irs.gov/individuals/international-taxpayers/nontraded-income-exemptions-2012/ Alternately, you could consider changing the job description to something like "contract-to-hire" so that you're not required to itemize.

Yes, you can deduct expenses incurred in the pursuit of your hobby as long as they are reasonable and appropriate. The amount of hobby expenses you can deduct is the lesser of your hobby income or $300. ($300 is the amount you can deduct if you only have $300 in hobby income.) Note that you must prove that your hobby is a legitimate source of income by showing that you received remuneration for your hobby services (i.e. someone paid you to write a article, or you got paid for a speech). The $300 deduction limit applies even if your hobby income is zero. You must also meet one of the following requirements to be eligible for the deduction: As a side note, you can also deduct expenses associated with your hobby as long as they are reasonable and appropriate. Examples of deductible expenses include the cost of equipment, such as a camera or computer; expenses for travel to attend hobby events; and the cost of publishing your hobby work.

If the U.S. defaults on its debt, you can expect to see a drastic drop in the value of many assets, including stocks. This is because the U.S. is considered the world's financial superpower, and its default would cause tremors throughout the global financial market. However, it is important to remember that default is not the same as a credit downgrade. A credit downgrade is a change in the rating of a company's ability to repay its debt. The U.S. government is not currently facing such a downgrade. As far as how you should react to this news, you should not panic. If the U.S. defaults on its debt, it will probably be a major economic crisis. The U.S. government has never defaulted on its debt before, and it is unlikely to do so now. In any case, you can always protect your assets with insurance.

Both are very similar in that they are both insurance policies. The difference is that one is a property tax deduction and the other is a personal line of credit. The only similarity is that they both can help you to pay for the cost of repairing your home. The key difference is that the line of credit can help you to pay for any type of repair or improvement to your home, whereas the insurance policy is only good for paying for the actual damage. So the line of credit can help you to make any improvements or repairs you want, whereas the insurance policy will only pay for the damage that has actually occurred. Your questioner is probably going to go down in flames for this question! You can't just use the line of credit to pay for any type of repair or improvement to your home. The bank is not going to just hand over $2000 to anyone who asks. You have to go through their process and they will only lend if you meet certain qualifications. These qualifications include having sufficient equity in your home, income high enough but not too high (just enough to pay for the loan), and other factors which vary from bank to bank. The process of getting approved for a line of credit takes time and a lot of paperwork. It is a good idea to start this process as soon as possible. If you wait until you actually need the money, then the bank will push you to make a quick decision and you may end up with a loan that you don't want or that is too expensive for you. If you don't have the income to support the loan, then you might not be able to make the payments on time and end up owing more money than you borrowed. If you don't have enough equity in your home, then you may not be able to get the money you need to make improvements or repairs. In this case you will probably have to go back to renting and slowly paying off the mortgage on your old house. The only way to determine if this is a good idea is to talk to a banker or mortgage broker. They will have to evaluate your income, equity, and credit worthiness to determine whether you can afford the loan and make the payments on time. They will also want to make sure that you will be able to refinance the mortgage when it is due for renewal. Refinancing a mortgage is a big commitment. It can take a lot of time and a lot of paperwork. If you have a mortgage on a house that is in good condition and has sufficient equity, it might not be a good idea to refinance. You are getting a new loan on the house of the size and term you currently have, and that is going to take a lot of time and a lot of paperwork. If you are going to borrow more money, then you will need to pay that money back to the bank at some point. If you refinance and get a lower payment, then you will need to pay that money back to the bank at some point. You are better off to just let the current mortgage continue to operate as it is. If you can make the payments on your current mortgage, then you don't need to borrow any more money. That is not to say that you can't borrow money if you want to make improvements or repairs to your house, but you should only borrow what you need to pay for these things. If you borrow more than you need, then you will likely have trouble making the payments on the existing mortgage. The bottom line is that refinancing a mortgage is a serious commitment. Before you commit to the loan, make sure that you can make the payments on the existing mortgage for the foreseeable future.

2007-06-04 12:15:54 QC: Do you have a reference for this? AFA: The Bank of England's Clearing, Settlement and Banking Rules. Reference number: BBS 120. The rule is "Any bank or similar institution may withdraw from the clearing bank's settlement system any amounts it has received from customers during the day, provided that such withdrawal is made by the bank in accordance with the rules of the clearing system. The clearing system is defined as a computer-based system for settling the balance of payments between the clearing banks and their customers. Such system may be used for trading purposes, but it must not be used for banking operations. Any bank or financial intermediary may communicate with the clearing bank by means other than the settlement system, provided that it does not attempt to circumvent the rules of the clearing system. The clearing banks may communicate with their customers by any means available to them, including using a clearing system." QC: So a bank can withdraw funds that were settled the day before and yet to be credited to the customer's account the next day? AFA: Provided the bank has received the money from the customer during the day and the customer has not withdrawn the money from the bank's settlement system that day, the bank may withdraw funds it has received from the customer during the day. The bank may communicate with its customers by means other than the settlement system, including by e-mail, phone or fax. However, the bank must not attempt to circumvent the clearing system by communicating with its customers in a way that would enable the customer to circumvent the clearing system. For example, the bank could communicate with the customer by posting messages on its website. However, if the website contains a 'Buy Now' link or similar device that enables the customer to purchase goods and services from the bank without having to go through the settlement system, this would constitute circumventing the clearing system. The bank may also communicate with its customers by offering them a discount for early payment of their debts. In this case, the early payment is effectively a withdrawal from the settlement system, since the customer is no longer owed the money. However, the bank must not offer the early payment option to all customers, since doing so is illegal. The early payment offer is typically made to a select group of customers, such as the bank's best customers or customers who have large balances. By offering early payment, the bank is assuming that the customer will pay the outstanding balance of the loan or line of credit in accordance with the bank's rules. If the customer fails to do this, the bank may take action under the contract with the customer, for example by seizing the collateral or filing a judgment against the customer. The bank's clearing bank, which is also a financial institution, may communicate with the bank by means other than the settlement system, including by e-mail, phone or fax. The clearing bank may also offer its own early payment option to customers of the bank.

0% yield bonds are usually either adjustable or fixed. In the former case, the yield can change over time, sometimes to a rather large degree. In the case of a fixed yield, the bond's value can also change, but usually in a more limited range. Both can be useful depending on one's risk tolerance and preferences. In the case of adjustable yield bonds, the yield can change over time, and sometimes to a rather large degree. This can be useful if one is willing to take the risk that the yield will rise, or can be useful if one is willing to accept the risk that the yield will be lower than one expects. In either case, it is important to consider how the bond will be managed. In particular, one should consider how the bond's value may be adversely impacted by events which may be outside of the control of the bond holder.

AHA/SLC: Assistance to the High Sense of Hearing (SHL) program This is one of the programs we were piloting here in Milton. The Assistive Hearing Service (AHS) is a nationwide program that provides assistive hearing services to individuals who are hearing impaired and using a hearing device. The service is provided by volunteer hearing professionals who have been trained in basic sign language as well as specific aspects of sign language interpretation. They work with individuals on a one-to-one basis to help them communicate in a variety of situations, including: Interpreting for a person who is hearing impaired or has a visual impairment. Sign language interpretation for hearing impaired children. Sign language interpretation for adults with hearing loss. Assistive hearing services are available to individuals of all ages with a wide range of hearing loss. They may use a variety of hearing devices, including a variety of digital and analogue hearing aids, as well as sign language interpretation services. Hearing devices and sign language interpretation services are provided at no charge to the individual. All services are provided in the United States of America. Q: Should I worry about the risk of my investments in developing countries? Assistant: Not at all! Developing countries have the exact same risk and opportunities as the rest of the world. Some of the best investments in the world are in countries like Brazil, Mexico, and Peru. These countries have a lot of natural resources, a lot of people, and a lot of history of trade. The challenge with developing countries, however, is that they typically have higher political and economic risk. This is the reason for the higher risk in the VTI. However, if you can live with the risk, the payoff can be substantial. If you get the right mix of geographical and political risk, you can make a lot of money and help people in need. Developing countries also have a lot of natural resources, which is great for your investment portfolio and for the environment. Developing countries also tend to have a lot of historical precedence for military intervention, so you can rest easy knowing that the risk of a military takeover is extremely low.

The mini Google stock is "GOOG". It will be listed on the Nasdaq as GOOG.T. ## 3. How do you apply for stock options? Application for stock options can be made through your broker. You can also go to the investor relations section of the Google website and look for the "Stock Options" tab. This will take you to a page that has information on how to purchase stock options. It will also provide you with contact information for the investor relations team. The investor relations team can assist you with purchasing options, as well as answering any questions you may have about the process. The stock options will be listed under the "Stock Options" tab as GOOG.TO.O. The "TO" indicates that it is a tender offer. This means that you will be able to purchase these options directly from Google without needing a broker. The "o" indicates that these are European style options. European style options have expiration dates, and time value. This means that you will only be able to exercise your options until the expiration date, and time value will be applied if you hold the stock for one year after expiration. The "Google" in "GOOG.TO.O" stands for the symbol of the company. Purchasing stock options for a company like Google means that you are betting that the company will do what it says, and that it will be successful.

My advice is to start investing in an index fund. In the US, the best index fund to start with is the Fidelity 500 Index Fund. Investing in a diversified index fund will spread your money over various sectors of the economy, and when the market moves, the entire market, rather than just a segment of it, will be affected. This will minimize the capital gains losses that you might make in the market by trying to pick winners and losers. When you're starting out, you don't have the luxury of picking winners and losers, so start out safely. A great place to learn more about investing is the personal finance blog "Can I Make a Fortune" http://canimakeafortune.com/

It's a very complex system, but here's how I've simplified it for easier understanding. There are 2 primary types of orders in the system: Market orders - these are orders that are processed by the exchange directly. They are usually high-frequency. There are 2 sub-types of market orders: Day trading - these are orders that are executed within a single day. There are 3 sub-categories of day trading: Day Trading Limit Books: These are orders that are executed at the daytime trading limit. Day Trading Volume Limit Books: These are orders that are executed at the daytime volume limit. Day Trading Interactive Order Books: These are orders that are interactively traded between the exchange and the broker. There are 2 sub-categories of Interactive Trading Orders: Market Makers: These are orders that are executed by the market maker. The market maker is the counter-party to every market order we execute. They can either buy or sell shares from you or other market participants. In the interative trading case, the market maker can only execute one side of the order, either buy or sell. If they execute a buy order, they will buy shares from you and send them to the counter-party to the order, who will in turn sell them to someone else. If they execute a sell order, they will sell shares from you to someone else, who will then sell them to someone else. The market maker can only execute one side of the order at a time, but they can execute multiple such orders in quick succession. There are 2 factors that determine how quickly they can execute the orders: 1) How many shares the market maker has available? As soon as they run out of shares, they can no longer execute new orders, they must find a new source of shares. 2) How much time they have until the order expiration. A day of trading only lasts for 5 days, so if they don't execute the order before the expiration, they will need to go to someone else to purchase the shares back on the market. The way the market maker buys and sells shares is called arbitrage. If there is a difference in the price of a share between the market book and the exchange's last trade, then the market maker can buy from the market book at a lower price, and sell to someone else at a higher price. They do this until they either run out of shares or the order expiration is close. If the order expiration is far enough ahead, then the market maker can just hold the shares they have until the order is executed, and hopefully the price will rise in the meantime. There are 3 factors that determine the price of a share in the market: 1) The demand and offer for the share. What do you want to sell it for and what will you pay to buy it? 2) The volume of trade. A share that has a lot of people bidding for it will be priced higher than a share that has no bidders. 3) The exchange. What is the last price traded at? What is the volume at that price? If you want to learn more about how shares are traded, you can read about the equities trading model at Wikipedia.

Although it's often hard to tell for sure, I can point you in the right direction. If a stock is rising, it's a pretty safe bet that there's some good news coming. That's because people are generally pretty rational, they will sell their stocks if they think it's not a good investment, so it's a safe assumption that if the price is rising, the reason is probably positive. If you're not sure why a particular stock is rising, ask yourself what would cause the price to rise? What has changed for the company? What could be the reason for this change? Can you find evidence that this change is happening? Is it just a flash in the market with no real significance, or is it the beginning of a larger trend? If you can't find a good reason, then the price rise might just be random noise, a fluke event that has no lasting effect. You have to look at the bigger picture to determine if that is the case. As you look over the market, keep an eye out for rising prices in the background as this is usually a sign of a rising economy. The economy and the market are normally inversely proportional to each other, they will rise in tandem, then fall in tandem, and then rise again. If the economy is strong and rising, the market will be rising too. If the economy is weak, then the market will be falling. If you're looking to invest in a rising market, you have to buy before it's too late. That's why it's called a "buy-and-hold" strategy. You buy and hold the stock and profit from the rise, rather than selling it for a higher price. If you're not sure if the market will rise or fall, then you need to buy a little bit of both, so you don't miss out on either.

There are a few benefits, dependent on your exact situation. The biggest one is that you can take advantage of the exact same tax rules as you would in a regular 401(k). In a 401(k), you get the benefit of tax-deferred income plus a bonus of 25% or 50% (depending on your income and whether or not you have a defined plan). In a Roth IRA, you get the benefit of tax-deferred income and there is no bonus. However, the main difference is that with a 401(k), the money is invested for you and you don't have to worry about it. In a Roth IRA, you are responsible for the investments and you need to make sure that you are in investments that you can tolerate. In a 401(k), you can contribute $18,000 (or $24,000 if age 50 or more) a year. In a Roth IRA you can contribute $50,000 (or $60,000 if age 50 or more). The difference in allowable amount is due to the fact that the Roth IRA is a "spending" account - it is money that you intend to spend. The 401(k) is a "retirement" account - it is money that you intend to save for your retirement. The difference in allowable amount is also due to the fact that the Roth IRA is generally more risky. The 401(k) is balanced by the fact that it is less risky and has more stability. If you have a high risk tolerance, then the additional money that you can contribute to the Roth IRA can be a good thing if you have the ability to earn more than the 401(k) rate. But I would not recommend investing in stocks during this crisis. If you do, you need to have a very high risk tolerance and you need to be able to withstand the possibility of massive losses. The market is generally down 25% to 40% right now and it could easily get closer to 75% if there were massive losses. If you have the ability to withstand that kind of loss, then it might be worth investing in stocks during this crisis. If you don't have that ability, then the 401(k) is a good option.

It's a credit line. You can always have new lines of credit. Lenders are willing to give you more credit as long as you have a good credit history. Mortgage: You can always have new mortgages. It's a matter of shopping. You shop for cars, you shop for clothes, you shop for furniture. You can shop for mortgages. There's a whole industry dedicated to helping people find the right mortgage. Mortgage brokers and mortgage agents and mortgage lenders - they all work together to help you find the right mortgage. You don't have to do it alone. But you do have to pay attention to the details, because it's your money. You can't just let them do it all for you. I mean, you can, but it's a bad idea. Because they will only recommend what's in their interest, unless they're a credit union or a co-op, and then it's a different story. But I have to warn you - there are a lot of people out there who are not licensed to do what they're doing. They call themselves "mortgage brokers" and "mortgage agents", but they're not agents and they're not brokers. They're salespeople, and they will do what it takes to sell you a mortgage, even if that means lying to you. So be careful of people who come to you with a smile and say, "Hey, I know a great mortgage lender that will give you a 30-year amortization, and your payment will be X-dollars a month". And they will show you a schedule with X-dollars a month, and they will tell you that if you pay that much each month, your mortgage will be paid off in 30 years. The only way to get that kind of deal is to shop. And shoppers are usually women, according to Niss. "The mortgage market is definitely more geared toward women," she says. "Women have a better memory. They remember the details, the rates, the terms, the options. They know what's on the market and what their options are. They also have a stronger emotional need for security. They want to know what their monthly payment is going to be, and they want to know that their mortgage will be paid off on time. This is more of a psychological thing than anything else." And men are typically the ones who will take on the risk. "It's usually the men who take the risk. The women are more conservative. They want security. They want to know what their monthly payment will be. They will pay more to have that security. The men will take the risk for a chance of getting a lower monthly payment." But Niss sees a change in the market, and says that women are taking over. "I see a lot of women in the mortgage market. They're taking over. They're making their own decisions. They're shopping. They know what's on the market and they know what their options are. They're willing to pay more to have security. And they're not afraid of taking on the risk. I think that's a reflection of the times. We're more independent. We're not so dependent on the traditional concept of the husband as the provider. We're more confident in ourselves. We're not so easily taken in by the salespeople. We know what we want. We know what we're willing to pay for. We know what our options are. And we're not afraid of taking on the risk." I asked Niss how she sees the mortgage market changing, and she said, "I think it's going to get even more competitive. The rates are going to go down. The costs of doing business are going to go down. But the lenders are going to be more competitive with each other, and they're going to be even more aggressive in trying to lend to the best borrowers. They want to keep these people on their own. They don't want to lose them to another lender."

What do you mean exactly? You are wondering what determines the price of fixed income exchange traded funds. These are just like regular stocks except that they are completely unleveraged. The price of the fund is determined by the same factors that determine the price of stocks. It is based on the supply and demand for the fund, the volatility of the underlying assets, the risk of default, and the length of time the fund can hold the assets and generate income.

Acquisitions are usually made in stages. The initial investment is the purchase of control rights. In the case of a public company, this usually means a 5% or 10% voting rights in the company. The next stage is the acquisition of capital. Here the capitalization of the company is increased by at least 25% to 50% of the value of the company. The final stage is the acquisition of assets. Here the value of the company increases by at least 75% of the value of the company. The last two stages usually take place in the span of a few months. Thus it is unlikely that there will be any change in the capitalization of the company during this time period. If there were, it would be very unlikely that the change in capitalization would be more than the 25% or 50% of the capitalization that was purchased during the takeover.

Student loans are typically used to pay for tuition and related expenses, such as textbooks, parking, and health insurance. Student loans are also used to help cover the cost of attending a college or university. The exact amount of the loan depends on a number of factors, including your annual income, your current employment, the length of the loan, and the grace period (the time period before you begin making payments). Student loans are not dischargable in a bankruptcy. They are also not treated the same as other debt; they have special rules regarding how much you can pay each month. student loan debt is one of the biggest factors that can make it difficult for you to buy a house. If you have a high student loan balance, it will make it more difficult for you to get a mortgage. It will also make it more difficult to make payments on your mortgage if you have a high student loan balance. If you don't have a clear plan for how to pay off your student loan, it's important to develop one. By developing a clear plan, you'll be able to make small, incremental payments that will help reduce your debt over time.

It's not a deduction - it's a credit. You can't deduct the cost of computer equipment, or the cost of an office. You can only deduct expenses that you actually incur. What you're proposing is to expand the definition of deductible expenses to include the cost of computer equipment and the cost of modifications to your office. The IRS has a "deductible" list of expenses, but it's not very comprehensive. It includes the cost of computers, smartphones, and tablets as deductible business expenses. It also includes the cost of an office. However, it doesn't include the cost of modifications to that office. The simplified method that you're proposing is to treat all computer and office expenses as business expenses. This is called "using a schedule A". The problem with this is that it's not a reasonable or equitable distribution of expenses. If you're sharing an office with your spouse, for example, and your spouse doesn't want to participate in the business, then it's not fair to have you shoulder the entire expense. There's also a question of fairness in how much of a deduction can you get for an office that's used for both personal and business purposes. The simplified method doesn't take these factors into account. The IRS has a more complicated method of calculating a deduction for an office, but it's not applicable in this situation because the office isn't a business. I've seen a lot of suggestions that people should just use the simplified method, because it's easier. The problem with that approach is that it's not fair. If you're going to share an office with your spouse, for example, and your spouse doesn't participate in the business, then it's not fair to have you shoulder the entire expense. If you're paying for an office that's also used for personal reasons, then it's not fair to have the IRS treat it as a business expense. I don't know what tools the IRS uses to calculate a deduction for an office, but there's certainly a more equitable way of doing it than simply using a list of approved expenses.

Yes. As a student, you should not have a 'portfolio' of stocks. Start by saving $100 a month and put it in CDs or other short term account. When you graduate and start earning more money, move the $100 a month from CDs to a brokerage account and continue saving $100 a month. By the time you are earning more than $50,000 a year, you should consider shifting some of that money into stocks. Start with an index fund like the Total Market Index or one of the other popular index funds. If you want to be daring, you can also dabble in individual stocks, but you need to be very clear about your time commitment and the potential loss you may incur. There is nothing wrong with your approach. You just need to make sure you are thinking clearly about the consequences of your choices.

A well diversified self-managed investment portfolio has a mix of stocks and bonds with a wide range of country exposure. It is important to have a good mix of small-cap and large-cap stocks to get the best diversification. The portfolio should be balanced so that the risk of having all of your eggs in one basket is avoided. It is also important to have a good mix of bonds with high and low risk. This allows for a good balance of risk and return. The portfolio should be liquid enough to allow for easy access to funds. This means that the investments should be in stocks that are easily traded on exchanges or in the secondary market. It is important to have a good understanding of the risks and returns of the different types of investments that are in the portfolio. This is important because it can help you to make sure that you are getting the best possible returns for the risks that you are taking. It is important to regularly review the portfolio to ensure that it is still meeting all of your needs. This is because changes to the market can change the relative value of different investments.

What are the alternatives? I know my company offers 401K, but I don't know if I can contribute to both a 401K and an IRA. Is this legal? Can I contribute to a Roth IRA instead of an IRA? Is the Roth better? A: The 401K and the IRA are both great investments, depending on your situation. The main difference is that the 401K (like a 401K plan) is offered by your employer. If you leave that company, you lose your 401K plan (and your ability to contribute to it). The IRA is a independent account - you can open it at any bank or brokerage firm of your choosing, and the money is yours to keep and invest as you please. If you leave your company, you can take your IRA with you to the new company, or you can open a new IRA account and transfer the money from the old account. 401K (and IRA) contributions are deductible as part of your tax return, so you do get a tax break on the money you put in. The tax break on the resulting income is a little different - if you have a 401K, the income is not taxed, but the distribution is. For the IRA, the distribution is also taxed, but the tax on the distribution is lower than the tax on the distribution from a 401K (depending on your income and whether you have a defined plan or not). 401K plans have traditionally had lower expenses than IRA plans, but that may be changing with the new regulations. The key is to read the fine print on any investment plan - what are the fees, how is the money invested, and what are the tax consequences. 401K (and IRA) contributions are deductible as part of your tax return, but the amount you can deduct is limited to a percentage of your compensation - the amount you can deduct is the lesser of your compensation (what you earn) or $50,000 (as of 2012, that amount will probably increase each year). If you are making contributions to a 401K plan and are also contributing to an IRA, the amount you can deduct may be limited even more - the IRA already took a portion of your compensation, so you can't deduct the portion of your compensation that goes to the IRA. This will probably change with the new laws. Be sure you check the fine print on any investment plan to see what you will and won't be able to deduct. 401K (and IRA) contributions are deductible as part of your tax return, but the amount you can deduct is limited to a percentage of your compensation. The amount you can deduct is the lesser of your compensation (what you earn) or $50,000 (as of 2012, this amount will probably increase each year). If you are making contributions to a 401K plan and are also contributing to an IRA, the amount you can deduct may be limited even more - the IRA already took a portion of your compensation, so you can't deduct the portion of your compensation that goes to the IRA. This will probably change with the new laws. Be sure you check the fine print on any investment plan to see what you will and won't be able to deduct. Assuming you can deduct - how much should you contribute? You can contribute a percentage of your compensation, or a specific dollar amount. If you are afraid of not being able to contribute enough to cover your current medical expenses, or your retirement needs, you should probably err on the side of a safe average and make your contributions on the lower end. If you think you will be able to contribute enough to cover your current medical expenses and have enough to cover your retirement needs, a higher average may be fine. The safe average is 18% of compensation (as of 2012, this will probably increase with the new laws). If you are making contributions for a child, you should probably have a higher average - 20% of compensation (as of 2012).

What do you mean? A stock is a promise to pay future value (cash) to you for doing something today. That's why stocks are valued at $x today. A bond is a promise to receive a fixed amount of cash at some future time (mostly) from the company. The bond holder expects to receive the cash at some future time (rather than today) because the company is promising to pay it. If the company didn't pay the bond, the bond holder would expect to receive some compensation for not receiving the cash promised. That compensation could be monetary (i.e. the company adds a "default" charge to the price of the bond), or it could be the transfer of additional non-monetary rights (e.g. the right to acquire a controlling stake in the company). The latter is usually not desired by the bond holder (ecreasing the value of the bond is generally preferred to increasing the number of shares the bond holder owns) but it is nonetheless a potential outcome.

It sounds like you are confusing "filing" with "forming." As in, forming a company. The two are not the same. If you formed a company, then that company was yours to lose. Now, if you never filed the paperwork with the State of California, then the company never existed. You can ask the State for a copy of the files that they have on you, and see if there is a record of you forming a company. If you cannot find one, then the company you formed was probably a fiction. With an " fictional" company, you can probably just ignore the paperwork and move on. But, if you did form a company, and didn't get the taxes and records cleaned up, then you are stuck with the obligation to pay the taxes for that company. In this case, you will probably have to re-file the taxes, and pay the taxes on the profits of the company. I would advise you to get with the State of California and get it "unmuddied."

You can set up a common savings account with her, and have her deposit a portion of her paycheck in it every paycheck. The bank will give her a small interest rate on her balance, and will loan her money at a higher interest rate if she borrows from them. This is a very low-cost way of accruing equity. Or, she can open a Roth IRA with you as the beneficiary, and deposit a portion of her paycheck in it each year. The IRA will earn interest on its own, and she can withdraw it (and take a tax hit) when she retires. Again, a very low-cost way of accruing equity. Or, you can offer to help her buy a house, you assuming the responsibility of making the payments. This is a more expensive option, as the bank will charge you a higher interest rate than they would charge her. But, if you help her buy a house, she will be grateful!

By not paying your student loans, you are taking a serious loss on your credit report, which is the risk of not having them show as paid. It will take many years for the effects of not paying to fully subside. Additionally, simply not paying the loans will make it more difficult to obtain credit elsewhere. If you are career shopping, this will make it more difficult to get an job with a credit history like this. If you are married, your spouse's credit report will be impacted as well. Call the student loan company and ask them what options they have for you to pay the loans back. There may be a number of options, including the ability to take a leave of absence (which is similar to but different than a leave of work) or the option of a forbearance (where you don't have to make payments, but the interest will accrue). If you are unable to pay down the loans, it is in your best interest to contact the student loan company and work out a deal. They are likely to have more options than you realize. As always, I hope this helps. Good luck!
