4.2 million USD at 10% per year for 20 years. 4.2 million USD is the amount of money you need to have in order to get a 2.5% return on your money. This amount of money is called "liquidity". It means that you can easily access your money at any time. The 10% per year is the rate of return you will get on your money. If you have 4.2 million USD in liquidity, and you get 10% return on it, then you will end up with 4.2 million USD in 20 years. But you need to invest your money in stable, high quality investments. This means that you need to invest your money in stocks. The 20 year time frame for stocks is adequate. If you look at the charts for stocks, you will see that they tend to track each other. The 5 year chart for the stock market shows how it tracks the 10 year chart for the stock market. So if you look at the chart for the S&P 500, you will see that it tracks the chart for the S&P 100. The chart for the S&P 100 shows that it tracks the chart for the S&P 500. This is the case for most stocks. It means that you can safely assume that if the market for one set of stocks behaves like the market for another set of stocks, then the market for your stocks will behave like the market for those stocks. This limits your choices a bit, but it does ensure that your money will not be lost to wild swings in the market. If you have 4.2 million USD in stocks, and the market is giving you 10% per year, then in 20 years you will have 4.2 million USD in stocks. If the market is giving you 10% per year, this means that in 20 years your portfolio will be worth 4.2 million USD in 20 years. But you need to remember that the market is not always on an upward trend. So you need to make sure that your investments are in stable, high quality investments. This means that you need to ensure that the companies that you are investing in are not going to disappear or go out of business. So a stable high quality investment is something like Microsoft or Johnson & Johnson. Both of these companies are household names. They have been around for a long time. They are stable. They are high quality. They will be around forever. Microsoft and Johnson & Johnson are not high yield investments. They are not getting the 10% per year that you need in order to make money. But they are stable. And your portfolio is not going to go down every time you look at them. So ensuring that your portfolio is stable and high quality is the main concern. Once you have that taken care of, then you can start thinking about making more money. If you want to make more money, you need to think about trading. Trading is not necessarily a skill that you learn in a course. It is a skill that you learn by doing it. Many people start out by trading their own stocks. I started out by trading stocks. I didn't have any particular reason to do this. I was just trying to make more money. But I discovered that this was harder than it seemed like it should be. So I read a book on technical analysis and started using a trading platform. Now I trade on a daily basis. I trade stocks. I trade options. I have a good feel for what I am doing. I know what to look for in a trade. I know how to manage my money and make sure that it is not being lost to fees. I have a good understanding of the market and I can analyze the data to find good trades. Most importantly, I know how to lose money. I have lost money several times and learned from it. Now I am not looking to make money. I am looking to lose money. Because losing money is the only way to make money in this business. You can't win every trade. So if you don't lose money in every trade then you are not doing it right. Emphasize that you will lose money. Because when you lose money in a trade, that money is there for you to lose it again. Losing money is not a bad thing. It is the price of doing business. If your returns are 10% or more per year, then you are doing well. You can then go on to make more money. But

You are describing a situation where the homeowner has a mortgage on the house, and the home is in such poor condition that it is not worth the value of the mortgage. In this case, the bank is probably going to foreclose the mortgage and take possession of the home. Most likely, the bank will sell the home at a deep discount to a buyer who is willing to purchase a fixer upper. Fixer uppers are homes that have been neglected and are in some state of disrepair. The buyer of the fixer upper will probably have to pay off the old mortgage on the fixer upper, and will probably have to repair and improve the home. So, the bank is not necessarily losing money on the mortgage, they are just not making as much money as they might have made if the home had been well kept up. It is possible that if the home is in such poor condition that no one will buy it, the bank will have to take possession of it and sell it to a buyer who is willing to rehabilitate the home. In that case, the bank will make a profit, even though they are no longer making as much money on the mortgage. What is not to like? Most people who own homes are afraid of having a home improvement loan, because they do not want to have the risk of foreclosure if they fail to make the payments. It is possible to have a loan for the improvement of the home, and if the home is rented out and the money is used for the improvement of the home, then that money is coming back to the home owner. So, a home improvement loan can be a good thing. Just make sure that you have a reliable method of repaying the loan.

Nate Dilliard, CFA®, MBA, former Wall Street executive and investment advisor With all the talk about "necessities" in the personal finance arena, it's easy to lose sight of the things that are not necessarily required to maintain a healthy lifestyle. While needs are generally subjective, there is some consensus on what constitutes a basic necessity. The aforementioned report by the National Bureau of Labor also identifies the top five most important financial needs, which are: 1) Cash management 2) Credit management 3) Insurance 4) Retirement planning 5) Mortgage payment The report also identifies the five most common money-management problems, which are: 1) Poor savings/investment habits 2) Unorganized cash management 3) Insufficient insurance coverage 4) The inability to make a budget 5) The inability to pay for a major expense unexpectedly I'd like to think that the "means" test is a useful tool for evaluating whether or not you have the means to live off of your savings. While it's certainly important to have a plan for how to spend your money, it's also important to have a plan for how to save for the things that you need. Too many people, particularly in the younger portions of the baby boomers, have gotten themselves in trouble by not having a plan for savings. While it's easy to argue that everyone should have a plan for rainy-day scenarios, it's harder to argue that everyone should have a plan for saving for the things that they actually need. Too many people either don't have a plan or have a plan but don't have the money to make it happen. The first step that I would recommend is to make a list of all of the things that you currently spend money on every month. For most people, this will include the mortgage, utilities, credit card payments, insurance premiums, etc. Make sure to include items that are both optional and necessary (i.e. if you can't pay for something then it is in fact a necessity). Once you have a list of all of your monthly expenses, you can begin to think about where you want to spend your money. If possible, keep it out of the banking system and into a private account. This makes it easier to pay for things that you do not want to pay for (i.e. if you hate credit cards and don't want to pay for them, then don't do it). The second step is to develop a plan for saving for the things that you want. This can be done in a number of different ways. If you are starting out, I would recommend that you read up on and implement the concepts of "paying off your credit cards". If you are further along, I would recommend that you look at "building a rainy-day fund". Regardless of the method, make a plan to save for the things that you want and not the things that you don't want. The final step is to actually implement the plan. For most people, this will require changing the way that they handle their money. If that's you, please feel free to read my previous post on getting started. The key thing to remember is that you can do it. I'd also recommend that you review the reports that I linked above. While they are based on data from the personal finance forums, they provide a good starting point for people of all ages. Keep in mind that they are based on the experiences of a few people and may not apply to everyone. The most important thing that you can do is to start saving for the things that you need. I'd also recommend that you find a like-minded person to talk to about money and share experiences with. For me it's all about the journey, not the destination. If you can find a friend who is willing to discuss finances with you, that will help you to make progress. I've found that the best way to make progress is to pay off credit cards and build a rainy-day fund. Good luck.

Large cap ETFs in the U.S. typically focus on the major U.S. stock markets, the S&P 500, and its components. The performance of these ETFs is heavily weighted towards these assets. It is not uncommon for these ETFs to underperform broad market indexes and even smaller cap ETFs by large amounts. In addition to the general focus, many of these ETFs have a mandatory 40-day no sell time frame, which can make for rather dramatic performance swings. 40 days is a pretty good amount of time to evaluate the performance of a stock, so from a pure investment perspective, these ETFs are perfectly adequate. If you have a preference for high-quality, stable stocks then the larger cap ETFs may not be a good match for your risk appetite. In your example, the Russell 2000 ETF (RTY) performed about 15% worse than the S&P 500 over the last 5 years, which is a pretty substantial difference. The performance of the Russell 2000 ETF also varies much more from day to day than the S&P 500, which can make for an unpleasant trading experience. If you have a preference for high-volatility stocks then the Russell 2000 ETF might be a good match, in which case the 15% difference in performance might not be a deal-breaker. Regarding your concern for false data. Google defines the Return from 2011-2015 for the Russell 2000 ETF as "Returns for the Russell 2000 ETF from 2011 through 2015 (5 years)". If you want data on the Russell 2000 ETF's performance from 2016 through 2019, you must look elsewhere.

You could create your own set of metrics. One such set of metrics is described in the book "Fooled by Randomness" by Nassim Taleb. Here are a few examples: Correlations: The book says that you should look for correlations that have a negative correlation with your own portfolio. For example, if your portfolio is heavily weighted toward healthcare stocks, then you should look for stocks that are anti-correlated with healthcare. You can create your own set of anti-correlated stocks by looking at the beta of each stock and creating a new portfolio that has zero beta. You can then look at the beta of that new portfolio to see which stocks are the most anti-correlated. You would then buy those stocks and ignore the fact that they are in different industries. This is an oversimplification, of course, and there are many ways to create a portfolio with good beta. The book also discusses the "alpha" of a portfolio. Alpha is the difference between the performance of a portfolio and that of a benchmark (usually the market index). For example, if your portfolio's alpha is 0.7, that means that your portfolio's performance is 0.7% better than the market index. The book says that you should look for alpha of 0.5 or higher for your portfolio. This means that your portfolio should be able to beat the market by 50% or more. Again, this is an oversimplification. Many portfolio managers would be happy with a portfolio that had an alpha of 0.3. And there are many ways to measure alpha. One popular way is to calculate the alpha of the differences in returns on stock prices and the differences in prices of corresponding bonds. The book says that this method of calculating alpha is 'wrong' because it takes into account only price fluctuations. It therefore provides a false alpha. The book calls this 'wrong' alpha 'beta-alpha'. The book also says that beta-alpha is 'wrong' because it ignores the fact that some prices are mean-adjusted. The mean adjusted prices of stocks and bonds are calculated as the average of all observed prices after adjusting for market seasonality, which is calculated as the average of the seasonally adjusted prices of all the stocks and bonds in your portfolio. The book calls this mean-adjusted alpha 'alpha-alpha'. The book says that alpha-alpha is the best way to calculate alpha because it takes into account all of the factors that affect a portfolio's price. Therefore it is the most accurate measure of a portfolio's performance. The book says that beta-alpha is useful for measuring the performance of a portfolio over a short period of time, such as a month. The book says that alpha-alpha is useful for measuring the performance of a portfolio over a long period of time, such as a year. If you use alpha-alpha to calculate your alpha, make sure to use a look-up table or a spreadsheet to look up the alpha for each stock or bond in your portfolio. Calculate the alpha for each stock or bond over the period that the stock or bond was in your portfolio. Then average the alphas of all the stocks and bonds in your portfolio. This average is the alpha of your portfolio. If you use beta-alpha to calculate your alpha, this average will be the beta of your portfolio. You can therefore use either alpha-alpha or beta-alpha to calculate your alpha. Which one is better? The book says that alpha-alpha is the gold standard. However, the book also says that beta-alpha is "pretty close". So if you're lazy, beta-alpha will do.

No, not at all. In a world of perfectly competitive markets, the price of a product is the sum of the costs of producing that product, plus a suitable profit margin. The only way an economy can become less efficient is when the costs of producing a product are reduced while the price of the product stays the same. This cannot happen in a perfectly competitive market. Prices are determined by the market, so they can't be manipulated. If the economy were not competitive, the price of a product would be driven by the demand and supply of its market participants, but those same participants would be driven by the price of the product itself. The idea that an economy becomes less efficient when it grows is nonsense. Less efficient means less usage of resources, and thus lower supply of a product. In a world of perfectly competitive markets, the growth of an economy would result in the opposite: an increase in the supply of a product, and a decrease in the price of the product.

Forward P/E is useful in estimating the price of a company, especially for industries where valuations are not readily available. The downside is that it may not be a reflection of true value, just an indication of relative value. If the P/E of a company is lower than 20, then you can be pretty sure that the company is undervalued (though not necessarily overpriced). If it's above 20, then you need to investigate further. The good thing about P/E is that it's easy to calculate - just divide the current price of the stock by the last earnings per share (earnings per share is the inverse of the P/E).

The first step I would do is determine if the problem is that the employer is expecting you to pay a smaller amount of FICA taxes than you are paying or if the employer is expecting you to pay the entire amount of FICA taxes. If the latter is the case then there is no legal reason to try to recover the excess FICA payments. You would just need to continue paying the same amount as you have been. If the former is the case, then you will need to determine how much of a different amount is you are required to pay and then calculate how much you would need to recover from the employer. Here are some pointers to consider: How did the error occur? Was it the result of an accounting error by the employer or was it your mistake? What has the employer done to correct the error? Has the employer refunded the excess FICA payments or is it still owed to the employer? If the employer has refunded the excess FICA payments, then you need to determine if you still owe the employer more than the refunded amount and if so, then you will need to decide how to recover that extra amount from the employer. If the employer has not refunded the excess FICA payments, then you will need to determine how much more you owe the employer and then decide how to recover that amount. In either case, you will need to calculate how much you owe the employer for your last year of work together with how much you owe the federal government for that year. This amount will be more than the amount you would owe if you had paid the correct amount of FICA taxes in that year. Once you have calculated the amount you owe the employer and the amount you owe the federal government, you can either choose to recover the excess from the employer or have the IRS refund it to you. If you decide to have the IRS refund it to you, you will need to calculate how much you owe the IRS for that year and then determine how to recover it from the IRS. You can get the necessary information from the Social Security Administration. In either case, you will need to be sure to include the necessary paperwork when you send in your tax return.

The penalties collected by the IRS for criminal convictions are generally used to pay for: 1) The cost of processing criminal cases; 2) Enforcement of criminal laws; and 3) The IRS's general purpose. Specifically, the penalty funds are used to: 1) Pay the salaries of IRS special agents and officers; 2) Cover the costs of investigations and prosecutions of tax violations; 3) Fund programs that provide financial assistance to taxpayers; and 4) Pay the costs of compliance with certain laws and regulations, including the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) Code. While some of this money goes to pay for the cost of processing criminal cases, some of it also goes to pay for the cost of processing civil cases. Civil cases are those that involve a citizen's right to information, such as a business person's right to challenge an IRS decision, or a taxpayer's right to be heard in a court of law. The civil cases are processed through the IRS Service Center. Some of the money that goes to the Service Center comes from penalties paid by taxpayers or businesses for civil infractions. These infractions include failing to file a return, filing a late return, or filing an incorrect return. The Service Center also receives money from penalties paid for criminal convictions. Some of the money that comes from criminal convictions is used to pay for investigations and prosecutions of tax violations, such as the investigation and prosecution of tax evaders. In addition to the money that comes from penalties paid by taxpayers and businesses, the IRS also receives money from interest payments, which are the proceeds from settlements that result from taxpayers' compliance with tax laws. The IRS also receives money from payments made by taxpayers for their tax returns. Taxpayers are required to file tax returns by a certain date each year. If they fail to file a return or file a late return, the IRS will send them a notice of penalty. The amount of the penalty is based on a percentage of the taxes owed for that year. If the taxpayer files a late return or a missing return, the IRS will also add the penalty to the amount owed for that year. In 2010, the IRS received $2.66 billion in penalties. Of that amount, $1.35 billion was from individuals and $1.35 million was from businesses. In 2011, the IRS received $2.72 billion in penalties. Of that amount, $1.16 billion was from individuals and $555 million was from businesses. In 2012, the IRS received $2.79 billion in penalties. Of that amount, $1.14 billion was from individuals and $654 million was from businesses. In 2013, the IRS received $2.86 billion in penalties. Of that amount, $1.14 billion was from individuals and $732 million was from businesses. In 2014, the IRS received $2.96 billion in penalties. Of that amount, $1.14 billion was from individuals and $822 million was from businesses. In 2015, the IRS received $2.96 billion in penalties. Of that amount, $1.14 billion was from individuals and $814 million was from businesses.

Why are interest rates on saving accounts so low in USA and Europe? In general, you will always get the highest possible rate for your money if you put it in CDs or other short term account. The problem with CDs is that they are not very liquid, it takes time to liquidate them. That is why banks pay such low rates on CDs. Banks get most of their funds from depositors, but they have to have liquidity to meet the demands of their depositors so they balance the needs of depositors with the cost of having those funds in CDs. In Europe, the interest rates are so low because the European Central Bank (ECB) controls the interest rates of all the member nations. They set a minimum rate that all banks must follow. Currently the ECB is trying to increase rates. You can get a higher rate outside of Europe in countries that do not participate in the ECB. In the USA, the Federal Reserve Bank controls the interest rates. It is a separate institution from the bank of America. The Federal Reserve Bank also tries to keep rates low to stimulate economic activity. You can argue that the Federal Reserve is not doing a good job at keeping rates low because of the high levels of inflation, but that is a whole new can of worms. Read up on monetary history to understand the role of the Federal Reserve and other banks and how they have affected monetary policy. See also, "The Fed" http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Federal_Reserve_Bank#Treasury_Bonds_and_Federal_Reserve_Assets and http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank#Monetary_policy In the USA, you can also get higher rates by depositing your money in CDs or other short term account. Remember that you may have to lock your funds in for an extended period of time to get the higher rates. Do you have the patience to wait? And remember that you may be penalized if you withdraw your funds before the period is over. See also, "Savings_accounts_and_certificates_of_deposit" on wikipedia.
